source.title: Marketplace entitlement verification API (AWS/GCP) for subscription → org provisioning flow
source.creator: Priya Natarajan
source.body:
## Context
Marketplace buyers currently hit a confusing failure mode in the first 30–90 minutes after subscription: Redwood Console can’t reliably determine whether the customer is entitled (and to what SKU limits), especially when entitlement propagation is delayed or when the customer already has a Redwood org.

This ticket implements/extends control-plane API endpoints used by the marketplace onboarding entrypoint to:
1) verify a buyer’s AWS/GCP marketplace subscription is active and mapped to a Redwood SKU
2) return a stable entitlement object (or a clear, retryable error) that Console + provisioning can act on
3) support edge cases (existing org linking, multiple subscriptions, delayed propagation)

This is a critical dependency for the marketplace refresh launch (time-to-first-token + reduced support escalations).

## Goals / Non-goals
### Goals
- Provide a single verification API surface for both AWS and GCP.
- Standardize error codes/messages so Console can show actionable UI states.
- Keep the source-of-truth in our entitlement DB (fed by sync job), while allowing on-demand refresh when safe.
- Add audit-grade logging for verification attempts and outcomes (no secrets).

### Non-goals (for this ticket)
- Rewriting the entitlement sync pipeline (tracked in ENG-22116).
- Implementing billing dimension mapping (tracked in ENG-22111).
- Console UI work (tracked in ENG-22112).

## Proposed API surface
All endpoints are under control-plane ("api.redwoodinference.com") and require Redwood user auth (session/JWT) unless otherwise noted.

### 1) Verify entitlement (primary)
**POST** `/v1/marketplace/entitlements:verify`

**Request fields (conceptual)**
- `provider`: `aws` | `gcp`
- `subscription_id`: provider-specific subscription identifier (AWS customer identifier / agreement id; GCP entitlement id)
- `account_id`: provider account/project identifier (AWS payer/account; GCP billing account/project) (optional but recommended)
- `org_id`: Redwood org being linked (optional; required for "link" flows)

**Response**
- `status`: `entitled` | `not_entitled` | `pending` | `blocked`
- `entitlement_id`: Redwood internal entitlement id (when available)
- `sku`: marketplace SKU identifier (e.g., `ri.hosted.usage`, `ri.dedicated.reserved`, `ri.private.license`, + add-ons)
- `limits`: derived limits/quotas for onboarding display (high-level; no billing internals)
- `effective_at`, `expires_at` (if contract/term-based)
- `next_action`: `link_org` | `create_org` | `retry` | `contact_support`

### 2) Fetch entitlement by provider identifier (support/debug + Console polling)
**GET** `/v1/marketplace/entitlements/{provider}/{subscription_id}`
- Returns the last-known entitlement record from our DB (including `last_synced_at`).

### 3) Link entitlement to org (explicit step)
**POST** `/v1/marketplace/entitlements:link`
- Requires user has admin role on `org_id`.
- Idempotent: linking the same entitlement to the same org is a no-op.
- If entitlement is already linked to a different org, return a deterministic error for Console (see error codes).

## Error codes / Console-facing semantics
Return structured errors with a stable `code` and human-safe `message`.

Required codes:
- `ENTITLEMENT_PENDING_PROPAGATION`: No entitlement found yet; recommend retry after N minutes.
- `ENTITLEMENT_NOT_FOUND`: Verified lookup completed but no active entitlement exists.
- `ENTITLEMENT_INACTIVE_OR_EXPIRED`: Entitlement exists but is not active.
- `ENTITLEMENT_ALREADY_LINKED`: Entitlement is linked to a different org.
- `PROVIDER_ID_MISMATCH`: Subscription id does not match account/project context.
- `PROVIDER_UNAVAILABLE`: Provider API timeout/unavailable (only if we attempt on-demand provider validation).
- `SYNC_STALE_TRY_AGAIN`: DB record exists but is older than staleness threshold and refresh attempt failed.
- `UNAUTHORIZED_ORG_ADMIN_REQUIRED`: user lacks permission to link.

Console mapping guidance (to share w/ Chloe/Design):
- `PENDING_PROPAGATION` and `SYNC_STALE_TRY_AGAIN` are retryable with clear CTA and expected timing.
- `ALREADY_LINKED` should direct user to contact support with entitlement id.

## Implementation notes
### Data sources
- Primary: `marketplace_entitlements` table (populated by entitlement sync job).
- Optional on-demand refresh:
  - If `last_synced_at` older than threshold (proposed: 10 minutes during onboarding), we may enqueue a sync for that provider+subscription and immediately return `pending` with `next_action=retry`.
  - Avoid synchronous calls to provider APIs on the request path unless we have strict timeouts + caching; preference is enqueue refresh + return.

### Idempotency + concurrency
- `:link` endpoint must be idempotent; use transaction with unique constraint on `entitlement_id`.
- Protect against double-linking in multi-tab onboarding.

### Authz
- Verify caller is a Redwood user.
- For link operations: require org admin.
- Do not expose provider raw contract details; only return minimal SKU + status + timestamps.

### Logging / audit
- Emit audit event: `marketplace.entitlement.verify` with fields: provider, subscription_id_hash (hash), org_id (if present), result status, error code, latency_ms.
- Emit audit event: `marketplace.entitlement.link` with entitlement_id, org_id, actor_user_id, outcome.

### Telemetry hooks
Coordinate event names w/ Nadia Rahman (ENG-22114):
- `mp_verify_start`, `mp_verify_result` (status + error_code)
- `mp_link_result`

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] POST `:verify` returns deterministic status (`entitled`/`pending`/`not_entitled`/`blocked`) for AWS and GCP test subscriptions.
- [ ] GET entitlement endpoint returns last-known record including `last_synced_at`.
- [ ] POST `:link` is idempotent and enforces org-admin permission.
- [ ] Error codes above are implemented and documented in internal API docs.
- [ ] Unit + integration tests cover:
  - pending propagation
  - inactive/expired
  - already-linked
  - multi-subscription per org
  - stale sync record triggering refresh enqueue
- [ ] Audit logs/events are emitted and visible in staging.

## Rollout plan
1) Ship behind feature flag `marketplace_entitlement_verification_v2` (default off).
2) Enable in staging; run end-to-end test plan from Partnerships (AWS/GCP).
3) Enable for internal dogfood orgs + a small % of marketplace onboarding traffic.
4) Full rollout after 48h with no increase in provisioning-delay tickets.

## Notes from partner + support feedback
- Support escalation SUP-4821 indicates customers can wait ~45 minutes for entitlement propagation; Console must show "pending" state with guidance.
- We must avoid wording that implies Redwood can see full marketplace billing details; keep responses minimal.

## Open questions
- What is the canonical AWS identifier we can reliably request from users in Console (CustomerIdentifier vs AgreementId)? (need alignment w/ Partnerships)
- For GCP, do we require both entitlement id and project id, or is entitlement id sufficient for verification?
- Staleness threshold during onboarding: 10 minutes vs 5 minutes?

- Console entrypoint task: ENG-22112
- Sync hardening task: ENG-22116

title: Marketplace entitlement verification API (AWS/GCP) for subscription → org provisioning flow
preview:
## Context
Marketplace buyers currently hit a confusing failure mode in the first 30–90 minutes after subscription: Redwood Console can’t reliably determine whether the customer is entitled (and to what SKU limits), especially when entitlement propagation is delayed or when the customer already has a Redwood org.

This ticket implements/extends control-plane API endpoints used by the marketplace onboarding entrypoint to:
1) verify a buyer’s AWS/GCP marketplace subscription is active and mapped to a Redwood SKU
2) return a stable entitlement object (or a clear, retryable error) that Console + provisioning can act on
3) support edge cases (existing org linking, multiple subscriptions, delayed propagation)

This is a critical dependency for the marketplace refresh launch (time-to-first-token + reduced support escalations).

## Goals / Non-goals
### Goals
- Provide a single verification API surface for both AWS and GCP.
- Standardize error codes/messages so Console can show actionable UI states.
- Keep the source-of-truth in our entitlement DB (fed by sync job), while allowing on-demand refresh when safe.
- Add audit-grade logging for verification attempts and outcomes (no secrets).

### Non-goals (for this ticket)
- Rewriting the entitlement sync pipeline (tracked in ENG-22116).
- Implementing billing dimension mapping (tracked in ENG-22111).
- Console UI work (tracked in ENG-22112).

## Proposed API surface
All endpoints are under control-plane ("api.redwoodinference.com") and require Redwood user auth (session/JWT) unless otherwise noted.

### 1) Verify entitlement (primary)
**POST** `/v1/marketplace/entitlements:verify`

**Request fields (conceptual)**
- `provider`: `aws` | `gcp`
- `subscription_id`: provider-specific subscription identifier (AWS customer identifier / agreement id; GCP entitlement id)
- `account_id`: provider account/project identifier (AWS payer/account; GCP billing account/project) (optional but recommended)
- `org_id`: Redwood org being linked (optional; required for "link" flows)

**Response**
- `status`: `entitled` | `not_entitled` | `pending` | `blocked`
- `entitlement_id`: Redwood internal entitlement id (when available)
- `sku`: marketplace SKU identifier (e.g., `ri.hosted.usage`, `ri.dedicated.reserved`, `ri.private.license`, + add-ons)
- `limits`: derived limits/quotas for onboarding display (high-level; no billing internals)
- `effective_at`, `expires_at` (if contract/term-based)
- `next_action`: `link_org` | `create_org` | `retry` | `contact_support`

### 2) Fetch entitlement by provider identifier (support/debug + Console polling)
**GET** `/v1/marketplace/entitlements/{provider}/{subscription_id}`
- Returns the last-known entitlement record from our DB (including `last_synced_at`).

### 3) Link entitlement to org (explicit step)
**POST** `/v1/marketplace/entitlements:link`
- Requires user has admin role on `org_id`.
- Idempotent: linking the same entitlement to the same org is a no-op.
- If entitlement is already linked to a different org, return a deterministic error for Console (see error codes).

## Error codes / Console-facing semantics
Return structured errors with a stable `code` and human-safe `message`.

Required codes:
- `ENTITLEMENT_PENDING_PROPAGATION`: No entitlement found yet; recommend retry after N minutes.
- `ENTITLEMENT_NOT_FOUND`: Verified lookup completed but no active entitlement exists.
- `ENTITLEMENT_INACTIVE_OR_EXPIRED`: Entitlement exists but is not active.
- `ENTITLEMENT_ALREADY_LINKED`: Entitlement is linked to a different org.
- `PROVIDER_ID_MISMATCH`: Subscription id does not match account/project context.
- `PROVIDER_UNAVAILABLE`: Provider API timeout/unavailable (only if we attempt on-demand provider validation).
- `SYNC_STALE_TRY_AGAIN`: DB record exists but is older than staleness threshold and refresh attempt failed.
- `UNAUTHORIZED_ORG_ADMIN_REQUIRED`: user lacks permission to link.

Console mapping guidance (to share w/ Chloe/Design):
- `PENDING_PROPAGATION` and `SYNC_STALE_TRY_AGAIN` are retryable with clear CTA and expected timing.
- `ALREADY_LINKED` should direct user to contact support with entitlement id.

## Implementation notes
### Data sources
- Primary: `marketplace_entitlements` table (populated by entitlement sync job).
- Optional on-demand refresh:
  - If `last_synced_at` older than threshold (proposed: 10 minutes during onboarding), we may enqueue a sync for that provider+subscription and immediately return `pending` with `next_action=retry`.
  - Avoid synchronous calls to provider APIs on the request path unless we have strict timeouts + caching; preference is enqueue refresh + return.

### Idempotency + concurrency
- `:link` endpoint must be idempotent; use transaction with unique constraint on `entitlement_id`.
- Protect against double-linking in multi-tab onboarding.

### Authz
- Verify caller is a Redwood user.
- For link operations: require org admin.
- Do not expose provider raw contract details; only return minimal SKU + status + timestamps.

### Logging / audit
- Emit audit event: `marketplace.entitlement.verify` with fields: provider, subscription_id_hash (hash), org_id (if present), result status, error code, latency_ms.
- Emit audit event: `marketplace.entitlement.link` with entitlement_id, org_id, actor_user_id, outcome.

### Telemetry hooks
Coordinate event names w/ Nadia Rahman (ENG-22114):
- `mp_verify_start`, `mp_verify_result` (status + error_code)
- `mp_link_result`

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] POST `:verify` returns deterministic status (`entitled`/`pending`/`not_entitled`/`blocked`) for AWS and GCP test subscriptions.
- [ ] GET entitlement endpoint returns last-known record including `last_synced_at`.
- [ ] POST `:link` is idempotent and enforces org-admin permission.
- [ ] Error codes above are implemented and documented in internal API docs.
- [ ] Unit + integration tests cover:
  - pending propagation
  - inactive/expired
  - already-linked
  - multi-subscription per org
  - stale sync record triggering refresh enqueue
- [ ] Audit logs/events are emitted and visible in staging.

## Rollout plan
1) Ship behind feature flag `marketplace_entitlement_verification_v2` (default off).
2) Enable in staging; run end-to-end test plan from Partnerships (AWS/GCP).
3) Enable for internal dogfood orgs + a small % of marketplace onboarding traffic.
4) Full rollout after 48h with no increase in provisioning-delay tickets.

## Notes from partner + support feedback
- Support escalation SUP-4821 indicates customers can wait ~45 minutes for entitlement propagation; Console must show "pending" state with guidance.
- We must avoid wording that implies Redwood can see full marketplace billing details; keep responses minimal.

## Open questions
- What is the canonical AWS identifier we can reliably request from users in Console (CustomerIdentifier vs AgreementId)? (need alignment w/ Partnerships)
- For GCP, do we require both entitlement id and project id, or is entitlement id sufficient for verification?
- Staleness threshold during onboarding: 10 minutes vs 5 minutes?

## Links
- Design spec (subscription → provisioning flow): sources/confluence/product-docs/design-specs/marketplace-subscription-to-org-provisioning-flow.json
- Instrumentation task: ENG-22114
- Console entrypoint task: ENG-22112
- Sync hardening task: ENG-22116

source.title: Route normalization library (templating IDs) shared by Gateway + Telemetry
source.creator: Logan Wright
source.description:
Problem
- Usage Insights requires cost/usage breakdown by API route. Today we emit inconsistent route strings depending on where the event is produced (gateway vs downstream services) and whether a request hit OpenAI-compat vs Redwood-native endpoints.
- Unnormalized paths with IDs (e.g. /v1/workspaces/ws_abc123/projects/prj_9f.../keys/key_...) explode cardinality and make aggregations expensive/unreliable.

Goal
- Ship a single, shared route normalization implementation used by both:
  1) API Gateway request metering/telemetry emitters
  2) Telemetry ingestion/aggregation jobs (as a fallback / validation layer)
- Normalize routes by templating path params (IDs) and standardizing prefixes so aggregation keys are stable.
- Provide tests + a migration/rollout plan to avoid breaking billing reconciliation and existing dashboards.

Non-goals (Phase 1)
- Not a general-purpose URL parser for arbitrary customer-defined routes.
- Not auto-discovering templates from traffic (we will use a controlled template set + a conservative heuristic fallback).
- Not changing billing semantics; only adding/standardizing dimensions used for analytics.

Proposed output fields (event tags)
- route_raw: exact request path from gateway (no query string); retained temporarily for debugging.
- route_template: normalized template used for analytics (e.g. /v1/workspaces/{workspace_id}/projects/{project_id}/api-keys/{key_id}).
- route_group: canonical grouping (mostly equals route_template; may collapse minor variants like trailing slash).
- route_source: gateway | ingestion (for auditing/coverage metrics).

Normalization rules (Phase 1)
1) Strip query string and fragment.
   - /v1/chat/completions?stream=true -> /v1/chat/completions
2) Remove trailing slash (except root).
   - /v1/embeddings/ -> /v1/embeddings
3) Prefer exact match against a maintained template allowlist for Redwood + OpenAI-compat.
   Examples (expected route_template):
   - /v1/chat/completions -> /v1/chat/completions
   - /v1/embeddings -> /v1/embeddings
   - /v1/rerank -> /v1/rerank
   - /openai/v1/chat/completions -> /openai/v1/chat/completions
   - /openai/v1/embeddings -> /openai/v1/embeddings
4) For templated routes with IDs, replace segments in known positions.
   - /v1/workspaces/ws_2kQ1/projects/prj_A91/deployments/dpl_77/logs -> /v1/workspaces/{workspace_id}/projects/{project_id}/deployments/{deployment_id}/logs
   - /v1/keys/key_abc123 -> /v1/keys/{key_id}
5) Conservative heuristic fallback for unknown routes:
   - If a segment matches common ID patterns (uuid v4, base58-ish tokens >= 16 chars, ws_/prj_/dpl_ prefixes), replace with {id}.
   - If heuristic would replace >2 segments OR the result would be too generic, return /unknown (and keep route_raw for debugging).

Rationale
- Using an allowlist first prevents over-templating and avoids grouping distinct endpoints under the same template.
- Heuristic fallback covers missed templates while limiting cardinality blowups.
- Keeping route_raw temporarily unblocks debugging and customer support cases (e.g. “missing OpenAI route breakdown”) without trusting it for aggregation keys.

Implementation plan
- Create shared package: go module "github.com/redwoodinference/platform/route-normalization" (exact path TBD by repo conventions).
- Expose stable API:
  - Normalize(path string) -> {route_template, route_group, normalization_reason}
  - NormalizeFromURL(url string) -> same, with query stripping
  - Metrics helpers: normalization coverage counters (matched_allowlist, matched_template, heuristic, unknown)
- Add template registry (versioned): templates.go + templates_test.go
  - Explicit test fixtures for every supported route.
- Integrate in Gateway emitter:
  - Emit route_template + route_group on every metering event.
  - Emit route_raw behind a temporary feature flag (default on in beta environments; off in GA unless requested).
- Integrate in Telemetry ingestion:
  - If route_template missing (older agents/regions), compute it at ingestion and set route_source=ingestion.
  - If both present, compute and compare; log mismatch rate with sampling.

Testing
- Unit tests:
  - Allowlist exact matches (Redwood + OpenAI-compat)
  - ID templating for all “ID-bearing” endpoints
  - Query stripping / trailing slash
  - Heuristic behavior (replace 1–2 segments only)
  - Negative tests: do NOT template common nouns (e.g. /v1/models/llama-3.1-70b stays literal if in allowlist)
- Integration tests:
  - Gateway event emission contains stable route_template for representative traffic
  - Ingestion fallback produces identical templates for the same request
- Regression tests:
  - Fixtures sourced from recent gateway logs to ensure no surprises with real traffic patterns

Rollout / migration plan
1) Phase A (1–2 days): library lands + gateway emits route_template + route_group in parallel; ingestion fallback enabled.
2) Phase B (1 week): monitor normalization coverage + mismatch rate dashboards.
   - Target: >= 99% matched_allowlist/template/heuristic (non-unknown) for /v1/* and /openai/v1/*.
   - Mismatch target: < 0.5% sampled mismatches (excluding known legacy paths).
3) Phase C: switch Usage Insights aggregation (ENG-3812) to key on route_group.
4) Phase D: deprecate raw route usage in analytics; keep route_raw only for debug sampling, gated by env + retention.

Risks / open questions
- OpenAI-compat routing: ensure we treat /openai/v1/* as first-class templates; do not collapse into /v1/*.
- Streaming: stream=true is a query param; we strip it so route_template is stable. Streaming overhead will be attributed via separate dimension(s) (not via route).
- Cardinality: heuristic fallback must remain conservative. If unknown traffic increases, we add templates explicitly rather than broadening heuristics.
- Backfill: we will not backfill historical data in Phase 1; insights will show normalized routes from rollout forward. (Backfill plan tracked separately.)

Acceptance criteria
- Shared library merged with full test suite.
- Gateway emits route_template + route_group for 100% of metering events in at least one canary region.
- Ingestion fallback enabled and mismatch rate is within target.
- Template registry includes all current public API endpoints used for Usage Insights (chat, completions, embeddings, rerank + auth/admin endpoints that generate meaningful spend).

Links
- ADR: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-route-normalization-and-attribution.json
- Slack thread (edge cases): sources/slack/eng-platform/1737468804.json
- Related: ENG-3822 (route tag instrumentation), ENG-3812 (aggregation dataset keyed by route/model)
comments: 2026-01-09 (Thomas Baird): Drafted initial template allowlist for /v1/* and /openai/v1/* based on gateway route table + known public endpoints. Need confirmation from Dylan/Chloe on any internal admin routes we want included for spend attribution (e.g. /v1/fine-tunes is out of scope but may show up).

2026-01-11 (Nadia Rahman): Please keep heuristics conservative. We saw previous “UUID replacement everywhere” collapse unrelated endpoints and made reconciliation vs billing painful. Prefer /unknown + raw route sampling over over-grouping.

2026-01-14 (Chloe Martin): For ingestion fallback, please only compute when route_template missing. If present, compute and compare but don’t overwrite; we’ll use mismatch metric to catch rollout issues per-region.

2026-01-16 (Dylan Brooks): UX wants the dimension called “Route (API endpoint)” in Console. Internally we can keep route_template/route_group, but docs/tooltips should avoid the word “templated” unless expanded.

2026-01-20 (Thomas Baird): Implemented NormalizeFromURL with strict query stripping; trailing slash normalization added. Added fixtures for workspace/project/deployment ID routes. Still need OpenAI-compat templates for embeddings + chat (in progress).

2026-01-23 (Felix Schneider): Please ensure /v1/chat/completions and /v1/completions remain distinct; some customers use both and want separate breakdowns. Don’t group them under a shared “completions” bucket.

2026-01-27 (Thomas Baird): PR up with library + gateway integration behind feature flag "telemetry.route_normalization_v1". Adds mismatch counters + sampling logs. Requesting review from Logan + Chloe + Nadia.

2026-02-03 (Logan Wright): Reviewed. Asked for two changes before merge: (1) add explicit tests for base58-ish segments vs common words, (2) reduce heuristic replacement limit from 3 segments to 2 to prevent over-templating. Otherwise looks good.
2026-02-06 (Thomas Baird): Addressed review notes: heuristic now templates max 2 segments; added negative tests (don’t template model slugs) and explicit OpenAI-compat fixtures. Ready for re-review.

2026-02-10 (Thomas Baird): Merged and rolled to canary region. Early metrics: 99.6% non-unknown for /v1/* + /openai/v1/*; mismatch 0.18% (mostly legacy paths with double-prefix). Follow-up issue needed to add template for legacy /api/v1/chat/completions seen in one region.
activity: Follow-ups: ENG-3818 (cardinality guardrails), ENG-3812 (aggregation dataset), ENG-3814 (query endpoints)

- route_template: normalized template used for analytics (e.g. /v1/workspaces/{workspace_id}/projects/{project_id}/api-keys/{key_id}).
- route_group: canonical grouping (mostly equals route_template; may collapse minor variants like trailing slash).
- route_source: gateway | ingestion (for auditing/coverage metrics).

Normalization rules (Phase 1)
1) Strip query string and fragment.
   - /v1/chat/completions?stream=true -> /v1/chat/completions
2) Remove trailing slash (except root).
   - /v1/embeddings/ -> /v1/embeddings
3) Prefer exact match against a maintained template allowlist for Redwood + OpenAI-compat.
   Examples (expected route_template):
   - /v1/chat/completions -> /v1/chat/completions
   - /v1/embeddings -> /v1/embeddings
   - /v1/rerank -> /v1/rerank
   - /openai/v1/chat/completions -> /openai/v1/chat/completions
   - /openai/v1/embeddings -> /openai/v1/embeddings
4) For templated routes with IDs, replace segments in known positions.
   - /v1/workspaces/ws_2kQ1/projects/prj_A91/deployments/dpl_77/logs -> /v1/workspaces/{workspace_id}/projects/{project_id}/deployments/{deployment_id}/logs
   - /v1/keys/key_abc123 -> /v1/keys/{key_id}
5) Conservative heuristic fallback for unknown routes:
   - If a segment matches common ID patterns (uuid v4, base58-ish tokens >= 16 chars, ws_/prj_/dpl_ prefixes), replace with {id}.
   - If heuristic would replace >2 segments OR the result would be too generic, return /unknown (and keep route_raw for debugging).

Rationale
- Using an allowlist first prevents over-templating and avoids grouping distinct endpoints under the same template.
- Heuristic fallback covers missed templates while limiting cardinality blowups.
- Keeping route_raw temporarily unblocks debugging and customer support cases (e.g. “missing OpenAI route breakdown”) without trusting it for aggregation keys.

Implementation plan
- Create shared package: go module "github.com/redwoodinference/platform/route-normalization" (exact path TBD by repo conventions).
- Expose stable API:
  - Normalize(path string) -> {route_template, route_group, normalization_reason}
  - NormalizeFromURL(url string) -> same, with query stripping
  - Metrics helpers: normalization coverage counters (matched_allowlist, matched_template, heuristic, unknown)
- Add template registry (versioned): templates.go + templates_test.go
  - Explicit test fixtures for every supported route.
- Integrate in Gateway emitter:
  - Emit route_template + route_group on every metering event.
  - Emit route_raw behind a temporary feature flag (default on in beta environments; off in GA unless requested).
- Integrate in Telemetry ingestion:
  - If route_template missing (older agents/regions), compute it at ingestion and set route_source=ingestion.
  - If both present, compute and compare; log mismatch rate with sampling.

Testing
- Unit tests:
  - Allowlist exact matches (Redwood + OpenAI-compat)
  - ID templating for all “ID-bearing” endpoints
  - Query stripping / trailing slash
  - Heuristic behavior (replace 1–2 segments only)
  - Negative tests: do NOT template common nouns (e.g. /v1/models/llama-3.1-70b stays literal if in allowlist)
- Integration tests:
  - Gateway event emission contains stable route_template for representative traffic
  - Ingestion fallback produces identical templates for the same request
- Regression tests:
  - Fixtures sourced from recent gateway logs to ensure no surprises with real traffic patterns

Rollout / migration plan
1) Phase A (1–2 days): library lands + gateway emits route_template + route_group in parallel; ingestion fallback enabled.
2) Phase B (1 week): monitor normalization coverage + mismatch rate dashboards.
   - Target: >= 99% matched_allowlist/template/heuristic (non-unknown) for /v1/* and /openai/v1/*.
   - Mismatch target: < 0.5% sampled mismatches (excluding known legacy paths).
3) Phase C: switch Usage Insights aggregation (ENG-3812) to key on route_group.
4) Phase D: deprecate raw route usage in analytics; keep route_raw only for debug sampling, gated by env + retention.

Risks / open questions
- OpenAI-compat routing: ensure we treat /openai/v1/* as first-class templates; do not collapse into /v1/*.
- Streaming: stream=true is a query param; we strip it so route_template is stable. Streaming overhead will be attributed via separate dimension(s) (not via route).
- Cardinality: heuristic fallback must remain conservative. If unknown traffic increases, we add templates explicitly rather than broadening heuristics.
- Backfill: we will not backfill historical data in Phase 1; insights will show normalized routes from rollout forward. (Backfill plan tracked separately.)

Acceptance criteria
- Shared library merged with full test suite.
- Gateway emits route_template + route_group for 100% of metering events in at least one canary region.
- Ingestion fallback enabled and mismatch rate is within target.
- Template registry includes all current public API endpoints used for Usage Insights (chat, completions, embeddings, rerank + auth/admin endpoints that generate meaningful spend).

Links
- ADR: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-route-normalization-and-attribution.json
- Slack thread (edge cases): sources/slack/eng-platform/1737468804.json
- Related: ENG-3822 (route tag instrumentation), ENG-3812 (aggregation dataset keyed by route/model)
text:
Problem
- Usage Insights requires cost/usage breakdown by API route. Today we emit inconsistent route strings depending on where the event is produced (gateway vs downstream services) and whether a request hit OpenAI-compat vs Redwood-native endpoints.
- Unnormalized paths with IDs (e.g. /v1/workspaces/ws_abc123/projects/prj_9f.../keys/key_...) explode cardinality and make aggregations expensive/unreliable.

Goal
- Ship a single, shared route normalization implementation used by both:
  1) API Gateway request metering/telemetry emitters
  2) Telemetry ingestion/aggregation jobs (as a fallback / validation layer)
- Normalize routes by templating path params (IDs) and standardizing prefixes so aggregation keys are stable.
- Provide tests + a migration/rollout plan to avoid breaking billing reconciliation and existing dashboards.

Non-goals (Phase 1)
- Not a general-purpose URL parser for arbitrary customer-defined routes.
- Not auto-discovering templates from traffic (we will use a controlled template set + a conservative heuristic fallback).
- Not changing billing semantics; only adding/standardizing dimensions used for analytics.

source.title: Clarify token-source attribution and recovery after shard replay
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Summary: During a recent audit of reconstructed usage we observed persistent per-model attribution oscillations after shard replays and targeted backfills. The symptom is that daily peak order and per-model top lists shift unexpectedly after backfill windows complete. Root cause appears to be non-deterministic attribution when rate-limit retry fan-in and replay markers are present: downstream aggregators sometimes favor the replay event's model_alias (or lose the original_model_id) resulting in charge redistribution to 'fallback' model names in per-model charts.
source.impact_details: Impacts console per-model breakdowns and top-model widgets for customers with heavy retry rates or subscribers that replay streams. Leads to transient billing surface differences in the console (not billing system source-of-truth), confusing customers and SRE debugging. Affects model-level trend alerts and any rollout decisions based on recent peaks.
source.reproduction_steps: 1) Identify tenant with high retry fan-in (rate-limit heavy) and short-lived backfill. 2) Replay partial shard with dedup disabled and with synthetic retry-chain ids. 3) Observe per-model daily top lists before and after replay; look for model_alias shifts. 4) Run the reconciliation query (see telemetry_queries) to compare original_event.model_id vs aggregate.model_alias after ingestion and after backfill window completes.
source.investigation_notes:
- Ingest pipeline preserves original_model_id in event.payload but some downstream protobuf mappers drop it when retry_chain_id is present.
- Rate-limit retries are coalesced at the gateway layer; coalesced events are annotated with canonical_model_alias but lose 'source_provenance' fields if they have been compacted.
- Backfill ingestion path sets is_replay=true and assigns replay_watermark; aggregator currently picks latest non-null model_alias seen in the window, which surfaces replayed alias if replay arrives after live event.
- We validated using synthetic replays that preferring earliest upstream_timestamp rather than ingestion timestamp reduces oscillation by ~87% in our test harness.
- Also observed that UI queries use aggregated rollups that don't expose event provenance and so cannot mark rows as 'replayed', hiding the difference from end users.
source.root_cause: Aggregator attribution policy uses ingestion_time ordering and a last-write-wins model_alias tie-breaker. Replay and retry workflows can present later ingestion_time with a different alias or missing original_model_id, causing incorrect attribution. Additionally, some compacting paths drop provenance fields causing permanent loss at aggregation time.
source.proposed_change: 1) Update aggregator to use upstream_timestamp (or original_event.upstream_ts) as the primary ordering key for model attribution, falling back to ingestion_time only when upstream timestamp is missing. 2) Preserve original_model_id and retry_chain_id through compacting steps; modify protobuf schemas and mappers as needed. 3) Add a 'staleness' / 'replay' flag on per-model rollups exposed to Console UI so charts can visually indicate backfilled data. 4) Implement incremental smoothing for UI charts: weight backfilled events with a half-life window (configurable) so large late batches don't create sharp spikes in top-model lists. 5) Add a post-replay reconciliation job that can reassign ambiguous rows when provenance exists.
source.rollout_plan: Phase 1 (Canary): Deploy aggregator changes to a single region and enable provenance-preservation on a synthetic tenant; run reconciliation job in dry-run mode and compare outputs for 72h. Phase 2: Enable UI 'replay' badge and smoothing algorithm for 5% of tenants; collect feedback and telemetry. Phase 3: Full rollout across regions and enable auto-reconcile for tenant rollups. Reconciliation job will be opt-in for tenants until post-mortem window closes.
source.testing_plan:
- Unit tests for aggregator ordering logic and provenance passthrough.
- Integration test: synthetic stream generator that emits interleaved live and replayed events with controlled upstream_ts and retry_chain_id; assert stable model attribution.
- Load test: verify aggregator performance under typical shard bursting and backfill volumes; ensure latency SLOs are not violated.
- Console A/B test for smoothing and replay badge to confirm reduced oscillation and acceptable user comprehension.
telemetry_queries: -- Reconciliation compare: SELECT tenant_id, model_id, count(*) FROM reconstructed_rollup WHERE is_replay=false GROUP BY tenant_id, model_id;
-- After backfill: SELECT tenant_id, model_alias, SUM(tokens) FROM rollups GROUP BY tenant_id, model_alias;
-- Find lost provenance: SELECT event_id FROM compacted_events WHERE original_model_id IS NULL AND retry_chain_id IS NOT NULL LIMIT 100;
source.observability_notes: Add new dashboard panel 'Attribution drift: model_alias flips per 24h' with alert when >5% of top-50 model slots for a tenant change after a backfill window. Instrument reconciliation job with before/after diff counts and a histogram of upstream_ts vs ingestion_time skew.
source.open_questions:
- What should be the default half-life for backfill smoothing? (1h / 6h / 24h)
- Are there any downstream billing consumers that require ingestion_time ordering semantics today?
- Should the reconciliation job modify historic rollups or only maintain a correction layer exposed to the Console?
source.next_steps: 1) Lock protobuf/mapping changes and get schema review (CC: infra-schema). 2) Implement aggregator ordering patch and unit tests. 3) Deploy canary and run dry-run reconciliation for 72h. 4) Add Console UI 'replay' indicator and smoothing toggle behind a feature flag.
notes_history: 2026-01-14: Initial investigation by Rohit Malhotra; reproduced with synthetic shard replay.
2026-01-20: Confirmed ingestion-side provenance loss in compacting path; opened ingest PR for schema changes.
2026-01-28: Benchmarked upstream_ts ordering; saw 87% reduction in attribution flips in test harness.
2026-02-09: Design review with Console and Billing teams; agreed on reconciliation opt-in during rollout.

source.title: Clockless anchoring reset for tenant-granular quotas
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem: Tenant quota resets across daily/monthly and rolling windows are fragile in the presence of clock skew, cross-region failover, DST leaps, and reserved capacity handoffs. Existing epoch-based resets rely on UTC or tenant TZ anchors which complicates reserved pool migrations and produces brief double-counting or missed resets for high-throughput tenants.

Goal: Implement a clockless anchoring reset mechanism that uses distributed checkpoints and idempotent reset tokens to produce deterministic, idempotent quota resets for tenant-scoped meters. The mechanism should work for both rolling and fixed-window quotas, be compatible with reserved-capacity leases, and allow fast safe cutovers during migrations.

Approach summary:
- Checkpoint epochs: persist lightweight reset checkpoints to the usage ledger as monotonic markers (reset_token). Checkpoints are produced by the lease owner for a tenant or by a quorum probe when leases are transferred.
- Clockless anchor: instead of relying on wall-clock boundary, the reset is anchored to the checkpoint token sequence number + committed ledger offset. Reset logic uses token idempotency so repeated or concurrent resets converge.
- Lease-aware semantics: when reserved-capacity leases change owners, the takeover process writes a handoff checkpoint and a planned-reset marker. Consumers use these to avoid double resets during in-flight transfers.
- Reconciliation job: background reconcile worker scans ledger to detect missing checkpoints and backfill deterministic resets; reports anomalies to billing telemetry.

Why different from existing solutions: focuses on ledger-driven deterministic resets (token/offset anchored) rather than epoch math. This reduces edge cases during cross-pool migrations, DST edges, and leap seconds, and minimizes customer-visible quota drift.

Success criteria:
- No increase in customer-visible overage incidents during cutovers in canary tenants (target 0 regressions).
- Reconciliation latency median < 2 minutes for missing checkpoint detection.
- End-to-end idempotent reset verified via automated property tests and integration tests using simulated clock skew and lease flapping.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: Reconciliation false positives causing force resets. Mitigation: require a quorum of ledger writes and a short jitter window before backfill.
- Risk: Performance overhead on high-throughput tenants. Mitigation: checkpoint writes are sharded and batched; use compact marker records and non-blocking fast-path for common requests.

Implementation plan:
1) RFC and Confluence spec (linked below) describing ledger markers, reset_token format, and idempotency semantics.
2) Low-risk prototype: implement checkpoint write in quota-service with feature flag; add auditor job that reports but does not backfill.
3) Canary rollout to 5 mid-size tenants with monitoring dashboards and rollback feature flag.
4) Enable backfill in reconciliation after 1 week of clean signals.
5) Full rollout and deprecation of older epoch anchoring for new tenants, with a migration path for existing reserved pools.

Testing:
- Unit tests for token idempotency and edge-case merges.
- Integration tests that spin up 3 ledger replicas, simulate lease transfers, DST and leap-second events, and validate quota invariants.
- Load tests with synthetic tenants to validate checkpoint write latency under 95th percentile SLA.

Metrics and alerts:
- telemetry.quota.reset_attempts.count (by tenant, status)
- telemetry.quota.reset_idempotency_conflicts.count
- latency.quota.checkpoint_write.p95
- reconciliation.backfill.jobs.duration.p99
- alert if reconciliation anomalies > 0.1% of tenants over 1h window

Docs and PRs: see attachments list for design doc and early PRs.
comments: 2026-01-12 - Ava Chen: Kickoff meeting notes - agreed to use ledger-first tokens and to avoid synchronous blocking on request path. Action: Luis to draft Confluence RFC.

2026-01-18 - Luis Martinez: Uploaded RFC to Confluence (link in attachments). Prototype branch created: github.com/redwood-inference/quota-service/pull/312. Note: initial approach used synchronous flush; replaced with batched append to avoid p99 latency regression.

2026-01-25 - Priya Singh (SRE): Reviewed prototype. Feedback: ensure reconciliation job is rate-limited to avoid thundering herd during large migrations. Also request an endpoint to pause backfill per-tenant.

2026-01-29 - Luis Martinez: Implemented pause/resume backfill API + added per-tenant cooldown. Added additional metrics and PromQL queries referenced in dashboards. PR updated: github.com/redwood-inference/quota-service/pull/328.

2026-01-31 - Finance (Carlos Vega): Raised question about invoice boundary semantics during migration. Decided: billing uses the last successful checkpoint before invoicing cutoff; any backfills after cutoff are recorded as adjustments and routed to billing adjustments pipeline. Action: Finance owners to review adjustment workflow by 2026-02-08.

2026-02-02 - QA (Maya Patel): Integration tests green for prototype in staging. Found a concurrency window where two simultaneous lease handoffs produced duplicate planned-reset markers; fixed by making planned-reset compare-and-set on marker epoch.

source.title: Adaptive setpoint autoscale soak probe (reverse-sawtooth + steady-state)
source.creator: Maya Chen
source.description: Create and validate an adaptive 'setpoint' autoscale soak probe that exercises Dedicated capacity under a compound workload: long steady-state baseline plus periodic reverse-sawtooth bursts that ramp down quickly then sustain mid-level traffic. The harness should measure tokens/sec, p95/p99 generation latency, cold-start incidents, and KV cache hit ratio; detect regressions vs a rolling baseline; and produce signals suitable for integration with our autoscaler fuzzing and alerting pipelines. Unlike previous burst/soak harnesses, this probe uses a thermostatic controller to nudge target capacity when p95 drifts above configurable thresholds, emulating an operator-adjusted with hysteresis to avoid oscillation.
acceptance_criteria: Harness can replay 24h scenario: 18h steady-state (15k tokens/sec aggregate) + 6x reverse-sawtooth bursts per day
Generates per-minute metrics for tokens/sec, p95, p99, infra CPU/GPU utilization, KV cache hit ratio, and cold-start count
Regression detector flags deviations when p95 exceeds baseline by >15% sustained for 10 minutes
Thermostat controller config reduces noisy autoscale churn: empirically <= 2 scale actions per burst window
Integration hooks emit events to observability pipeline and create a GitHub issue template on regression detection
plan: 1) Build workload generator extension to support reverse-sawtooth ramp-down profile and warm/cold request mixes
2) Instrument runtime probes for per-actor KV cache hit/miss and prefix cache retention metrics
3) Implement thermostatic setpoint controller with configurable hysteresis and backoff windows
4) Wire metrics to regression detector; define rolling baseline snapshots with windowed quantiles
5) Run initial 48-hour validation on dedicated staging cluster and iterate thresholds
6) Publish runbook and Grafana dashboard; schedule long soak on customer-like traffic patterns
risks: Thermostat tuning may mask genuine capacity shortages if hysteresis too wide
Reverse-sawtooth bursts could trigger subtle scheduler pathologies not seen in symmetric bursts
KV cache instrumentation adds minor overhead; ensure sampling rate doesn't perturb p95
False positives in regression detector from upstream infra changes (node kernel/hypervisor updates)
comments: 2025-11-18 Maya Chen: Created ticket. Motivation: reproduce real-world operator responses to slow p95 drift and validate that autoscaler heuristics won't over-react during short ramp-downs.

2025-11-20 Ethan Park: Spike prototype for reverse-sawtooth generator complete. Initial generator accepts ramp-down duration, trough-time, and steady plateau. Observed that generator needs randomized jitter to avoid synchronized bursts across clients.
2025-11-24 QA Run: 6-hour smoke on staging. Metrics collected. Observed transient p95 spikes during trough recovery — investigating whether KV cache TTL renewal is correlated.

2025-11-26 Feature review with PM/Design: Agreed to expose two knobs in Console: setpoint target (tokens/sec) and hysteresis window (seconds). PM requested a safe default that errs on fewer scale actions.
2025-11-29 Engineering note: Implemented thermostat backoff (exponential) to cap scale frequency. Unit tests added for controller edge cases.

2025-12-01 Observability: Regression detector baseline computed from last 14 days of stable runs. Baseline p95=78ms, baseline tokens/sec=15.2k. Current 24h run shows p95 median=92ms (18% up) during two burst windows — detector flagged and created candidate issue.

2025-12-02 Ethan Park: Investigating flagged regressions. Early hypothesis: node kernel patch on staging resulted in slower CUDA driver response under partial packing. Scheduling dedicated soak on hardware pinned to previous kernel to isolate.
TODO: Run 72h soak with pinned kernel, collect per-shard KV hit ratio, and evaluate thermostat parameters. If pass, plan rollout to canary Dedicated cluster with monitoring playbook.

source.title: Run KMS outage game day (timeout/throttling/AccessDenied) + capture follow-ups
source.creator: Rafael Mendes
source.project: Security & Compliance Foundation: KMS integration hardening + rotation docs
source.description:
## Goal
Validate end-to-end behavior and operator readiness when KMS is degraded or misconfigured across Hosted/Dedicated/Private deployment modes.

We want to confirm:
- KMS wrapper timeouts/retry budgets/circuit breaker prevent retry storms + cascading failures
- Error taxonomy is consistent (logs/metrics), and on-call can quickly triage
- Dashboards/alerts are actionable (not noisy) and point to correct remediation
- Runbooks cover the most common failure modes with concrete steps
- Private installer preflight checks catch the “obvious” misconfigs before customers hit runtime failures

## Scope / systems under test
- Control plane: key version config/dual-read cutover flags
- Data plane: envelope decrypt path (runtime)
- Observability: KMS dependency health dashboard + alerts
- Private installer: KMS preflight validation (where available)

References:
- Confluence game day doc: sources/confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/game-days/kms-outage-game-day-plan-and-results-2025q4.json
- Near-miss learnings (retry storm): sources/slack/incidents/1747779033-near-miss-kms-retry-storm.json
- Prior incident (timeout spike us-east): sources/slack/incidents/1746508855-kms-timeout-spike-us-east.json
- Epic: sources/linear/engineering/ENG-4821-kms-integration-hardening-rotation-docs-epic.json

## Scenarios (run in order)
### Scenario A — Regional KMS latency + intermittent timeouts (most realistic)
**Injection:** Add 2–4s latency + 10–20% timeout rate for KMS calls in staging (us-east). Duration 20 minutes.
**Expected behavior:**
- Wrapper enforces per-call timeout and bounded retries with jitter
- Circuit breaker opens after threshold; service returns controlled errors (no unbounded queue growth)
- No retry amplification; request rate to KMS drops once breaker opens
- Alerts trigger once and point to the correct dashboard panels

### Scenario B — KMS throttling (rate limiting)
**Injection:** Simulate throttling responses and/or reduce KMS quota to force 429/Throttling. Duration 15 minutes.
**Expected behavior:**
- Backoff/jitter observed; no synchronized retry bursts
- Backpressure/throttle budget protects KMS and preserves overall system health
- Error taxonomy shows Throttling, not Timeout, and remediation guidance is consistent

### Scenario C — Permission regression / AccessDenied
**Injection:** Remove a required decrypt permission from the staging role (or swap to a role missing kms:Decrypt). Duration 10 minutes.
**Expected behavior:**
- Fail fast with AccessDenied classification (no retries)
- Operator-facing errors include missing action/principal hints
- Runbook leads on-call to confirm principal/ARN mismatch quickly

### Scenario D (optional if time) — Key disabled / wrong key version during cutover
**Injection:** Disable the “new” key version while dual-read is enabled; then attempt cutover.
**Expected behavior:**
- Dual-read continues to succeed via old key; cutover is blocked / rollback guidance is clear
- Audit log events emitted for toggles and failed cutover attempts

## Participants / roles
- Facilitator: Rafael Mendes (SRE)
- Incident Commander (IC): Tomáš Novák (On-call readiness)
- Comms lead: Fiona McCarthy (Security Ops)
- Observers / scribes: Allison Grant (observability standards), Kevin Osei (security telemetry)
- Control plane support: Ethan Park / Benji Okafor
- Private deployments support: Vivek Kulkarni / Bruno Silva
- Security escalation: Marcus Lin / Hanae Suzuki

## Schedule
- Prep review (30 min): 2025-11-18 10:00–10:30 PT
- Game day execution (90 min): 2025-11-19 13:00–14:30 PT
- Hotwash (30 min): 2025-11-19 14:30–15:00 PT
- Follow-up triage + ticketing (30 min): 2025-11-20 10:00–10:30 PT

Zoom bridge: “SRE War Room” (calendar invite sent by Rafael)
Primary Slack channel: #eng-sre (threaded)
Secondary/executive updates (if needed): #incidents (no paging; informational only)

## Pre-req checklist (must be true before execution)
- [x] Staging environment selected + traffic generator ready
- [x] Fault injection mechanism confirmed (timeouts/latency/throttling/AccessDenied)
- [x] Dashboards deployed and readable for participants (ENG-4828)
- [x] Feature flags documented (dual-read/cutover toggles)
- [x] On-call runbook draft available (kms decrypt failures)

## Comms plan
- T-15 min: announce start in #eng-sre with link to dashboard + runbook
- During event: IC posts status updates every 10 minutes in the same thread (what’s injected, what we’re seeing, next step)
- If staging-only impact escalates unexpectedly (e.g., cross-environment spill): IC escalates to #incidents and pauses injections
- End of event: facilitator posts summary + initial action list and assigns owners

Template for updates (copy/paste):
- Time:
- Scenario:
- Injection details:
- Observed behavior (latency, error rate, breaker state):
- Alerts fired (yes/no, noisy/actionable):
- Operator experience (runbook usefulness):
- Decision (continue/pause/rollback):

## Success criteria
- No cascading failures (no sustained queue growth, no runaway retries)
- Circuit breaker behavior matches ADR-007 expectations (opens/closes predictably)
- Alerts are actionable (<= 1 page for each scenario; no alert storms)
- On-call can diagnose root cause within 10 minutes using dashboards/logs/runbook
- Clear remediation steps exist for AccessDenied + throttling

## Results (executed 2025-11-19)
### What worked
- Timeout/latency scenario triggered the expected KMS latency panels; traces clearly showed KMS spans as top contributor.
- AccessDenied was correctly classified (no retries), and the error message included missing permission hints.
- Circuit breaker prevented retry amplification; KMS request volume dropped after breaker opened.

### Gaps found
1) Alert noise: KMS latency alert fired repeatedly across regions because panels were not properly scoped by env/region.
2) Runbook gap: runbook lacked a “first 5 minutes” checklist and a direct log query for mapping error code -> taxonomy category.
3) Throttling remediation: unclear guidance on quota vs client-side backoff; needed a link to AWS quota request guidance for customer-managed KMS.
4) Private operator experience: preflight report format was helpful, but the error text for wrong region/key ARN could be more explicit.
5) Cutover safety: dual-read status wasn’t visible in the standard dashboard; required manual checking of config.

## Follow-up actions (created / linked)
- [x] Reduce retry amplification on timeouts (hotfix): sources/github/redwood/pr-18563-hotfix-reduce-kms-retry-storm-on-timeout.json
- [x] Add throttle budget/backpressure integration: sources/github/redwood/pr-18570-add-kms-throttle-budget-and-backpressure.json
- [ ] Tune alert thresholds + scope by env/region (owner: Allison Grant) — follow-up in ENG-4828 comments
- [ ] Update runbook with “first 5 minutes” triage checklist + log queries (owner: Tomáš Novák) — follow-up in sources/confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/kms-decrypt-failures-runbook.json
- [ ] Add dashboard panel for dual-read/cutover flag state (owner: Nadia Rahman) — to be filed as new ENG ticket
- [ ] Improve Private installer messaging for wrong region/key ARN (owner: Bruno Silva) — follow-up on sources/github/redwood-private-installer/pr-392-add-kms-preflight-validation-and-permission-checks.json

## Notes
- This game day is intended to be repeatable quarterly, especially before enabling rotation/cutover for new Private customers.
- Evidence artifacts captured: screenshots of dashboards during each scenario, Slack thread transcript, and a short hotwash summary added to the Confluence game day page.
- Related discussions:
  - Alert noise thread: sources/slack/eng-sre/1746280021-kms-latency-alerts-too-noisy.json
  - Scenario brainstorming: sources/slack/eng-sre/1747024911-game-day-scenarios-for-kms-outage.json

source.title: Attention kernel choosing safety-net and synthetic benchmark simulator
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem: We continue to see intermittent latency regressions and memory spikes when the kernel selector flips between FlashAttention v2 and v3 at certain batch/sequence-shape combinations during prefill and decode. Root cause is unclear across cases: some relate to v3 kernel workspace fragmentation, others to startup jitter when speculative warmup probes run.

Goal: Build a synthetic benchmark + simulator that can: 1) reliably reproduce the shape/occupancy conditions that trigger regressions, 2) exercise the kernel selection logic (including hysteresis, gating, and fallback), and 3) produce a set of safety thresholds and automatic rollback triggers for staged rollouts. The simulator should be lightweight, deterministic for CI, and capable of running on CI/GHA runners and on an engineering GPU pool.

Scope: implement workload generator, kernel selector harness (pluggable for v2/v3/quantized variants), telemetry exporters (latency, mem footprint, workspace alloc/reuse stats, kernel switch frequency), regression detector, and an evaluation dashboard export. Deliverables include a reproducible harness, a README with experiment recipes, an initial set of safety thresholds, and a PR with a canary rollout plan tied to runtime-1.19.
source.acceptance_criteria:
1) Simulator can reproduce at least 3 distinct regression signatures (high tail latency, workspace OOM, kernel oscillation) deterministically given a seed.
2) Harness supports pluggable kernel implementations: FlashAttention v2, v3, and quantized shim.
3) Telemetry hooks emit the metrics required by the SRE dashboard and the regression detector.
4) Safety thresholds defined and validated via at least 5 synthetic experiments.
5) Canary/rollback gating documented and an automated rollback hook prototype submitted as a PR.
comments: 2025-11-05: Ticket created after triage meeting — PM highlighted customer-reported tail latency spikes for long-context summarization workloads.
2025-11-07: Spike archetypes collected from prod telemetry and mapped to shape buckets (short/long tokens x small/large batches). Added initial list to the ticket attachments.

2025-11-10: Decided to implement simulator in Python with a small C++ CUDA kernel shim for precise workspace accounting. Rationale: faster iteration vs full runtime changes; will reuse existing runtime kernel interface for plug-compatibility.

2025-11-12: Diego opened draft PR: github.com/redwood/runtime/compare/eng/eng-702314-sim (WIP). Added prototype workload generator that replays token-length distributions sampled from production traces.
2025-11-14: Design review notes: PM requests clear mapping between synthetic experiments and customer-facing SLOs. Infra asks that the harness can run on a single A100 with deterministic seeding.

2025-11-16: Implemented telemetry emitters for: per-request prefill latency, decode tail latency (p50/p95/p99), workspace peak/resident, kernel switch events, and KV cache hit/miss. Export format: Prometheus pushgateway + JSON summary for CI artifacts.

2025-11-18: Regression detector prototype in place: looks for >20% p99 delta or >1.5x workspace increase vs baseline. Working through false positives with QA; tuned thresholds down after initial runs.

2025-11-20: Engineering sync: preparing canary gating proposal for runtime-1.19. Next steps: harden PR, add integration test in gating pipeline, run expanded experiment matrix across sequence-length x batch-size x quantization settings.

source.title: Quantization load guardrails: qprofile-audit harness for perf/quality tradeoffs
source.creator: Diego Ramos
source.description: Build a reproducible, auditable benchmarking harness (qprofile-audit) to evaluate FP16/INT8/INT4 quant profiles across representative workloads and detect regressions in tokens/sec, p95 latency, and output quality. The goal is an actionable guardrail that allows model onboarding and automatic rollbacks if a quant profile causes unacceptable quality or latency regressions for production workloads. This ticket covers harness design, dataset selection, statistical detection rules, CI integration, dashboards, and a small pilot run with 2 models.
acceptance_criteria: Harness produces matrixed results for batch sizes [1,4,8,16,32] and seq lengths [64,128,512,1024] with tokens/sec and p95 recorded
Quality metrics (embedding cosine, ROUGE-L or summarization BLEU-like metric, and classifier-based hallucination rate) reported per cell
Statistical regression detector flags changes where p95 latency increases >10% or quality metric drops >1.0 absolute point (or >3-sigma by bootstrap) and writes a human-readable summary
Nightly CI job runs on dedicated GPU pool and stores artifacts in s3://redwood-qprofile-audit/YYYY-MM-DD/
Dashboard panels for tokens/sec, p95, memory, and quality deltas; alerts to #infra-oncall when a regression gate trips
tasks: Design micro-benchmark harness and CLI (qprofile-audit) that accepts model, quant-profile, dataset, batch/seq grid
Implement runner that can call runtime endpoints and local dedicated runtime with perf telemetry (tokens/sec, p95, mem peak)
Integrate quality chores: embed-sim, summarization ROUGE-L, hallucination classifier; provide calibration dataset
Create regression detector using paired bootstrap + EWMA; implement auto-summary and threshold config
Wire results to console dashboards and add alert rules
Run pilot on two models (rw-13b and rw-30b-lite) and produce recommendation docs
comments: 2025-11-05 Diego Ramos: Spike complete. Prototype can run a 5x5 grid in ~45m on a single A100. Measured tokens/sec via runtime counters and validated against sample-based timer. Next: formalize dataset and quality metrics.

2025-11-07 Ava Chen: Decided to keep FP16 as baseline. For quality metrics we will use: cosine similarity on embeddings (threshold 0.985), ROUGE-L for summarization prompts, and a lightweight hallucination detector trained on internal evals. Added candidate calibration set of 1k prompts.

2025-11-09 Priya Nair (review): Watch out for tokenization variance influencing p95; suggestion to pin tokenizer and run warmup requests to populate KV cache before measurement. Also suggested 10 warmup sequences per run.
2025-11-11 Ava Chen: Implemented warmup and KV cache warmup option. Also instrumented per-request flags to record fallback-to-FP32 occurrences (we saw ~0.7% fallback on early INT8 layer config).

2025-11-13 Bench run results: INT8 increased tokens/sec 2.3x vs FP16 on rw-13b with batch=8, seq=512 but ROUGE-L dropped 0.7 points. INT4 shows 3.8x throughput but ROUGE-L drop 2.3 points and hallucination rate increase. Logged detailed CSVs to s3 and pushed initial analysis to GH PR #1289.

2025-11-15 Diego Ramos: Implemented bootstrap-based regression detector. Rule: flag if (a) p95 increases >10% vs baseline OR (b) quality delta exceeds configured absolute threshold (default 1.0 point) OR (c) bootstrap 95% CI excludes zero. Detector produces a short natural-language summary for console and a machine-readable 'regression' tag in the artifact manifest.

2025-11-17 QA: Ran pilot on rw-30b-lite; found INT8 with per-layer scaling + per-channel calibration gives acceptable tradeoff for latency-sensitive customers. Need to add a 'hybrid' profile that keeps first N transformer layers FP16; create follow-up ENG task for automated per-layer profiling (ENG-77298).

2025-11-18 Ava Chen: PR #1296 adds dashboard panels and alert. Seeking review from infra and runtime teams for rollout gating. Blocking: finalize production thresholds (action required from product to approve default tolerances).

source.title: Dynamic SLI onboarding and traffic escrow policy for prioritized conversational endpoints
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Background: As we onboard more conversational routes and tuned model variants, teams need a predictable way to declare SLIs and safely migrate traffic without risking noisy error-budget burn across regions. This ticket defines a prescriptive onboarding flow that (1) creates per-model SLIs from sampled traffic and synthetic probe signals, (2) allocates an "traffic escrow" (error-budget currency) to new model variants, and (3) wires an automated regional failover choreography tied to the escrow balance and circuit-breaker state. The goal is to reduce rollouts that unexpectedly exhaust global error budgets and to enable short-lived experiments with automatic safe ejection.

Scope:
- Define canonical SLI signals for conversational endpoints (latency P95/P99, request-quality pass rate, token-error-rate, downstream HTTP error rate).
- Specify sampling rates and synthetic probe cadence for initial SLI estimation.
- Design the traffic escrow model: how much traffic/time is allocated to a variant, how escrow is consumed and replenished, and how credits are reconciled across regions.
- Define failover choreography triggered by escrow depletion, circuit open events, or regional capacity pressure (including warm-standby behaviors).
- Produce operational playbook snippets for oncall: what alerts mean, automated remediation steps, and manual escalation checklist.

Out of scope: long-term SLI visualization UI improvements (console work will be a follow-up), and per-tenant economic billing changes.
acceptance_criteria: A canonical SLI schema document exists (CSV/Markdown) listing signal name, collection window, aggregation, and alert thresholds.
A reproducible onboarding runbook is checked into docs with required telemetry sources and sample commands to bootstrap SLI from 24h of traffic.
Traffic escrow model defined with formulas and example allocations for small/medium/large variants; unit tests for escrow accounting logic exist.
A simulation run (CI job) demonstrates the escrow-driven rollout ejection behavior for a synthetic burst scenario and produces expected failover traces.
Playbook for oncall with clear mappings between escrow alerts, circuit breaker states, and automated rollback actions exists and is reviewed by SRE and Product.
No changes to live traffic routing outside of test/simulation without explicit rollout approval documented in PRs.
implementation_notes: Phase 1: Define SLI schema and sampling plan; implement SLI extractor in telemetry pipelines (sigma rules + new prometheus exporters).
Phase 2: Implement escrow accounting as a small service (stateless API + durable ledger) and unit tests; integrate with rollout controller (canary actor).
Phase 3: Wire escrow depletion events into regional failover state machine and circuit-breaker signals; add safety checks for cross-region credits transfer.
Phase 4: Write CI simulation harness that replays historical traffic and injects faults to validate ejection and failover logic.
Keep changes behind feature flag and gate rollout to internal dev clusters first.
work_log: 2025-11-04 Ava Chen: Created ticket after reliability sync; drafted initial scope and identified dependencies on telemetry and rollout controllers.
2025-11-07 Diego Martinez: Spike results: 24h sampling prototype produced stable P95 baseline for two conversational routes; added sampling cadence notes.
2025-11-10 SRE Review (Anika Rao): Suggested adding token-error-rate as explicit SLI and tighter guardrails for cross-region credit transfers.
2025-11-12 Product Review (Rohan Gupta): Approved escrow model in principle; requested example allocations for enterprise vs trial customers.
2025-11-15 Diego Martinez: Implemented escrow accounting PoC (ledger model) and pushed draft PR for internal review: github.com/redwood/infra/pull/2041.
2025-11-18 Ava Chen: Added CI simulation job and sample dataset; filing follow-up ticket for Console visualization (PM link included).
notes: Tradeoffs considered: higher sampling reduces noise but increases cost; chosen default sampling is 1% for low-traffic routes and 10% for high-traffic routes during onboarding window.
We opted for an escrow ledger that favors time-based decay over token-based refunds to simplify cross-region reconciliation.
Potential future improvement: integrate escrow with cost-metering so that enterprise customers can purchase additional safe-escrow capacity during major launches.

source.title: Disconnected-client heuristics and partial-output fanout cleanup
source.creator: Asha Malik
source.description:
Problem: When clients disconnect mid-generation we occasionally see duplicated partial payloads, lingering per-generation state in memory, and cross-tenant KV cache pollution during 'fanout' (when a single generation is mirrored to multiple downstream consumers). This causes elevated memory usage, confusing logs that look like duplicate outputs, and complicates function/tool-call coordination for resumed sessions.

Goal: Implement a deterministic, idempotent cleanup and resume protocol that: (1) detects and cordons disconnected clients quickly, (2) marks partial generations with a compact emission fingerprint (EID) to prevent duplicate downstream fanout, (3) quarantines orphaned generation state in a TTL-based soft-quarantine for safe rollback/inspection, and (4) exposes clear metrics and alerts for stale-generation accumulation.

Constraints: Must be backward-compatible with existing SDKs (compat shim for older clients), preserve structured function-call atomicity for resumed sessions, and keep end-to-end latency impact <= 5% at p95 under normal load. Non-goal: redesigning the entire lease/token system or changing cross-region routing policies in this ticket.
acceptance_criteria: Disconnected clients are cordoned within an average of <= 6s after the final heartbeat gap (configurable).
Every partial generation record includes a stable EID (emission-id) that prevents duplicate writes across the fanout layer.
Orphaned generation state is moved to a soft-quarantine store with a configurable TTL (default 30m) and is eligible for automated GC after TTL expiry.
Server-side logs and dashboards show a new metric 'stream.orphaned_generations' and an alert when it grows > 50 in 5m for a tenant.
Resume handshake succeeds end-to-end for 3 tested SDK versions (2 legacy, 1 current) with function-call rehydration preserved.
A migration/compatibility shim handles older clients that lack explicit disconnect signals without dropping in-flight function calls.
tasks: Design: document EID format, cordon state lifecycle, and quarantine schema
Prototype: server-side EID stamping and fanout de-duplication layer
Integrate: cordon signal emitter into session manager and add TTL quarantine storage
SDK change: small compat shim to emit graceful-disconnect and resume token (opt-in for legacy clients)
E2E tests: simulate abrupt disconnects, partial generation replay, and multi-consumer fanout
Monitoring: add metrics, dashboards, and SLO alert rule
Rollout: canary on non-critical region, validate metrics, then global rollout
source.implementation_plan: We will add an immutable emission-id (EID) to every generation at the point of creation: EID = sha1(session_id || gen_seq || truncated-create-ts). The fanout writer will be made idempotent keyed by (tenant_id, EID, target_stream). On client disconnect detection the session manager will set a cordon flag on the session and start a short soft-quarantine. During quarantine, reads of the generation return a special header 'X-QUARANTINED: true' and a debug id for inspection; downstream consumers treat quarantined payloads as read-only and cannot re-fanout. After TTL expiry the quarantine sweeper attempts a best-effort compaction: merge prefix caches when safe or GC remote KV when not. For resume we add an idempotent rehydrate endpoint that accepts EID and resume-token; it verifies that function-call boundaries are intact by checking call-scope checksums and replaying tool-call attachments if needed. Backpressure mapping: existing client backpressure hints (XB_HINT) are extended with a 'cordon-able' flag that allows server to reply with 429+Retry-After to abusive reconnect loops. All changes are gated behind a feature-flag 'orphan-quarantine-v1' for staged rollout.
investigation_findings: Observed incident 2025-10-12 where a large consumer disconnect left ~78 orphaned generations in the region causing memory pressure (see incident-runbook link).
Reproduction steps: simulate TCP drop during a long-running generation with 8MB KV cache; generation state persisted but fanout layer retried and duplicated outputs to sinks.
Root cause: fanout writes were not keyed with a stable emission identity and retry paths assumed at-most-once semantics.
Mitigation: short-term rate-limit retries and increase prefix-cache eviction to reduce memory footprint (rolled out as hotfix ENG-299900).
comments: 2025-11-03 - Asha Malik: Created ticket after post-incident review. Initial design doc linked. Targeting runtime-1.20 for rollout.
2025-11-04 - Ethan Park: Did initial spike - prototype EID generator and idempotent fanout writer in local branch. Observed 0.7% p95 latency increase in microbench for EID stamping; within budget.
2025-11-05 - Priya Raman (SRE): Reviewed approach; asked for stronger metrics around quarantine growth and failure modes. Requested an additional alert for quarantine-sweeper failures.

2025-11-06 - QA: Added e2e test matrix for legacy SDK pairing; discovered an edge-case where legacy clients sending no-close signals still caused double-fanout during hedged reconnections. Added task to SDK compat shim.
2025-11-07 - Ethan Park: Opened PR https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/5821 with prototype. Requesting architecture review from runtime leads and SRE before canary.
2025-11-07 - Lina Gomez (PM): Approved scope and requested a concise customer-facing note for any tenants that see quarantine alerts during rollout.

source.title: CI: reproducible model release flow with signed artifacts and profile promotion
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Implement a reproducible CI/CD flow that produces versioned model artifacts, cryptographically signs the build outputs and performance profiles, and promotes artifacts through staging -> canary -> prod with automated checks. The goal is to remove manual steps in onboarding new model weights and their associated performance profiles, provide verifiable provenance for sensitive customer deployments, and enable safe, auditable rollouts. Key pieces: 1) deterministic packaging of model artifacts + manifest (weights, tokenizer, quantization metadata, build environment hash); 2) artifact signing and attestation (Sigstore/cosign integration + Rekor transparency); 3) automated perf-profile generation, validation and version publish to the model catalog; 4) promotion workflow (staging acceptance tests, canary rollout with health checks and evaluation harness tie-in, automatic rollback on regressions); 5) integration with Dedicated/Private pinning manifests so deployments reference signed version tags rather than floating refs.
acceptance_criteria: CI job produces a single versioned artifact bundle (tar.gz) and a JSON manifest with SHA256 checksums for each file
Artifact bundle and manifest are signed with cosign and an attestation is recorded in Rekor
Perf profile JSON is generated by the eval job, validated against a schema, and published to the internal model-catalog API with a semantic version
A deployable release tag is created in git and the onboarding pipeline can trigger a staging deployment that references the signed artifact tag
Canary rollout can be initiated from CI and automatically increments weight if smoke tests and eval metrics pass; automatic rollback triggers on latency/cost/quality regressions
All steps are logged and linkable from a release artifact page in Console for audit purposes
tasks: Add build step to package model artifacts and embed deterministic build environment (docker digest + pip freeze + env hash)
Implement cosign signing step and Rekor upload in GitHub Actions job 'release/sign-and-publish'
Extend perf-eval job to emit profile-{model}:{semver}.json and publish to model-catalog via authenticated service account
Create pipeline job that validates perf-profile against control prompts and fails if regression > threshold
Wire promotion workflow that updates deployment pin in dev/staging/prod manifests and records promotion provenance
Document the flow in Confluence and add an ops runbook for rollbacks and emergency unsigned-acceptance
comments: 2025-09-10 - Ava Chen: Initial ticket created. Prototype notes: local deterministic packaging worked when container base immutability enforced. Need to pick signing solution (cosign vs in-house).

2025-09-12 - Diego Martinez: Spike completed for cosign + Rekor. Key management approach: use KMS-wrapped key for cosign (service account) and rotate monthly. See spike doc: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/infra/ci-signing-spike
2025-09-15 - Priya Nair (Security): Security review requested. Concern: private key handling in CI. Suggested use of ephemeral signing keys via Sigstore OIDC flow. Added label 'security'.
2025-09-18 - Diego Martinez: Implemented GH Actions job 'ci/sign-and-publish' and posted draft PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/platform/pull/1986. Unit tests for manifest format added.
2025-09-21 - Carlos Ruiz (Platform): Hooked model-catalog publish endpoint; perf profile upload requires service account with scope 'catalog:write'. Created IAM role 'svc-model-publisher'.
2025-09-23 - Diego Martinez: Canary promotion prototype working. Added automated rollback on eval delta > 2% or p95 latency increase > 20ms. Need UX for promotion audit in Console.
