source.title: Add burst configuration to Control Plane API (tenant enablement, limits, priority tier, validation, audit hooks)
source.creator: Ethan Park
source.description:
Context
Dedicated Burst Capacity requires a tenant-scoped configuration surface in the Control Plane so we can:
- enable/disable burst per Dedicated tenant
- set hard caps/guardrails that admission/routing can enforce deterministically
- expose read APIs to Console + support tooling
- produce audit events for all config changes (including admin overrides)

This ticket is specifically the Control Plane API + validation + audit logging hooks. The admission controller and routing integration are tracked separately.

Scope (in)
1) Tenant model/config schema
- Add a BurstConfig block on the tenant (or dedicated capacity config) with:
  - enabled: boolean
  - max_burst_ratio: float (relative to baseline reserved capacity) OR max_burst_gpus: int (absolute cap), with clear precedence rules
  - max_burst_gpus: int (optional)
  - priority_tier: enum (e.g., standard | silver | gold) used only for fairness weighting; must not allow starvation of non-priority tenants
  - cooldown_seconds: int (minimum time after sustained burst usage before admitting additional burst beyond baseline; exact semantics owned by admission controller but validated here)
  - min_burst_deny_retry_after_seconds: int (optional; used for 429 guidance / Retry-After)
  - notes: string (optional, internal-only; for CS/Sales context)

2) Control Plane APIs
- Admin API (internal) to set burst config for a tenant
  - PATCH /internal/v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/burst-config
  - GET   /internal/v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/burst-config
- Customer-facing read API (used by Console) to display burst enablement + limits
  - GET /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/burst-config (read-only; gated to tenant principals)

API response should include:
- effective_config (after defaults + validation)
- last_updated_at
- last_updated_by (redacted appropriately for customer view)

3) Validation + defaults (server-side)
- enabled default: false
- max_burst_ratio default: 1.5 for beta tenants unless explicitly overridden (safe default; can be adjusted later without schema changes)
- max_burst_ratio allowed range: [1.0, 3.0] (beta); reject outside range
- max_burst_gpus allowed range: [0, 512] with additional guard: cannot exceed fleet safety cap policy for the tenant’s region/hardware class (enforced via policy engine rule; Control Plane validates using policy evaluation results)
- priority_tier allowed values: standard, silver, gold (future-proofed: reject unknown)
- cooldown_seconds allowed range: [0, 3600]
- Precedence: if max_burst_gpus is set, admission uses min(max_burst_gpus, floor(max_burst_ratio * baseline_gpus)); if only ratio is set, cap is ratio * baseline; if neither set, use defaults.
- Reject invalid combinations:
  - enabled=true and max_burst_ratio < 1.0
  - max_burst_gpus set but tenant has no Dedicated baseline (Dedicated-only feature)
  - priority_tier != standard unless tenant has burst enabled

4) Audit logging hooks
- Emit audit events on:
  - burst_config.enabled changed
  - burst_config.max_burst_ratio changed
  - burst_config.max_burst_gpus changed
  - burst_config.priority_tier changed
  - burst_config.cooldown_seconds changed
  - burst_config overridden via emergency / admin override path (include override reason)
- Event requirements:
  - actor (user/service), actor_type, request_id, tenant_id
  - before/after (with safe redaction for internal notes)
  - source: console | api | internal_tool

Out of scope (for this ticket)
- Per-region overrides / cross-region bursting controls (explicitly out of scope for initial release)
- Customer-writable burst config (beta/GA is Redwood-enabled only; customers can view in Console)
- Admission semantics (queue vs immediate deny) beyond what’s needed for API shape

Implementation notes
- Store in tenant config model so routing + admission can consume same source of truth via existing config distribution.
- Avoid high-cardinality audit payloads; keep diffs concise.
- All writes must go through RBAC check (Control Plane)
  - internal write: platform-admin, dedicated-ops
  - customer read: tenant-admin + dedicated-viewer
- Include propagation expectations in response headers (we’ve seen customer confusion when Console lags).
  - Add "x-config-propagation-estimate-seconds" header (best-effort) OR include in response message.

Acceptance criteria
- [x] New BurstConfig fields added to tenant schema + persisted
- [x] PATCH endpoint supports partial updates and validates ranges/combos
- [x] GET endpoints return effective config (defaults applied) and metadata (last updated)
- [x] RBAC enforced for read/write paths
- [x] Audit events emitted for all config changes and admin override
- [x] Backward compatible migration (existing tenants unaffected; enabled=false default)
- [x] Unit tests for validation and RBAC; integration test for audit event emission
- [x] Docs for internal operators (runbook snippet: how to enable for a tenant; what fields mean)

Links
- PRD: sources/confluence/product-docs/requirements/dedicated-burst-capacity-prd.json
- Design spec: sources/confluence/product-docs/design-specs/burst-pool-architecture-and-guardrails.json
- Audit events spec: sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/burst-capacity-audit-events.json
- Related tickets:
  - ENG-4810 implement admission controller service
  - ENG-4821 cooldown + fairness enforcement
  - ENG-4881 denial reason codes + support tooling

- GitHub PR: sources/github/redwood/pr-23866-add-audit-log-events-for-burst-config-changes.json
- GitHub PR (Console read APIs): sources/github/redwood/pr-23905-console-api-endpoints-for-burst-usage.json

Notes for rollout
- Feature-gate write path behind "burst_config_writes_enabled" (default false). Enable only for internal tools during beta.
- Tenant allowlist: do not allow enabled=true unless tenant is on the Dedicated burst beta allowlist (Control Plane check; list managed by Product Ops / Dedicated Ops).

Open questions (resolved)
- Ratio vs absolute cap: support both; admission uses the tighter cap.
- Customer editability: read-only for GA v1; revisit after we have denial reason + billing UX in place.

title: Add burst configuration to Control Plane API (tenant enablement, limits, priority tier, validation, audit hooks)
preview:
Context
Dedicated Burst Capacity requires a tenant-scoped configuration surface in the Control Plane so we can:
- enable/disable burst per Dedicated tenant
- set hard caps/guardrails that admission/routing can enforce deterministically
- expose read APIs to Console + support tooling
- produce audit events for all config changes (including admin overrides)

This ticket is specifically the Control Plane API + validation + audit logging hooks. The admission controller and routing integration are tracked separately.

Scope (in)
1) Tenant model/config schema
- Add a BurstConfig block on the tenant (or dedicated capacity config) with:
  - enabled: boolean
  - max_burst_ratio: float (relative to baseline reserved capacity) OR max_burst_gpus: int (absolute cap), with clear precedence rules
  - max_burst_gpus: int (optional)
  - priority_tier: enum (e.g., standard | silver | gold) used only for fairness weighting; must not allow starvation of non-priority tenants
  - cooldown_seconds: int (minimum time after sustained burst usage before admitting additional burst beyond baseline; exact semantics owned by admission controller but validated here)
  - min_burst_deny_retry_after_seconds: int (optional; used for 429 guidance / Retry-After)
  - notes: string (optional, internal-only; for CS/Sales context)

2) Control Plane APIs
- Admin API (internal) to set burst config for a tenant
  - PATCH /internal/v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/burst-config
  - GET   /internal/v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/burst-config
- Customer-facing read API (used by Console) to display burst enablement + limits
  - GET /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/burst-config (read-only; gated to tenant principals)

API response should include:
- effective_config (after defaults + validation)
- last_updated_at
- last_updated_by (redacted appropriately for customer view)

3) Validation + defaults (server-side)
- enabled default: false
- max_burst_ratio default: 1.5 for beta tenants unless explicitly overridden (safe default; can be adjusted later without schema changes)
- max_burst_ratio allowed range: [1.0, 3.0] (beta); reject outside range
- max_burst_gpus allowed range: [0, 512] with additional guard: cannot exceed fleet safety cap policy for the tenant’s region/hardware class (enforced via policy engine rule; Control Plane validates using policy evaluation results)
- priority_tier allowed values: standard, silver, gold (future-proofed: reject unknown)
- cooldown_seconds allowed range: [0, 3600]
- Precedence: if max_burst_gpus is set, admission uses min(max_burst_gpus, floor(max_burst_ratio * baseline_gpus)); if only ratio is set, cap is ratio * baseline; if neither set, use defaults.
- Reject invalid combinations:
  - enabled=true and max_burst_ratio < 1.0
  - max_burst_gpus set but tenant has no Dedicated baseline (Dedicated-only feature)
  - priority_tier != standard unless tenant has burst enabled

4) Audit logging hooks
- Emit audit events on:
  - burst_config.enabled changed
  - burst_config.max_burst_ratio changed
  - burst_config.max_burst_gpus changed
  - burst_config.priority_tier changed
  - burst_config.cooldown_seconds changed
  - burst_config overridden via emergency / admin override path (include override reason)
- Event requirements:
  - actor (user/service), actor_type, request_id, tenant_id
  - before/after (with safe redaction for internal notes)
  - source: console | api | internal_tool

Out of scope (for this ticket)
- Per-region overrides / cross-region bursting controls (explicitly out of scope for initial release)
- Customer-writable burst config (beta/GA is Redwood-enabled only; customers can view in Console)
- Admission semantics (queue vs immediate deny) beyond what’s needed for API shape

Implementation notes
- Store in tenant config model so routing + admission can consume same source of truth via existing config distribution.
- Avoid high-cardinality audit payloads; keep diffs concise.
- All writes must go through RBAC check (Control Plane)
  - internal write: platform-admin, dedicated-ops
  - customer read: tenant-admin + dedicated-viewer
- Include propagation expectations in response headers (we’ve seen customer confusion when Console lags).
  - Add "x-config-propagation-estimate-seconds" header (best-effort) OR include in response message.

Acceptance criteria
- [x] New BurstConfig fields added to tenant schema + persisted
- [x] PATCH endpoint supports partial updates and validates ranges/combos
- [x] GET endpoints return effective config (defaults applied) and metadata (last updated)
- [x] RBAC enforced for read/write paths
- [x] Audit events emitted for all config changes and admin override
- [x] Backward compatible migration (existing tenants unaffected; enabled=false default)
- [x] Unit tests for validation and RBAC; integration test for audit event emission
- [x] Docs for internal operators (runbook snippet: how to enable for a tenant; what fields mean)

- Design spec: sources/confluence/product-docs/design-specs/burst-pool-architecture-and-guardrails.json
- Audit events spec: sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/burst-capacity-audit-events.json
- Related tickets:
  - ENG-4810 implement admission controller service
  - ENG-4821 cooldown + fairness enforcement
  - ENG-4881 denial reason codes + support tooling

Deliverables / code pointers
- GitHub PR: sources/github/redwood/pr-23741-add-burst-config-schema-to-tenant-model.json
- GitHub PR: sources/github/redwood/pr-23866-add-audit-log-events-for-burst-config-changes.json
- GitHub PR (Console read APIs): sources/github/redwood/pr-23905-console-api-endpoints-for-burst-usage.json

Notes for rollout
- Feature-gate write path behind "burst_config_writes_enabled" (default false). Enable only for internal tools during beta.
- Tenant allowlist: do not allow enabled=true unless tenant is on the Dedicated burst beta allowlist (Control Plane check; list managed by Product Ops / Dedicated Ops).

Open questions (resolved)
- Ratio vs absolute cap: support both; admission uses the tighter cap.

source.title: Filtering engine for SIEM-ready audit exports (allow/deny by category/action + actor/resource predicates)
source.creator: Ethan Park
source.project: Enterprise Security & Compliance Foundation — SIEM-ready audit export (Beta)
source.description:
Goal
Implement the filtering model for SIEM-ready audit exports to support enterprise requirements (noise reduction, least-data export) while preserving delivery guarantees and avoiding performance regressions.

This ticket covers:
- Runtime evaluation of export filters (exporter-side) for each audit event.
- Validation/normalization rules shared with control plane to prevent unsafe/expensive filters.
- Safe defaults and clear semantics (allowlist + denylist, deny wins).
- Performance tests to ensure filtering overhead is negligible at expected event volumes.

References
- Confluence ADR: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-audit-export-filtering-model.json
- Schema v2 + category taxonomy: sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/audit-event-schema-v2-and-siem-mapping.json
- Control plane export config API ticket: ENG-5132
- Exporter scaffold/config sync ticket: ENG-5139

Non-goals (v1)
- Arbitrary regex matching or JSONPath over event payloads (too risky + too expensive + hard to validate).
- Full text search over request/response bodies (prohibited by PII policy anyway).
- Cross-event correlation filtering.

Proposed semantics (v1)
1) Filter structure
- allow:
  - categories: [string]
  - actions: [string]
  - actor_types: ["user"|"service_account"|"api_key"|"system"]
  - resource_types: [string]
- deny:
  - categories/actions/actor_types/resource_types (same shapes as allow)
- predicates (optional):
  - actor_ids: [string] (exact match only; intended for service accounts / API keys)
  - resource_ids: [string] (exact match only)
  - workspace_ids: [string] (exact match)
  - source_ip_cidrs: [cidr] (optional; only evaluated when event has source_ip)

2) Matching rules
- If allow.* is empty / unset: treat as “allow all” for that dimension.
  Example: allow.categories unset means categories do not constrain.
- If allow.* is set and non-empty: event must match at least one value in each provided allow dimension.
  Example: if allow.categories AND allow.actions are set, event must match both.
- Deny is always applied. If an event matches any deny clause, it is excluded even if it matches allow.
- Predicates behave as additional allow constraints when present (i.e., must match).
- Unknown categories/actions are treated as non-matching (conservative) when allow.* is explicitly set.
- Missing optional event fields:
  - If predicate requires field (e.g., source_ip_cidrs) and event lacks it => does not match predicate (excluded if predicate is set).

3) Safe defaults
- Default export behavior when no filter is configured: export all audit events emitted by schema v2 (subject to audit event PII policy / redaction rules; filtering is not a substitute for redaction).
- For beta UI, we will recommend customers start with allow.categories only (least error-prone) and expand as needed.

Validation / guardrails
To prevent pathological configs and keep evaluation cheap:
- Hard limits:
  - max values per list: 100
  - max total values across allow+deny+predicates: 400
  - max CIDRs: 50
- Strings:
  - exact match only (no regex, no wildcards)
  - max length 128
- CIDR validation:
  - IPv4 and IPv6 supported; parsed at config load.
- Normalization:
  - de-duplicate lists
  - lower-case category/action tokens (category taxonomy is lowercase per schema v2)
- Config load failure behavior:
  - If filter fails validation, the exporter should mark destination as “config_error” and NOT export until fixed (avoid accidentally exporting too much).

Implementation notes
- Implemented as a small, allocation-friendly matcher compiled once per (tenant, destination) config refresh.
- Representation:
  - allow/deny token lists stored as hash sets for O(1) lookups.
  - CIDRs compiled to netaddr objects and checked only if event.source_ip exists and source_ip_cidrs is non-empty.
- Evaluation order (fast-fail):
  1) deny tokens (category/action/actor_type/resource_type)
  2) allow tokens
  3) predicate exact matches (workspace_id, actor_id, resource_id)
  4) CIDR predicate
- Observability:
  - exporter increments counters per destination for filtered_out_total with labels {reason=deny|allow_miss|predicate_miss} (label cardinality constrained; no category/action labels).
  - exporter logs per-batch summary at debug (not info) to avoid noisy logs.

Performance tests
Added microbench + load test coverage:
- Bench scenario: 10k events batch, 3 allow dimensions, 1 deny dimension, 10 CIDRs.
- Target: p95 evaluation overhead <= 5ms per 10k events on baseline exporter instance type (filtering should be dwarfed by encoding + network IO).
- Added regression test in CI to fail if overhead increases by >25% vs pinned baseline.

Acceptance criteria
- Export config supports allowlist/denylist by category/action/actor_type/resource_type.
- Export config supports optional predicates for actor_id/resource_id/workspace_id and source_ip CIDRs.
- Deny overrides allow.
- Empty allow dimension does not constrain.
- Exporter behavior on invalid filter is fail-closed (no export) with surfaced status.
- Unit tests cover edge cases (missing fields, unknown categories, empty lists, duplicates).
- Bench tests exist and run in CI (or nightly) with documented thresholds.

Rollout considerations
- Behind SIEM export beta feature flag; matcher compiled only for enabled tenants.
- Requires schema v2 categories/actions to be stable enough for customers to build allowlists; PM to provide canonical category list in docs.

Follow-ups (out of scope here)
- “Preview filter” endpoint in control plane / Console that shows sample counts by category over last N minutes (requires aggregation store).
- Support for “starts_with” on resource_id for hierarchical resource naming (needs careful validation).
- Tenant-level default denylist for known noisy system actions (needs Security review to avoid hiding security-relevant events).
acceptance_criteria: Implement exporter-side filter evaluation supporting allow/deny on category/action/actor_type/resource_type.
Support actor_id/resource_id/workspace_id exact-match predicates and optional source_ip CIDR predicate.
Deny wins over allow; unknown tokens conservative when allowlist is explicitly set.
Validation limits (counts/lengths) enforced; exporter fails closed on invalid filters and surfaces config_error.
Unit test coverage for missing fields, empty/mixed allow/deny, duplicates, and invalid CIDRs.
Add performance benchmark/regression coverage with documented thresholds.
source.testing_plan: Unit tests: matcher semantics (allow/deny interactions, missing field handling, normalization). Integration tests: exporter applies filter per destination and increments filtered_out_total counters with constrained reason labels. Perf: benchmark suite for compiled matcher evaluation on representative batches; threshold-based regression gate (nightly acceptable if CI runtime too high).
links: Linear dependency: ENG-5132 (export config CRUD API)
Linear dependency: ENG-5139 (exporter scaffold + config sync)
comments: 2025-02-18 (Jordan Lee): Drafted filter matcher semantics aligned to ADR. Key decision: deny overrides allow; allow unset => allow all. Avoid regex/wildcards for v1 (validation + perf + security).

2025-02-21 (Marcus Lin): +1 on fail-closed if filter invalid (better to stop export than accidentally broaden). Also confirm we cannot filter on request/response bodies; should be explicitly out of scope and enforced by PII policy.

2025-02-25 (Benji Okafor): Control plane can enforce the structural validation + limits. Exporter should still re-validate on config sync (defense in depth) and surface config_error in status endpoint.
2025-03-04 (Sean Gallagher): Please add explicit perf targets and ensure metrics do not label on category/action (cardinality). We’ll page on exporter lag/errors, not on filtered counts.
2025-03-11 (Jordan Lee): Implemented compiled matcher with hash-set membership; added CIDR parsing at compile-time. Added reason-coded filtered counter without high-card labels.
2025-03-19 (Chloe Martin): Console status needs a clear message for config_error vs downstream delivery error; please ensure exporter status includes a stable error code.
2025-03-27 (Jordan Lee): PR up with unit tests + microbench. Noted one edge case: allow.categories set + unknown event.category => excluded (conservative).

2025-04-08 (Rafael Mendes): Reviewed perf numbers on staging replay (synthetic burst). Overhead acceptable; biggest cost still gzip + adapter IO. Marking ready to close after final nit: document fail-closed behavior in runbook/FAQ.
2025-04-10 (Jordan Lee): Landed and verified with config sync + status endpoint; added short note for docs/runbook follow-up. Closing.

- deny:

- predicates (optional):
  - actor_ids: [string] (exact match only; intended for service accounts / API keys)
  - resource_ids: [string] (exact match only)
  - workspace_ids: [string] (exact match)
  - source_ip_cidrs: [cidr] (optional; only evaluated when event has source_ip)

2) Matching rules
- If allow.* is empty / unset: treat as “allow all” for that dimension.
  Example: allow.categories unset means categories do not constrain.
- If allow.* is set and non-empty: event must match at least one value in each provided allow dimension.
  Example: if allow.categories AND allow.actions are set, event must match both.
- Deny is always applied. If an event matches any deny clause, it is excluded even if it matches allow.
- Predicates behave as additional allow constraints when present (i.e., must match).
- Unknown categories/actions are treated as non-matching (conservative) when allow.* is explicitly set.
- Missing optional event fields:
  - If predicate requires field (e.g., source_ip_cidrs) and event lacks it => does not match predicate (excluded if predicate is set).

3) Safe defaults
- Default export behavior when no filter is configured: export all audit events emitted by schema v2 (subject to audit event PII policy / redaction rules; filtering is not a substitute for redaction).
- For beta UI, we will recommend customers start with allow.categories only (least error-prone) and expand as needed.

Validation / guardrails
To prevent pathological configs and keep evaluation cheap:
- Hard limits:
  - max values per list: 100
  - max total values across allow+deny+predicates: 400
  - max CIDRs: 50
- Strings:
  - exact match only (no regex, no wildcards)
  - max length 128
- CIDR validation:
  - IPv4 and IPv6 supported; parsed at config load.
- Normalization:
  - de-duplicate lists
  - lower-case category/action tokens (category taxonomy is lowercase per schema v2)
- Config load failure behavior:
  - If filter fails validation, the exporter should mark destination as “config_error” and NOT export until fixed (avoid accidentally exporting too much).

Implementation notes
- Implemented as a small, allocation-friendly matcher compiled once per (tenant, destination) config refresh.
- Representation:
  - allow/deny token lists stored as hash sets for O(1) lookups.
  - CIDRs compiled to netaddr objects and checked only if event.source_ip exists and source_ip_cidrs is non-empty.
- Evaluation order (fast-fail):
  1) deny tokens (category/action/actor_type/resource_type)
  2) allow tokens
  3) predicate exact matches (workspace_id, actor_id, resource_id)
  4) CIDR predicate
- Observability:
  - exporter increments counters per destination for filtered_out_total with labels {reason=deny|allow_miss|predicate_miss} (label cardinality constrained; no category/action labels).
  - exporter logs per-batch summary at debug (not info) to avoid noisy logs.

Performance tests
Added microbench + load test coverage:
- Bench scenario: 10k events batch, 3 allow dimensions, 1 deny dimension, 10 CIDRs.
- Target: p95 evaluation overhead <= 5ms per 10k events on baseline exporter instance type (filtering should be dwarfed by encoding + network IO).
- Added regression test in CI to fail if overhead increases by >25% vs pinned baseline.

Acceptance criteria
- Export config supports allowlist/denylist by category/action/actor_type/resource_type.
- Export config supports optional predicates for actor_id/resource_id/workspace_id and source_ip CIDRs.
- Deny overrides allow.
- Empty allow dimension does not constrain.
- Exporter behavior on invalid filter is fail-closed (no export) with surfaced status.
- Unit tests cover edge cases (missing fields, unknown categories, empty lists, duplicates).
- Bench tests exist and run in CI (or nightly) with documented thresholds.

Rollout considerations
- Behind SIEM export beta feature flag; matcher compiled only for enabled tenants.
- Requires schema v2 categories/actions to be stable enough for customers to build allowlists; PM to provide canonical category list in docs.

Follow-ups (out of scope here)
- “Preview filter” endpoint in control plane / Console that shows sample counts by category over last N minutes (requires aggregation store).
- Support for “starts_with” on resource_id for hierarchical resource naming (needs careful validation).
- Tenant-level default denylist for known noisy system actions (needs Security review to avoid hiding security-relevant events).
text:
Goal
Implement the filtering model for SIEM-ready audit exports to support enterprise requirements (noise reduction, least-data export) while preserving delivery guarantees and avoiding performance regressions.

This ticket covers:
- Runtime evaluation of export filters (exporter-side) for each audit event.
- Validation/normalization rules shared with control plane to prevent unsafe/expensive filters.
- Safe defaults and clear semantics (allowlist + denylist, deny wins).
- Performance tests to ensure filtering overhead is negligible at expected event volumes.

References
- Confluence ADR: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-audit-export-filtering-model.json
- Schema v2 + category taxonomy: sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/audit-event-schema-v2-and-siem-mapping.json
- Control plane export config API ticket: ENG-5132
- Exporter scaffold/config sync ticket: ENG-5139

Non-goals (v1)
- Arbitrary regex matching or JSONPath over event payloads (too risky + too expensive + hard to validate).
- Full text search over request/response bodies (prohibited by PII policy anyway).
- Cross-event correlation filtering.

Proposed semantics (v1)
1) Filter structure
- allow:
  - categories: [string]
  - actions: [string]
  - actor_types: ["user"|"service_account"|"api_key"|"system"]
  - resource_types: [string]
- deny:
  - categories/actions/actor_types/resource_types (same shapes as allow)
- predicates (optional):
  - actor_ids: [string] (exact match only; intended for service accounts / API keys)
  - resource_ids: [string] (exact match only)
  - workspace_ids: [string] (exact match)
  - source_ip_cidrs: [cidr] (optional; only evaluated when event has source_ip)

2) Matching rules
- If allow.* is empty / unset: treat as “allow all” for that dimension.
  Example: allow.categories unset means categories do not constrain.
- If allow.* is set and non-empty: event must match at least one value in each provided allow dimension.
  Example: if allow.categories AND allow.actions are set, event must match both.
- Deny is always applied. If an event matches any deny clause, it is excluded even if it matches allow.
- Predicates behave as additional allow constraints when present (i.e., must match).
- Unknown categories/actions are treated as non-matching (conservative) when allow.* is explicitly set.
- Missing optional event fields:
  - If predicate requires field (e.g., source_ip_cidrs) and event lacks it => does not match predicate (excluded if predicate is set).

3) Safe defaults
- Default export behavior when no filter is configured: export all audit events emitted by schema v2 (subject to audit event PII policy / redaction rules; filtering is not a substitute for redaction).
- For beta UI, we will recommend customers start with allow.categories only (least error-prone) and expand as needed.

Validation / guardrails
To prevent pathological configs and keep evaluation cheap:
- Hard limits:
  - max values per list: 100
  - max total values across allow+deny+predicates: 400
  - max CIDRs: 50
- Strings:
  - exact match only (no regex, no wildcards)
  - max length 128
- CIDR validation:
  - IPv4 and IPv6 supported; parsed at config load.
- Normalization:
  - de-duplicate lists
  - lower-case category/action tokens (category taxonomy is lowercase per schema v2)
- Config load failure behavior:
  - If filter fails validation, the exporter should mark destination as “config_error” and NOT export until fixed (avoid accidentally exporting too much).

Implementation notes
- Implemented as a small, allocation-friendly matcher compiled once per (tenant, destination) config refresh.
- Representation:
  - allow/deny token lists stored as hash sets for O(1) lookups.
  - CIDRs compiled to netaddr objects and checked only if event.source_ip exists and source_ip_cidrs is non-empty.
- Evaluation order (fast-fail):
  1) deny tokens (category/action/actor_type/resource_type)
  2) allow tokens
  3) predicate exact matches (workspace_id, actor_id, resource_id)
  4) CIDR predicate
- Observability:
  - exporter increments counters per destination for filtered_out_total with labels {reason=deny|allow_miss|predicate_miss} (label cardinality constrained; no category/action labels).
  - exporter logs per-batch summary at debug (not info) to avoid noisy logs.

Performance tests
Added microbench + load test coverage:
- Bench scenario: 10k events batch, 3 allow dimensions, 1 deny dimension, 10 CIDRs.
- Target: p95 evaluation overhead <= 5ms per 10k events on baseline exporter instance type (filtering should be dwarfed by encoding + network IO).
- Added regression test in CI to fail if overhead increases by >25% vs pinned baseline.

Acceptance criteria
- Export config supports allowlist/denylist by category/action/actor_type/resource_type.
- Export config supports optional predicates for actor_id/resource_id/workspace_id and source_ip CIDRs.
- Deny overrides allow.
- Empty allow dimension does not constrain.
- Exporter behavior on invalid filter is fail-closed (no export) with surfaced status.
- Unit tests cover edge cases (missing fields, unknown categories, empty lists, duplicates).
- Bench tests exist and run in CI (or nightly) with documented thresholds.

Rollout considerations
- Behind SIEM export beta feature flag; matcher compiled only for enabled tenants.
- Requires schema v2 categories/actions to be stable enough for customers to build allowlists; PM to provide canonical category list in docs.

Follow-ups (out of scope here)
- “Preview filter” endpoint in control plane / Console that shows sample counts by category over last N minutes (requires aggregation store).
- Support for “starts_with” on resource_id for hierarchical resource naming (needs careful validation).
- Tenant-level default denylist for known noisy system actions (needs Security review to avoid hiding security-relevant events).

source.title: Orchestrator peering route propagation & session-thrash follow-up
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: During a routine orchestrator upgrade on private deployments using cross-account VPC peering, a subset of control plane nodes lost connectivity due to delayed route propagation combined with stale session tokens on agents. This caused repeated leader re-elections, API timeouts for customer control plane actions, and transient capacity loss for several customers in EU-West-1. The incident surface was primarily control plane API errors and agent reconnection storms rather than model-serving compute failures.
source.postmortem_summary: Root cause: race between automated peering route table updates and the orchestrator upgrade sequence that relied on immediate propagation. Secondary cause: agents retained session handles which were invalidated during control plane partitioning, leading to mass reconnect and token-refresh storms that amplified the outage. We restored stability by rolling back the orchestrator change, re-establishing peering route tables, and throttling agent reconnection backoff centrally. The follow-up focuses on pre-upgrade route validation, session orchestration fixes, and runbook automation to avoid manual rollback latency.
timeline: 2025-03-20T01:12Z - Upgrade job triggered for orchestrator control plane nodes in eu-west-1 (scheduled cluster rolling restart).
2025-03-20T01:20Z - Peering route table update applied by automation; initial BGP/propagation started but not yet converged for all VPCs.
2025-03-20T01:25Z - Several control plane nodes lost connectivity to agent pools; API latencies spike and error rates increase (observed in controlplane/errors dashboard).
2025-03-20T01:27Z - Agents begin mass reconnect attempts; token refresh traffic increases 30x, causing auth backend throttle.
2025-03-20T01:35Z - Leads initiate rollback of orchestrator change and disable automated peering update job.
2025-03-20T01:42Z - Network team confirms route tables converged and peering accepted across accounts; control plane nodes gradually rejoin.
2025-03-20T02:05Z - Throttles and reconnection backoff policies applied to agents to dampen token refresh storms.
2025-03-20T02:30Z - API error rates back to baseline; post-incident monitoring window opened for 72 hours.
actions: Add pre-upgrade route propagation check to upgrade pipeline: validate routes across all peered accounts and confirm BGP/propagation convergence before making control plane changes (owner: Diego Morales, due: 2025-03-31).

Implement agent session fencing: when a control plane node transitions state during upgrades, mark sessions stale and require structured reconnect flow with randomized exponential backoff to avoid token-thundering (owner: Priya Singh, due: 2025-04-07).
Create a canary-level orchestrator upgrade that runs peering update and a single-node restart in a canary VPC to measure propagation latency under load (owner: SRE team, due: 2025-04-10).
Add automated preflight that runs for peering changes: run traceroutes and route-table diffs and fail upgrade if propagation > configured threshold (owner: Network Automation, due: 2025-03-28).

Update runbooks: explicit checklist for private upgrades to include peering verification, agent reconnection dampening toggle, and immediate rollback steps (owner: On-call Runbook Maintainer, due: 2025-03-26).
Dashboarding: add 'peering-propagation-lag' metric and synthetic agent-connect check per region with alerting to the SRE rotation (owner: Observability, due: 2025-03-30).
Post-deploy audit job: a short window (15m) post-upgrade that flags unexpected agent reconnect rates and pauses further rollouts if threshold exceeded (owner: Release Eng, due: 2025-04-02).
decisions: Short-term mitigation prioritized: throttle agent token refresh centrally instead of increasing heartbeat timeouts globally (rationale: avoids weakening latency SLOs for healthy clusters).
We will not increase default heartbeat timeouts for private deployments at this time; instead, implement session fencing and backoff to address root cause.
Introduce a staged peering-change rollout (canary -> shard -> global) for all future automation touching route tables.
notes: Trade-off considered: increasing BGP timers would reduce false positives but increase failover detection time. We decided to leave timers unchanged and solve via preflight and canary rollouts to balance availability and correctness.

During the incident we observed that affected customers had high agent density and short-lived ephemeral sessions – these workloads amplified the reconnection storm. This influenced action items to improve agent backoff.

source.title: Embedding quality simulator and telemetry adaptor for offline & drift detection
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Build an extensible evaluation harness that (1) synthesizes controlled perturbations to create robust golden test cases, (2) runs nightly offline quality metrics across models/versions, and (3) publishes drift and regression signals into Redwood Console telemetry and alerting. This ticket contains the simulator + adaptor components and a lightweight orchestration job for nightly runs. Goal is to catch embedding quality regressions (semantic flips, topical drift, model regressions) earlier and provide actionable signal (metric + example anchors) for model onboarding and runtime rollouts.
acceptance_criteria: A reproducible simulator that can generate perturbation variants for a given golden set (typos, paraphrase, entity masking, domain shift) and export labeled pairs for offline scoring.

Nightly job that computes a baseline and fresh metrics for each model version and writes results to S3 and the Console metrics API (MAP@k, MRR@k, Recall@k, cosine-shift distribution, embedding-norm change, PSI).
Drift/regression detection rules implemented: statistical significance (bootstrapped CI) and effect-size thresholds; alerts routed to #embeddings-quality and PagerDuty for high-impact regressions.
Per-model baseline storage and a small UI in Console showing time series, example failures (top 20), and comparison against previous stable version.
Integration tests and a documented runbook; PRs reviewed and merged; at least one production model (text-embed-v1-small) onboarded to the harness.
Performance constraints: nightly incremental run <= 3h for common models; full baseline run completes in <12h with current infra quota.
implementation_notes: Simulator design: modular transforms pipeline (CharNoise, TokenShuffle, Paraphrase via small LLM, DomainSwap using doc-level sampling). Each transform records a ground-truth label for expected similarity change.

Scoring: compute MAP@10, MRR@10, Recall@1/5/10, average cosine similarity on positives vs negatives, embedding L2/norm statistics, and pairwise median distance per class. Store raw vectors sample for failed examples.

Stat tests: bootstrap difference-in-means for MAP and Recall; compute PSI for distribution shifts; use KS test on similarity distributions as secondary signal. Apply Benjamini-Hochberg FDR control across per-model checks.

Pipeline: Airflow job (orchestration) triggers nightly job that runs per-model incremental evaluation against stored golden set. Outputs written to s3://redwood-embedding-evals/{model}/{date}/ and POSTed to Console metrics endpoint.
Adaptor: small service that translates evaluation outputs into telemetry schema accepted by Console (time-series metrics + top-N failure examples with hashed context to avoid PII leaks).
Privacy: golden sets sanitized; example artifacts hashed and only short context snippets kept. Security review flag set but no PII should be present.
risks: Golden set bias: overfitting to curated domains may miss customer-specific failure modes. Plan to add customer-supplied sample ingestion later.
Compute cost: full baselines are expensive; need incremental strategy and sampling. Risk of runaway costs if we onboard many models without quotas.
False positives from sensitive statistical thresholds causing alert fatigue. We'll tune thresholds and add label-based suppression windows.
comments: 2025-02-04 - Ava Chen: Initial spec drafted after design sync with PM and SRE. Focus on modular simulator to allow new transform plugins.
2025-02-12 - Marco Alvarez: Proof-of-concept simulator implemented (CharNoise, Paraphrase via small LLM). Local scoring pipeline returns MAP@10 and cosine stats for sample goldset.
2025-02-20 - Marco Alvarez: Prototype PR opened: https://github.com/redwood-inference/embedding-evals/pull/472. Requesting review from @sre-team and @pm-embeddings.
2025-02-25 - Priya Patel (PM): Reviewed PR, asked for: (1) clearer failure example export (hashed), (2) PSI added as a metric, (3) nightly cadence and cost estimate. Updated AC accordingly.
2025-03-03 - Marco Alvarez: Integrated PSI, added bootstrapped CI for MAP. Nightly job implemented in Airflow but currently running on a test queue.
2025-03-10 - Marco Alvarez: Blocked by GPU quota for bulk vectorization job (request ENG-33611 filed). If quota not increased, fallback to CPU vectorization with longer run-time (~2x).
2025-03-12 - Ava Chen: Merged adaptor to staging; Console dashboard prototype available. Next: onboard text-embed-v1-small and run first full baseline.

source.title: Opaque bundle signer and staged traffic shim for profile rollout
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Goal: Build a hardened CI/CD flow that produces an opaque, signed model bundle, publishes a corresponding performance profile, and wires a staged traffic shim so new model versions receive controlled traffic during rollouts. This ticket covers the signer service integration, bundle metadata handshake with the model registry, automated profile broadcast to the profile-store, and a small-traffic routing shim plus automatic rollback hooks when eval or telemetry thresholds fail.
acceptance_criteria: CI pipeline produces a single opaque artifact bundle (.rmb) that contains model weights, deterministic build metadata, and a signed manifest (cosign-compatible).
Signer service rotates keys monthly and exposes a verification endpoint consumed by the runtime and registry. Rotation must maintain ability to verify artifacts signed with previous keys for 30 days.
On successful publish, the pipeline writes a performance profile JSON to the profile-store and emits a publish event that the console ingests for rollout controls.

Traffic shim routes 1% -> 5% of production requests to the new model variant for a configurable staging window, with automatic rollback if latency or error-rate exceed configured thresholds for 5 consecutive minutes.
End-to-end test (integration stage) exercises bundle signing, verification by a verification agent, profile publish, staged routing, and rollback when a synthetic regression is injected.
notes: Rationale: Existing onboarding pipelines produce separate artifacts and signatures. Operators must manually coordinate profile publish and rollout. This flow reduces human coordination and shortens median time-to-serving for vetted models.

Security note: We will adopt our cosign-compatible KMS-backed signer (keymaster) and ensure all signing keys are HSM-backed in prod. Key rotation semantics must be reversible for verification grace periods.
Compatibility: Runtime verification will accept bundles signed by any key in the registry's trust root list. We will publish a migration doc for Dedicated/Private customers to update trust roots.
updates: 2025-03-03: Spike completed — PoC signer integrated into pipeline; PoC produces .rmb with manifest and cosign signature. Verified verification agent can validate the PoC bundle. (Ava)
2025-03-05: Security review kickoff scheduled; security team requested additional audit logs around signing operations and non-repudiation metadata. Added to acceptance criteria. (Diego)
2025-03-08: Implemented staging shim prototype that toggles routing percentage via feature flag service. Needs integration with rollout policy engine for automated rollback. (Diego)

2025-03-10: Integration tests failing intermittently due to non-deterministic build timestamps embedded in bundles — added deterministic-build step to strip volatile metadata; re-running pipeline. (Ava)
2025-03-12: Deterministic build step stabilized. Draft PRs created for runtime verification and pipeline templates. Planning a canary publish to internal dev cluster tomorrow. (Diego)
follow_up_tasks: Add audit-export hook to signer service for SOC2 evidence (ENG-67102)
Expose staged-rollout window configuration in Console (PM-881)
Write migration guide for Dedicated/Private customers to import trust roots (DOC-445)
Postmortem template for automated rollback events to capture false-positives in metrics (OPS-99)
implementation_steps: 1) Finalize signer API and KMS integration; ensure cosign-compatible artifacts.
2) Extend CI templates to produce .rmb bundles with normalized metadata and manifest signing.
3) Implement registry handshake: publish manifest + signed proof and mark artifact as "ready-for-profile".
4) Profile publisher: generate and atomically write performance_profile.json to profile-store and emit publish event.
5) Staged traffic shim: add small percentage routing capability to router with rollback hooks tied to telemetry thresholds.
6) Integration tests: synthetic regression injection to validate rollback and observability alerts.
7) Docs + Console UI changes for rollout controls and trust root management.
8) Security review and SOC2 evidence collection.

source.title: Per-device performance envelopes and model-pin release workflow
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Goal: Define per-device 'performance envelopes' (latency P50/P95, token/sec, memory footprints) for A100/H100/L40S/MI300 variants and codify a model version pinning + publish workflow that ensures safe rollout of new performance profiles to Hosted/Dedicated/Private deployments. This ticket produces a reproducible onboarding checklist, a canonical JSON schema for published profiles, and a gated release process that ties perf profile publication to canary rollout steps and telemetry gating. Scope excludes model compression experiments (separate RFC).
success_metrics: Published performance profile schema validated by >=3 benchmark runs per device variant
Automated profile publish pipeline reduces manual steps to <=3 commands
No regression incidents in canary clusters for 2 weeks post-rollout
constraints: Benchmarks must use standard tracing harness (bench/serving/latency-v2)
Profiles should account for 2 sequence-length buckets: 512 and 2048 tokens
Private deployments: profile publish must not auto-upgrade customers without explicit opt-in
deliverables: CI jobs that produce per-device profile artifacts
merge-ready PR to docs/perf-profiles and infra/publish-profile pipeline
acceptance_criteria: Schema accepted by APISpec and Console teams
CI generates profiles for all four device families in < 6 hours
Documented rollback plan and automated tag-based rollback action
PM sign-off on rollout gating thresholds
source.performance_matrix_summary: Summary results from baseline runs (median of 3 runs per config): A100-40GB (fp16): P50=18ms/step at batch=1 for seq512, throughput 210 tok/s; H100-80GB (fp16+optim): P50=12ms, throughput 340 tok/s; L40S-48GB (bf16): P50=14ms, throughput 300 tok/s; MI300-128GB (bfloat16/miopt): P50=22ms, throughput 260 tok/s. Memory working set estimates: A100 ~22GB, H100 ~28GB, L40S ~20GB, MI300 ~30GB for a 13B variant. These numbers are illustrative; CI artifacts contain full matrix per model family and quantization profile.
publish_workflow: 1) Run full bench harness for model+weights on all target SKUs using bench/serving/latency-v2
3) Trigger automated validation job that runs 3 cold-start and 3 warm-start tests and compares against baseline thresholds (P95, throughput, mem)
4) If validation passes, create publish PR that includes profile metadata, changelog entry, and release notes; require 2 approvers (Infra + PM)
5) Merge PR triggers publish pipeline to s3://redwood-perf-profiles/prod/<model>/<version>/<device> and create Console entry; also auto-tag model in model-catalog with 'pinned-<runtime-release>'

6) Canary rollout: deploy new profile to 5% of Hosted traffic + one Dedicated cluster and one Private test tenant (opt-in). Monitor for 72 hours. If regression detected, run automated rollback action.
model_version_pinning_guidance: Default: newly onboarded models are published as 'floating' profiles for experimentation for 48 hours; after validation they should be explicitly pinned using the 'pin_model_version' API with a release tag

Pinning ties a model version to a performance profile artifact and a runtime release (e.g., runtime-1.21); Dedicated customers can opt to pin to an older runtime but require manual acceptance of performance deltas
When pinning across hardware families, include 'recommended_device' and 'fallback_device' fields to enable smart routing
private_deployments_notes: Private customers are never auto-upgraded. Publish pipeline emits a signed artifact and a customer-facing release note they can choose to import into their control plane
Provide migration guide for Private: 'apply-profile --tenant <id> --profile <s3-uri>' with pre-checks for memory and kernel compatibility
open_risks: Device thermal throttling variance can cause wider P95 swings than CI captures
Kernel-level optimizations on new driver versions may change latency characteristics post-publish
Some MI300 quantization paths currently lack parity tests against H100 fp16 baselines
mitigations: Expand CI to include longer-running soak tests under synthetic burst traffic
Add driver-version metadata to published profiles and require 'driver_compat' field in schema
Create parity job to compare MI300 quantized outputs vs reference outputs within 1% perplexity change
comments: 2025-03-05 Ava Chen: Initial ticket created after sync with Infra and PM. Draft schema attached to PR #1124.
2025-03-07 Diego Martinez: Added MI300 notes and parity requirement after discussion with hardware team. Allocated estimate 8 pts.
2025-03-09 Priya Nair (PM): Suggested adding Private tenant opt-in language and documented rollback steps.
2025-03-11 Infra review (Lena Ortiz): Requested driver_compat and kernel-version fields in schema; flagged need for extra validation in publish pipeline.
2025-03-12 Diego Martinez: Updated performance numbers with latest CI median runs and moved ticket to In Review. PR #1148 pending 2 approvals.

Scope excludes model compression experiments (separate RFC).

This ticket produces a reproducible onboarding checklist, a canonical JSON schema for published profiles, and a gated release process that ties perf profile publication to canary rollout steps and telemetry gating. Scope excludes model compression experiments (separate RFC).

source.title: Quant artifact anchoring and rollback ledger for reproducible calibration
source.creator: Priya Shah
source.description:
Problem: We currently produce FP8/INT8 quant artifacts and calibration snapshots without a single authoritative, signed record that binds the artifact bytes to the calibration inputs, quantization config, toolchain, and model binary. That makes retrospective verification, reproducible rollbacks, and automated safety checks brittle.

Goal: Implement an artifact anchoring ledger and CLI verification flow that: (1) computes deterministic content fingerprints for model binaries, calibration datasets, quant profiles and build metadata; (2) publishes a signed, append-only ledger record per quant release containing the fingerprints, human-readable manifest, and storage pointers; (3) provides a verification tool to replay/validate calibration results and detect bit-rot or untrusted modifications; (4) surface an automated rollback playbook when verification or accuracy validation fails.

Success criteria include deterministic hashing of outputs, signed manifests using KMS-backed keys, nightly automated verification against goldensets, and an operator playbook for rollback that is safe for Dedicated/Private tenants.
updates: 2025-03-02 — Triage complete. Scoped to: manifest schema, signing strategy, verification CLI, nightly verifier job, rollback runbook. Assigned to Ethan. Estimate 8 pts.

2025-03-04 — Design decision: use SHA-256 for content hashing and ed25519 signatures via KMS (AWS KMS with asymmetric ed25519 key) for ledger entries. Backend ledger format: newline-delimited JSON records (ndjson) with canonical field ordering and explicit version field to future-proof. Security okayed use of KMS after initial review — Security asked for explicit key rotation doc (see links).

2025-03-06 — Prototype PR created for ledger writer and verifier: https://github.com/redwood-inference/quant-anchor/pull/1234. CLI name: quant-anchor. Verifier can fetch manifest, validate signatures, compare hashes of model blob and calibration snapshot, and run golden inference check with configurable thresholds.

2025-03-08 — Nightly verifier job (ci/quant-anchor-nightly) run configured. First run discovered hash mismatch for artifact redwood-7B-v2-int8 built on 2025-02-14 (manifest root: 0x4a1f...). Action: flagged release, opened ENG-19907 to schedule rebuild, and alerted release manager. See dashboard: https://console.redwood.ai/dashboards/quant-anchor/verification-2025-03-08.

2025-03-09 — PM feedback: add field 'calibration_seed' and 'goldenset_id' to manifest. Design requested console UI copy for tamper-warning modal. Security requested KMS key policy and emergency key rotation playbook before GA. Added these to acceptance criteria.

2025-03-10 — Implementation notes: ledger writer implemented in GO, verifier in python to reuse existing eval harness. Integration tests added to CI. Next: implement atomic append to ledger (S3 appendix with manifest index + Dynamo index) and finalize rollback playbook with on-call runbook steps.
2025-03-10 — Blockers: need final approval for KMS asymmetric keys (sec), and a quick audit of storage lifecycle costs for keeping older artifacts (infra).
artifact_examples: Ledger fragment (append-only ndjson file): ledger-2025-02.ndjson (each line is a signed manifest with canonical ordering).
CLI verify example: quant-anchor verify --manifest s3://redwood-artifacts/ledger/ledger-2025-02.ndjson --artifact redwood-7B-v2-int8 --threshold perplexity: +0.5%
acceptance_criteria: Manifests are produced by the quant CI step for every quant build and uploaded to the ledger (S3 key) with a signature from KMS.
Verifier CLI can validate signature and recompute SHA-256 of model, quant profile, and calibration snapshot deterministically.
Nightly verifier runs for all active quant artifacts and posts a pass/fail result to the dashboard with diff details where applicable.

Rollback playbook evaluated: when accuracy regressions exceed thresholds or hashes mismatch, the playbook automatically flags releases and creates a rollback job for Dedicated/Private with manual approval step.
Security has approved KMS key usage and the emergency key rotation docs are published in SEC ticket.
qa_plan: Unit tests for canonicalized ndjson serialization to ensure deterministic ordering.
Fuzz tests for verifier to assert meaningful errors on corrupted manifests.
Integration test in CI: build quant artifact -> produce manifest -> append ledger -> run verifier -> assert pass.
Nightly run will validate ~30 active artifacts against goldensets; failures must produce actionable diffs (hash mismatch vs. quality regression).
Manual runbook dry-run with release engineering to exercise rollback steps in non-prod Dedicated environment.
notes: Trade-offs: using ndjson + KMS signatures keeps format human readable and easy to append but requires careful append/atomicity handling for S3; alternative was a compact Merkle tree store (more compute to maintain).
Storage cost: we will retain full binary calibration snapshots for 90 days and metadata indefinitely; infra asked for cost estimate — follow-up with infra team (ENG-19912).
Future work: consider Merkle-indexed catalog to support partial proofs for auditors and cross-tenant validation without exposing raw calibration data.

source.title: Tenant-aware fairness simulator for regional scarcity and graceful degradation
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Goal: validate that admission control, circuit breakers, and load-shedding policies enforce predictable SLO degradation across tenants when a region experiences progressive GPU capacity loss or dependency silence. We need a reproducible simulator and harness that can: (1) emulate partial region failures (gradual GPU eviction, abrupt node loss, increasing enqueue delays), (2) emulate downstream dependency silence/muting (redis/cache/kv proxies returning timeouts or dropped responses without raising downstream alerts), and (3) measure per-tenant SLOs and fairness metrics under different admission/policy configurations. The output will inform runtime default policies and provide test cases for canary gating of autoscaling and admission improvements.
scenarios: Scenario A - Gradual GPU attrition: remove GPUs from region A in 5% increments over 30 minutes while keeping control plane healthy; observe token-level p95 increase and queue growth.
Scenario B - Sudden pool loss: simulate preemption of 30% of active GPU workers in region B and verify circuit breakers trigger graceful fallbacks to cross-region routing.

Scenario C - Tenant contestation under scarcity: create 50 synthetic tenants with skewed QPS (top 10 tenants produce 80% traffic) and test weighted fairness admission vs strict per-tenant rate-limits.
Scenario D - Dependency muting: silence cache/retrieval layer for 10 minutes to force fallbacks to origin and measure end-to-end error amplification and retry storms.
Scenario E - Network egress constraint: emulate degraded egress bandwidth to an external embedding service to validate backpressure behavior and shed noncritical workloads.
Scenario F - Combined stress: run Scenarios A + D simultaneously to validate cascading protection (circuit breakers, token prioritization, regional evacuation).
acceptance_criteria: A reproducible automation script exists that can run each scenario deterministically in staging for at least 3 runs and produce a combined report.
Per-tenant SLO dashboard added to Console staging with p50/p90/p95 latency, error-rate, served-requests, and fairness score (Jain's index) for simulated tenants.

Default admission policy candidate (weight-based fairness + emergency tenant-protect thresholds) validated: top-10 tenants retained >=70% of baseline throughput while lower-priority tenants degraded predictably.

Circuit-breaker thresholds documented and validated: when region p95 latency exceeds X ms or GPU available capacity < Y%, traffic is routed or shed according to policy without triggering cascading retries.
Runbook updated with steps to reproduce and emergency rollback toggles for the experiment (circuit breaker false-positive rollback, cross-region routing disable).
implementation_plan: 1) Build synthetic tenant generator that can register tenants, issue requests with realistic distribution, and annotate requests for per-tenant metrics.
2) Extend fault-injector to support: gradual GPU eviction API, worker preemption event generator, network egress throttler, and dependency-mute for upstream caches.
3) Add telemetry hooks: per-tenant tracing tags, admission decision logs, circuit-breaker state transitions, and token-level cost breakdown.
4) Create a staging dashboard and automated report generator to export CSV/JSON for each run.
5) Run baseline, then iterate on admission weighting algorithms (strict cap, proportional fairness, minimum-guarantee) and collect metrics.
6) Produce a short PRD with recommended default policy and a proposal for Console UI controls for tenant-protect / emergency-shedding toggles.
metrics_to_monitor: Per-tenant success rate and HTTP 5xx counts
GPU utilization and active worker count per region
Queue depth and enqueue latency distribution
Fairness score (Jain's index) computed across tenants
Downstream dependency timeout counts and retry amplification
rollout_plan: Phase 0: unit & integration tests for harness components - local dev machines (2 days)
Phase 1: small-scale staging runs with 10 tenants to validate instrumentation (1 day)
Phase 2: full-stage runs with 50-200 synthetic tenants; collect three successful repeats (2-3 days)
Phase 3: tighten policies and dry-run canary: enable new admission policy for 1% of production traffic in a single non-critical region (requires PM approval)
Phase 4: post-mortem + incorporate findings into default runtime config and Console controls
source.notes: Trade-offs: strict tenant caps protect small customers only if enforcement is precise—too-aggressive shedding causes revenue impact for large customers. We should bias initial experiments toward preserving top-paying tenants while tracking fairness metrics. Observability cost: per-tenant telemetry at token level increases cardinality; will avoid sampling during long runs and use aggregated windows.
comments: 2025-03-02 - Ava Chen: Initial spike: implemented synthetic tenant generator prototype. Local runs show queue growth pattern under 20% GPU reduction.
2025-03-04 - Diego Morales: Added admission decision logs to tracing. Found noisy circuit-breaker flapping when gradual eviction steps at 5% per 2 minutes; tuning required.
2025-03-06 - Priya Kapoor (SRE): Reviewed harness and recommended limiting throttler to egress instead of pod-level IPTables on first runs to reduce blast radius.
2025-03-08 - Design review (Lina Park): Requested dashboard mockups to show per-tenant impact and an exportable CSV for PMs. Assigned to UX task DES-133.

2025-03-10 - Diego Morales: Staging run #2 completed. Fairness baseline (proportional) preserved 72% throughput for top-10 tenants; detailed report uploaded to Confluence link in artifacts. Next: test strict-minimum-guarantee policy.

source.title: Admin Workshop: Lightweight utilities + runbook-as-task simulator
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Problem: on-call and support engineers spend significant time running ad-hoc admin scripts and manually executing multi-step runbooks; current runbooks are hard to test and often have hidden side effects. Goal: deliver a lightweight admin utility library and a local/runtime 'runbook-as-task' simulator that lets engineers author runbook steps as composable tasks, execute dry-runs with synthetic data, and validate safety gates and telemetry before touching production. Scope: 1) small language-agnostic utilities (idempotent helpers, safe wrappers, canned retries) for reuse across adminlets; 2) a simulator binary (iterative prototype in Python) that loads a runbook definition, simulates each step with mock connectors, surfaces side-effect summary, and calculates estimated blast radius; 3) tight observability: emitted structured telemetry events for every simulated step; 4) safety policy hooks (RBAC checks, approval gates, canary rollouts) that can be toggled in sim mode. Out of scope: full UI console integration and production rollout of the simulator in this ticket (follow-ups will wire to console and runbook catalog).
acceptance_criteria: A small utility package (python and go wrappers) with at least 6 idempotent helpers is published to internal artifact registry
Simulator can load runbook-as-task YAML, execute a dry-run with mock connectors, and produce a step-by-step side-effect report
Simulator emits structured telemetry to the ops-workbench staging telemetry endpoint and we can query simulated-run events in Dashboard (sample dashboard link in comments)
Safety hooks enforce RBAC checks in sim mode and surface precise approval instructions when a step requires escalation
End-to-end smoke test exists that runs a 4-step runbook in sim mode and asserts no real infra calls were made
tasks: Draft implementation design and safety checklist (confluence) - DONE
Prototype simulator CLI (python) that reads YAML playbooks - IN PROGRESS
Implement idempotent helper library and ship initial artifact - TODO
Add structured telemetry schema and wire to staging collector - TODO
Create smoke test using synthetic env and validate no infra side effects - TODO
Write runbook examples and migration guide for existing admin scripts - TODO
comments: 2025-03-01 - Ava Chen: Created ticket after cross-team sync (SRE, Support, Security). Attached initial confluence draft: https://confluence.internal.redwood.ai/display/OPS/Admin+Workshop+Design

2025-03-03 - Mateo Rivera: Prototype CLI bootstrapped. Using Python click for argument parsing. Initial behavior: load YAML, instantiate mock connectors, step through without emitting real requests. PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/ops-sim/pull/142

2025-03-04 - Priya Patel (SRE): Reviewed prototype; asked for clearer idempotency semantics and a deterministic random seed for simulations. Suggested adding 'side_effect_score' heuristic to estimate risk per step.

2025-03-05 - Mateo Rivera: Pushed telemetry events schema (events include run_id, step_id, mock_result, estimated_cost, side_effect_score). Dashboard stub: https://dashboards.internal.redwood.ai/d/ops-sim/simulated-runs
2025-03-06 - Elena Morales (Security): Requested RBAC contract + security_review_required flag. Will provide a minimal JSON schema for approval flows by 2025-03-10.

2025-03-07 - Mateo Rivera: Experimented with a small Go wrapper for the helper library (to support internal agent binaries). Early perf numbers: simulator dry-run of 50-step runbook completes in ~1.8s on dev laptop (no network calls). Trade-off: Python prototype faster to iterate but Go wrapper required for low-latency embedding in agent binaries. Decided to keep Python prototype and ship Go helper small shim later.
decision_log: 2025-03-02 - Chose Python for fast prototyping of the simulator; will provide a thin Go shim for critical helpers to support agent embedding
2025-03-05 - Telemetry will be structured JSON LAC (log, audit, correlation) with run_id and deterministic timestamps to enable replay
2025-03-06 - Safety model: require explicit 'approved' flag for any step tagged 'destructive' and block dry-run-to-live promotion without a manual approval step recorded
2025-03-07 - Opted for a 'no-side-effects-by-default' policy in sim mode: connectors must opt-in to simulate state changes; default mocks return 'no-op'
current_blockers: Waiting on Security: RBAC JSON schema and approval flow contract (see comment from Elena Morales)
Need dev infra ephemeral env configured to ensure smoke tests cannot reach production endpoints (infra ticket pending)

source.title: Construct latency-utility frontier for stochastic batching and eviction-aware cache tuning
source.creator: Asha Patel
source.description: Objective: build an evaluation harness and dashboard to map the latency-utility (quality) frontier across stochastic batching policies and eviction-aware key-value (KV) cache TTL/eviction strategies. We want a compact signal that captures model-answer utility (win-rate and instruction-following) vs end-to-end latency for representative production workloads so we can automatically detect and rank acceptable config variants for rollouts and automated fallback rules.
acceptance_criteria: End-to-end harness can run automated sweeps (grid and random) over batch_size, microbatch_size, batch_timeout_ms, kv_cache_ttl_ms, eviction_fraction, and routing_sample_ratio
Produce a latency-utility frontier visualization that highlights Pareto-optimal configs and exports CSV of metrics (p50/p90/p99 latency, tokens/sec, normalized utility score, hallucination_rate)
Integrate frontier signals into regression-monitoring pipeline and emit alerts when a newly deployed config/model lies >5% utility below baseline at same latency bucket
Provide a canary test profile and suggested rollback/fallback config map for console rollout UI
plan_steps: Define utility metric: weighted composite of human-rated instruction-following (win-rate) and automated coverage signals (BLEURT proxy + NLI-based hallucination detector)
Assemble representative eval slices from production telemetry: short-chats, long-form generation, few-shot instructions, high-token prompts
Implement harness changes: stochastic batching sampler (controls inter-request sampling, microbatch sizing) and eviction-aware KV cache emulator (simulate prefix eviction and TTL semantics)
Run a pilot sweep (1000 jobs) across a 20-config Latin hypercube and compare to baseline config; log to perf-data S3 and metrics to grafana/observability
Build frontier visualizer and CSV export; add dashboard panels and automated alerts in regression monitor
risks_and_mitigation: Sampling bias: Latin hypercube might miss edge microburst patterns — mitigation: add slice-driven stress jobs using traffic traces
Metric noise: automated proxies can be noisy for instruction-following — mitigation: reserve a small human-eval holdout for high-sensitivity configs
Cost: large sweep runs are expensive on dedicated GPUs — mitigation: run early-stage emulation on smaller models and only validate top candidates on full stack
Rollout hazards: a config that improves latency but subtly lowers quality on rare prompts — mitigation: gate with slice-level alarms and progressive canary ramp
comments: 2025-02-10 Asha Patel: Created ticket after incident learnings where aggressive TTL changes caused a small but consistent drop in instruction-following on long-form prompts. Goal is to make these regressions obvious in a Pareto view.
2025-02-14 Mateo Alvarez: Initial prototype of stochastic batching sampler committed (GH PR linked). Ran local smoke tests; p50 improvements visible but p99 variance increased for long-form slice.
2025-02-18 Priya Natarajan (PM): Reviewed metric definition. Suggested adding a human-eval holdout for hallucination-sensitive slices and weighting utility more heavily for enterprise SLA customers.

2025-02-22 Mateo Alvarez: Pilot sweep finished (links above). Top candidate reduces p50 by 18% with <2% utility drop on median, but shows 6% utility drop on 'long-form' slice — flagged for human review.

2025-02-25 SRE notes (Liam Chen): Observed that microburst traffic causes batching sampler to produce unusually large microbatches when routing_sample_ratio > 0.6; suggested adding microburst cap and monitoring queue depth metrics.
2025-03-01 Design feedback (Riley Morgan): Frontier visualizer needs percentile bands and per-slice toggle; keeping as follow-up but implement basic bands for now.

2025-03-06 Mateo Alvarez: Merged harness PR after addressing test coverage. Next: wire frontier into regression-monitoring and add canary config mapping in console rollout page. Blocking on console API change (ENG-100451).

source.title: Pinned version I/O covenant and regression protocol
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Define and publish a machine-readable covenant for pinned model versions that guarantees I/O shape, parameter aliases, and a published performance signature. The covenant will be enforced by onboarding checks and the regression pipeline. This ticket scopes the contract format, tolerated deltas, validation harness, and the publishing flow to the model catalog. Goal: give product teams a clear SLA-like promise for pinned models that balances strictness (prevent regressions) with pragmatic adapters (parameter aliasing, safe canonicalizers).
acceptance_criteria: A canonical covenant JSON schema is finalized and checked into docs/confluence and code repo
Onboarding CLI validates a model package against the covenant and emits a human-readable report and machine status code
Regression thresholds (semantic delta, output shape diffs, P95 latency, tokens/sec) are defined and plumbed into the CI eval job
Model catalog UI shows the published performance signature for pinned versions (latency P50/P95, throughput, token cost estimate)
If a model build violates the covenant, the onboarding job fails and creates a GitHub issue template with diagnostics
implementation_notes: Covenant schema: top-level fields: io_schema, param_aliases, tolerated_delta, perf_signature, fingerprint_hooks, manifest_version
Io schema must support optional canonicalizers: whitespace normalization and tokenization adapter; adapters are explicit and versioned
Tolerated_delta will include numeric thresholds for numeric outputs, edit-distance bounds for text, and a flag for unsupported non-deterministic operators
Perf signature: publish median and P95 latency for three representative request shapes (short/medium/long) plus throughput tokens/sec under standard batch sizes
Add a small Python harness (redwood-onboard) that loads the model artifact, runs the golden prompt set, and emits a signed fingerprint file
Compatibility adapters: implement parameter alias mapping at routing-time to preserve old SDK parameter names for pinned versions
milestones: Integrate regression thresholds into CI eval (2025-03-10)
comments: 2025-02-16 - Ava Chen: Kickoff meeting notes: emphasize that I/O shape must be both machine-verifiable and readable by product managers.
2025-02-21 - Liam Patel: Schema draft posted; raised question about how to express stochastic outputs (we propose 'semantic_delta' with qualitative flags).
2025-02-26 - Priya Nair (PM): Approved core schema structure; requested example covenants for a chat model and an embedding-only model.
2025-03-01 - Engineering review: Add explicit handling for parameter deprecation with 'redirect' entries so SDKs can auto-map deprecated names.
2025-03-04 - QA: Preliminary harness found 3 failing prompts due to tokenizer differences; added tokenizer-adapter step to harness
testing_plan: Unit tests for covenant JSON schema validation
E2E onboarding job that runs against 3 representative model artifacts (open-model-small, open-model-medium, redwood-base)
Regression CI job: run golden prompt set and compare fingerprints, fail if semantic_delta > threshold or output-shape mismatch > 0
Performance sanity: run perf signature measurement on standard infra (g4dn-equivalent) and compare to baseline
rollout_plan: Deploy onboard CLI to internal CI and run against incoming model onboarding PRs for two weeks in shadow mode
Publish covenant-aware catalog entries for new pinned versions; existing pinned models will be backfilled on demand
After two weeks of shadowing and fixes, flip gating to block onboarding when covenant fails
Communicate to customers via release notes and a short migration guide for parameter aliasing
