source.title: Hysteresis + probe noise led to underscaling during a bursty cold-start (postmortem)
source.creator: Riley Morgan
source.description: During a production traffic spike on 2025-02-17 we observed a period of sustained high tail latency and elevated 5xxs for a subset of inference routes. The immediate mitigation restored capacity, but analysis showed HPA/KEDA configuration interacting with probe noise and cold-start latency produced sustained underscaling during the burst window. This document summarizes timeline, root cause, mitigations, and action items.
source.timeline:
2025-02-17 09:45 UTC - Traffic spike begins for /v1/generate routes (client A ramp test).
2025-02-17 09:47 UTC - SRE alerts: 95th percentile latency > 800ms and 5xx rate increase.
2025-02-17 09:49 UTC - Automated fallback to a lower-cost model activated for affected tenants.
2025-02-17 09:50 UTC - Manual scale-up request executed (increase pool target replicas).
2025-02-17 10:05 UTC - Latency returns to baseline after extra capacity and warm pool activation.
2025-02-18 - Deep-dive started with serving/runtime and platform teams.
2025-02-25 - Root-cause hypothesis validated in staging with traffic replay.
2025-03-03 - Configuration changes and PRs merged; controlled canary rollout completed.
source.detection: Alerting triggered on latency SLO breach and elevated 5xx. Initial dashboards suggested normal cluster CPU metrics, causing confusion. Further investigation of scaler telemetry and Prometheus scrape intervals revealed noisy target metric sampling around the spike onset which hid the actual queue-depth increase from the scaler for several scrape windows.
source.impact: Approximately 17 minutes of degraded latency for 4% of production requests on a subset of routes behind a particular model variant during the spike window. No persistent data loss. Some customers experienced higher tail latency and transient 5xxs; most recovered after routing fallback and manual scale-up.
source.root_cause: Multiple contributing factors combined to produce an underscaling regression: 1) KEDA Prometheus scaler used a raw per-replica latency metric (p95_latency) that had high short-term variance; 2) Prometheus scrape interval (30s) + KEDA polling introduced aliasing so KEDA saw a smoothed/attenuated signal for ~60-90s after spike onset; 3) HPA stabilizationWindowSeconds and KEDA cooldown settings were too short for our cold-start characteristics leading to premature scale-down decisions immediately prior to the spike; 4) Pod readiness probe initialDelay and model warm-up time caused new replicas to be unready for longer than KEDA/HPA expected, reducing effective capacity even as replicas were created; 5) No warm standby pool for certain dedicated capacity tiers, so cold-start cost was high and scaling decisions needed to account for it.
source.mitigation:
Short-term: 1) Enabled a temporary routing fallback to alternate model variants for affected tenants during high spike alerts (rolled back after stable). 2) Increased KEDA polling frequency to reduce detection latency for the specific Prometheus scaler (from 60s to 20s) in the affected clusters. 3) Temporarily increased HPA minReplicas for critical model routes to ensure headroom.
Medium-term: 1) Adjusted readiness probe initialDelaySeconds and added a model-warm readiness check to avoid marking pods Ready before the model is usable. 2) Updated Prometheus exporter to provide a more stable scaling metric (request_queue_depth) computed as a moving-average and exposed at 10s resolution. 3) Tuned HPA stabilizationWindowSeconds to 120s and KEDA cooldown to 90s to add hysteresis.
Long-term: Implement warm pool for model nodes and change default autoscaler config to use the new queue-based metric and smoothing window.
source.long_term_fix: 1) Implement node-level warm pool (pre-warmed replicas) for high-cost model families; integrate warm pool awareness into rollout/placement algorithms. 2) Replace per-pod noisy latency metric with cluster-level request_queue_depth moving average as primary autoscaling signal for inference groups. 3) Add adaptive hysteresis: when a cold-start penalty is detected, increase stabilizationWindow and temporarily bias scaling towards scale-up. 4) Build an autoscale test harness (synthetic bursts) in CI that validates HPA/KEDA reactions under realistic cold-start latencies. 5) Document recommended scaler metrics and platform defaults in runtime-ops runbook.
action_items: AH-101: Converge on scaling metric: switch Prometheus exporter to expose request_queue_depth_ma_30s (owner: Daniel Park, due: 2025-03-20)
AH-102: Implement model warm readiness probe that validates tokenization + 1-step forward pass (owner: Samira Patel, due: 2025-03-12)
AH-103: Adjust KEDA Prometheus scaler polling to 15s for inference clusters and add jitter (owner: Jorge Alvarez, due: 2025-03-07)
AH-104: Increase HPA stabilizationWindowSeconds default to 120s for inference workloads and add config toggle (owner: Priya Nair, due: 2025-03-14)
AH-105: Design and prototype warm pool implementation for heavy model families (owner: Ava Chen, due: 2025-04-01)
AH-106: Add autoscale burst test scenarios to CI and runbooks (owner: Riley Morgan, due: 2025-03-21)
AH-107: Postmortem review and ops runbook update; include playbook for temporary fallbacks and manual scale-up steps (owner: SRE Team, due: 2025-03-10)
AH-108: Create dashboard panel showing KEDA polling, Prometheus scrape timestamps, and replica creation events to aid future investigations (owner: Yun Lee, due: 2025-03-09)
AH-109: Run a controlled canary of the new scaler metric in staging with traffic replay (owner: Platform QA, due: 2025-03-18)

title: Hysteresis + probe noise led to underscaling during a bursty cold-start (postmortem)
preview: During a production traffic spike on 2025-02-17 we observed a period of sustained high tail latency and elevated 5xxs for a subset of inference routes. The immediate mitigation restored capacity, but analysis showed HPA/KEDA configuration interacting with probe noise and cold-start latency produced sustained underscaling during the burst window. This document summarizes timeline, root cause, mitigations, and action items.
text: During a production traffic spike on 2025-02-17 we observed a period of sustained high tail latency and elevated 5xxs for a subset of inference routes. The immediate mitigation restored capacity, but analysis showed HPA/KEDA configuration interacting with probe noise and cold-start latency produced sustained underscaling during the burst window. This document summarizes timeline, root cause, mitigations, and action items.

source.title: Model-tailored KV-slice memory profiles for long-context Llama/Mistral/Qwen variants
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Background:
Redwood's serving runtime benefits from per-model memory profiles that encode KV residency, slab sizes, and eviction heuristics. Existing generic profiles are conservative for long-context variants of Llama/Mistral/Qwen: they either waste GPU headroom or trigger OOMs under realistic long-sequence workloads.

Objective:
Produce model-specific, quant-variant-aware memory profiles ("KV-slice profiles") for Llama, Mistral, and Qwen families that optimize GPU utilization for long-context configs (16k-64k tokens) while preserving latency SLOs and reducing OOM incidence. Profiles should be consumable by the allocator and the routing layer to enable safe fallbacks to lower-footprint variants.

Scope & Non-Goals:
- In scope: offline profiling harness, profile data format, allocator knobs (slab size, span thresholds), KV-slice residency policies, eviction weighting, and integration tests with continuous batching.
- Out of scope: model training changes, model architecture modifications, and non-GPU runtime targets.

Success criteria / acceptance:
- 25–40% reduction in average GPU resident KV footprint for evaluated long-context workloads without increasing 95th percentile generation latency by >5%.
- OOM rate reduction to <0.5% on synthetic and customer-derived long-context traces.
- Auto-generated profiles for at least one quant variant (8-bit) of each model family and documented rollout plan for production traffic.

Proposed approach (experiments):
1) Offline microbench profiler: run representative sequences (2k/8k/32k/64k) across model variants and quantizations to capture per-layer KV page residency, peak allocator occupancy, and fragmentation metrics. Output: CSV + canonical profile JSON. Link: https://confluence.redwood.com/display/INF/kv-slice-profiles-harness

2) KV-slice profile format: small JSON describing per-layer expected residency curve, prefer-slab-size, eviction-weight, and batching-hint (max prefetched tokens). Profiles are intentionally conservative during canary.

3) Dynamic slice sizing heuristic: use model footprint + request sequence length histogram to compute per-GPU slice limits. Heuristic: base_slice = footprint * (seq_len / baseline_len) * safety_factor; safety_factor tuned per-model.

4) Eviction policy tuning: hybrid LRU+weight where layers with heavy KVs (attention keys/values) get lower eviction priority; quant-aware penalties for 8-bit variants. Implement a grace window to avoid thrashing with continuous batching.

5) Allocator improvements: introduce slab compaction triggers when fragmentation > threshold and free headroom < X MB. Validate performance impact (compaction must not spike latency SLOs).

6) Continuous batching integration: add batch-aware residency estimator so batch formation respects slice limits. If batch would exceed slice budget, prioritize earliest requests and optionally fall back to smaller model (via routing policy).

Testing plan:
- Unit tests for profile parser and heuristic calculator.
- Simulated load tests against long-context replay traces (customer A, internal long-doc corpus) on dedicated nodes (p3/onnx setups).
- Canary rollout: start with 5% traffic on runtime-1.22 with telemetry hooks (alloc headroom, OOMs, token throughput, p50/p95 latency). Grafana dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.com/d/kv-profiles-longctx

Risks & mitigations:
- Incorrect profiles cause regressions / OOMs: mitigate with conservative default safety_factor=1.5, trial canary, and automatic rollback if OOM rate > 1%.
- Compaction latency spikes: gate compaction behind latency budget and run on idle cycles where possible.
- Interaction with existing batching heuristics: coordinate with batching owners (see ENG-31472) and run combined stress tests.

Rollout steps:
1) Produce canonical profiles for 8-bit of Llama-2-Chat-70B, Mistral-Chat-22B, Qwen-14B: offline validation + PR (runtime + allocator).
2) Internal canary on dedicated capacity (10% traffic) for two weeks.
3) Expand to 50% with staggered region rollout.
4) Full production enable with opt-out flag for customers.

Artifacts & links:
- Profiler harness: https://github.com/redwood-inference/kv-profile-harness/pull/87
- Design doc: https://confluence.redwood.com/display/INF/Model-Tailored-KV-Slice-Profiles
- Perf results (initial): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2b3c-kv-profiles-report (internal)

Open questions / decisions needed:
- Should profile generation be part of the model onboarding pipeline (yes/no)? Recommendation: yes for any model accepted into catalog.
- Safety_factor default (1.2 vs 1.5) — PM input required.

Next steps:
- Diego to complete profiler run for the three target models by 2025-03-10.
- Create runtime PR to add profile parser and eviction-weight hook (ENG-915432-rt-run) and assign reviewers from allocator team.
- Schedule combined stress test with batching team (owner: Priya Nair).
comments: 2025-02-19 - Priya Nair: Reviewed scope, asked to include production traffic replay from customer X (added to test traces).
2025-02-24 - Diego Martinez: Initial profiler run completed for Llama-2-Chat-70B (8-bit). Peak KV reduced ~28% vs generic profile; prepping Mistral and Qwen runs.
2025-03-01 - Eng Review: Suggest lowering compaction frequency and adding metric for compaction-induced latency spikes. Action: Diego to prototype background compaction with throttling.
2025-03-04 - Ava Chen: Approved canary plan pending runtime PR and Grafana dashboards. Reminder to include automatic rollback CRON job for OOM spikes.
2025-03-05 - Diego Martinez: Uploaded preliminary profile JSONs to PR and linked canary dashboard; scheduling canary start 2025-03-12.

source.title: Rotation scoping mismatch causing privilege escalation between orgs during token refresh
source.creator: Aisha Malik
source.description: Multiple customers reported unexpected cross-organization access following API key rotations and token refreshes in Dedicated and Private deployments. Investigation shows that under a rare combination of rolling rotations and scaled routing, the token refresh endpoint returned a session token scoped incorrectly (missing tenant binding), allowing a refreshed token to be used against a different org's resources. Hosted deployments are largely unaffected due to a separate auth proxy that enforces org headers; the issue appears in our self-hosted auth-service path used by Dedicated/Private customers. This ticket tracks the immediate remediation, root cause fix, test coverage, rollout, and customer communications.
reproducible_steps: 1) Use Dedicated/private deployment with API key rotation enabled and at least two auth-service replicas behind the router
2) Start a request using API key A from org-A, trigger an in-flight refresh (client calls /token/refresh) while rotation moves API key A to a new rotation epoch

3) Router routes the refresh to replica-1 which has stale local cache but global rotation metadata updated, replica-1 performs cache lookup missing tenant-scoped key and falls back to a global-apikey lookup

4) Replica-1 issues a new access token lacking org-specific claim (tenant_id) or with the wrong tenant_id, client receives token and then is able to call endpoints scoped to org-B (if routing sends them there), reproducing cross-org access
5) Observe auth-service logs that show cache_miss->global_lookup->issued_token without tenant_id and a concurrent rotation event within the same second
source.impact_summary: High severity. Two enterprise customers (AcmeHealth and FinDataCo) reported elevated privileges after key rotations; they were able to access resources from other org contexts for a short window. No evidence yet of data exfiltration, but immediate token/key rotation and audit required. Affects Dedicated and Private (on-prem) installations using auth-service v1.12.0->v1.13.4; Hosted uses a hardened proxy and shows no reproductions.
source.technical_root_cause: Auth-service cache key derivation omitted tenant_id under a fallback path when a local cache miss coincided with a rotation epoch change. The fallback used an unscoped key (global api_key_id) leading to tokens being minted without tenant binding. Additionally, there was a race in the in-memory rotation_epoch update path between replicas where a refresh request could observe a partially updated state.
source.fix_summary: 1) Scope the cache and token minting logic to require tenant_id + api_key_id + rotation_epoch in all lookup paths. 2) Add an atomic rotation_epoch update with versioned metadata to avoid partial reads. 3) Return explicit errors for refresh attempts that cannot be bound to a tenant instead of falling back to global lookup. 4) Add unit, integration, and e2e tests that simulate rotation+refresh races. 5) Backport patch to auth-service releases 1.13.x and 1.12.x and publish a dedicated hotfix image for self-hosted customers.
source.rollback_plan: If the fix causes regressions, rollback to auth-service v1.13.3 (or previous stable) using the existing deployment rollback scripts. For Dedicated customers, revoke the hotfix image and instruct clusters to revert using the same helm rollbacks. Notify customers via the incident channel before rollback.
rollout_plan: Cut auth-service v1.13.5-hotfix with cache key scoping changes
Deploy canary to 3 pods in staging and run replayed rotating+refresh traffic
Canary to 10% of production Dedicated clusters (non-critical) for 2 hours while monitoring auth_mismatch_rate and token_issuance_without_tenant metrics
If metrics stable, ramp to 100% across Dedicated, then publish hotfix image and helm chart for Private customers and coordinate with SRE/Customer Success for on-prem upgrades
Trigger forced rotation of affected API keys for high-risk customers and provide rotation script + audit logs
postmortem_actions: Add automated e2e test in CI that replays rotation+refresh race scenarios
Instrument a new metric: token_issued_without_tenant -> alert at >0
Update runbook sections for token rotation to include tenant-scoped verification step
Audit all Dedicated/Private clusters for auth-service versions and schedule mandatory hotfix upgrades
Customer notification template and assisted rotation script prepared by Customer Success
comments: 2025-02-27 09:12 - Aisha Malik: Initial triage after customer report; creating ENG-10023. Severity set to P0 due to potential cross-org access.
2025-02-27 11:30 - SRE: Captured logs and confirmed pattern of cache_miss + global_lookup during rotation epochs on two Dedicated clusters.
2025-02-28 02:05 - Ethan Park: Drafted patch to require tenant_id in cache lookup; PR 2345 opened for hotfix.
2025-03-01 14:22 - QA: Added integration test that reproduces the race locally; failing on master, passes on hotfix branch.
2025-03-03 08:40 - PM (Leila Gomez): Coordinating customer communications. Draft sent to legal and CS for review.
2025-03-04 07:55 - Ethan Park: Hotfix merged to release branch and image built. Beginning staged rollout per plan.
2025-03-04 09:10 - SRE: Observed token_issued_without_tenant metric drop to 0 on canary clusters after hotfix deployment. Monitoring continues.

source.title: Warm-Standby Certification & Region Handoff Orchestrator (hopscotch method)
source.creator: Aisha Kaur
source.description: Goal: Implement an orchestrator and certification pipeline that exercises multi-region failover semantics and validates warm-standby readiness using a staggered 'hopscotch' priming pattern to avoid synchronized cache churn. This ticket covers: (1) a controller that coordinates region selection and tier routing decisions during outage windows, (2) an automated readiness scoring service that runs periodic probes and computes a single readiness_score per region+tier+model_variant, (3) an acceptance/test harness to certify warm standby pools before they are accepted as active failover targets, and (4) telemetry and alerting to detect degraded priming or variant incompatibilities. Context: previous warm-start efforts experienced synchronous cache invalidations when all standby pools followed the same priming cadence. The hopscotch method staggers priming across regions and model variants so at least one candidate remains cache-hot while others are being refreshed. This reduces client-visible latency spikes during cross-region handoffs and improves success rate of automatic failovers. Scope: orchestrator logic + readiness probe definitions + certificate issuance for standby pools + rollout plan and runbook updates. Out of scope: changes to model training or format conversions; separate ticket will handle model-variant packaging if needed.
acceptance_criteria: Readiness service produces a readiness_score (0.0-1.0) for each region:tier:variant every 5 minutes
Orchestrator will only nominate a standby region for active failover if readiness_score >= 0.85 and kv_cache_hit_rate >= 70% over the last 10 minutes
Hopscotch priming ensures no more than 1 region in a failover group is in 'priming' state simultaneously
Automated certification runbook (CI) exercises a synthetic traffic profile and verifies latency degradation <= 15% compared to baseline and token-error-rate increase <= 0.1%
Telemetry dashboard with per-route readiness, priming_phase, and fallback_variant_mismatch alert is live and wired to oncall with runbook links
Canary rollout to a single customer-scoped Dedicated fleet shows zero client-visible errors during a simulated region outage test
Unit and integration tests for orchestrator decision logic with coverage of priority inversion, partial priming, and model fallback rules
design_decisions: Adopt hopscotch priming: staggered timer offsets per region derived from region hash and topology group
Represent readiness as composite metric from kv_cache_hit_rate, tail_latency_p95, model_variant_compatibility_score, and successful_probe_ratio
Keep orchestration decisions policy-driven: separate the policy layer (routing rules) from execution layer (actuators) to allow safe policy rollbacks
Fallback variants are versioned and ranked; policy allows graceful degradation by preferring lower-cost variants only when latency delta < 15%
Telemetry schema to use existing tracing tags (region, tier, variant, readiness_score) and emit 'certification_event' for auditability
rollout_plan: Phase 0: Unit and fuzz testing locally against model-sandbox images (until 2025-02-28)
Phase 1: Internal canary in staging: enable orchestrator for a non-production region pair, run 48-hour soak and simulated region outage (2025-03-03 to 2025-03-06)
Phase 2: Small production canary: route 1% of cross-region failover traffic to orchestrator-managed standby for a single tenant, monitor errors and latency for 72 hours
Phase 3: Gradual ramp to all region pairs with automated rollback if readiness_score regression or SLO breach detected
telemetry_and_alerting: Dashboard: failover-readiness-overview (readiness_score trend, per-region priming_phase, kv_cache_hit_rate)
Alert: readiness_score_below_threshold -> P0 to oncall when score < 0.7 for 15m
Alert: priming_overlap_detected -> warn team when >1 region in group in 'priming' state
SLO: cross-region failover success_rate >= 99.5% over 24h window after enabling orchestrator
implementation_notes: Controller will be implemented as a new subservice (region-handoff-agent) that consumes health events and probe results from the readiness service
Priming timers computed with a 32-bit deterministic hash of (region_id, group_id, variant) to spread offsets
Certification artifacts: signed certificates issued to standby pools when they pass the CI certification harness; certificate TTL defaults to 6 hours
Actuators will call existing routing-gatekeeper APIs to promote/demote standby candidates; a safe-mode flag prevents automatic promotion in early rollouts
We will reuse the existing KV cache metrics pipeline; add a small probe that performs model-specific prefix lookups to validate cache effectiveness
risks_and_mitigations: Risk: misclassification of readiness leading to failed failover -> Mitigation: conservative thresholds and two-step promotion (nominate -> certify -> promote)
Risk: probe-induced cache pollution during priming -> Mitigation: synthetic probes use short-lived prefixes and tag traffic as 'certification_probe' so it is excluded from billing and analytics
Risk: increased control-plane load -> Mitigation: rate-limit certification runs and shard readiness computations by region group
Risk: variant incompatibility causing errors -> Mitigation: add a compatibility-check probe that runs a small canonical prompt set through candidate variants before certification
testing_results: 2025-02-24: Local POC showed hopscotch reduced peak cold-start p95 by ~34% in a 3-region simulation
2025-02-27: Staging soak run: readiness_score stability 0.9+ for primary region, standby regions varied 0.83-0.88 depending on load
2025-03-02: Integration test: certification CI failed intermittently due to race in certificate TTL refresh (flaky, investigating)
updates: 2025-02-19: Kickoff review with PM and SRE; agreed on composite readiness metric and conservative default thresholds
2025-02-22: Prototype probes implemented and surfaced in staging metrics; discovered a mismatch between storage-backed KV metrics and probe-level cache hits
2025-02-25: Implemented hopscotch offset algorithm; initial simulation shows reduced synchronized cache evictions
2025-02-28: Design doc posted to Confluence and linked (see links) after PM signoff
2025-03-01: PR #1234 opened for region-handoff-agent scaffolding; awaiting security review for new service account
2025-03-04: Integration CI failing in certificate refresh path; temporarily gated automatic promotion behind safe-mode flag

title: Warm-Standby Certification & Region Handoff Orchestrator (hopscotch method)
preview: Goal: Implement an orchestrator and certification pipeline that exercises multi-region failover semantics and validates warm-standby readiness using a staggered 'hopscotch' priming pattern to avoid synchronized cache churn. This ticket covers: (1) a controller that coordinates region selection and tier routing decisions during outage windows, (2) an automated readiness scoring service that runs periodic probes and computes a single readiness_score per region+tier+model_variant, (3) an acceptance/test harness to certify warm standby pools before they are accepted as active failover targets, and (4) telemetry and alerting to detect degraded priming or variant incompatibilities. Context: previous warm-start efforts experienced synchronous cache invalidations when all standby pools followed the same priming cadence. The hopscotch method staggers priming across regions and model variants so at least one candidate remains cache-hot while others are being refreshed. This reduces client-visible latency spikes during cross-region handoffs and improves success rate of automatic failovers. Scope: orchestrator logic + readiness probe definitions + certificate issuance for standby pools + rollout plan and runbook updates. Out of scope: changes to model training or format conversions; separate ticket will handle model-variant packaging if needed.

source.title: Synthetic Load Validator and Reservation Autocalibration Playbook
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Summary:
During a capacity incident on 2025-02-10 we observed degraded throughput across several Dedicated clusters. Investigation pointed to a combination of: (1) under-provisioned tenant reservations due to stale traffic estimates, (2) noisy-neighbor workloads that generated burst token exhaustion, and (3) burst policy thresholds that allowed aggressive short-term bursts without adequate backoff, leading to cascading latency spikes.

Goal:
Create a reproducible synthetic load validator and an automated reservation autocalibration playbook that: (a) reproduces the incident class with controlled noisy tenants and realistic KV/cache utilization, (b) derives corrective reservation deltas, and (c) produces safe degraded-burst fallback policies that reduce tail latency without unduly impacting throughput.

Scope and non-goals:
- Scope: Dedicated cluster behavior, tenant reservation sizing, burst token lifecycle, telemetry augmentations, runbook changes, CI gating for reservation changes.
- Non-goals: Changes to customer-visible pricing, on-prem deployment packaging, or host-level kernel changes.

Incident timeline (high level):
- 2025-02-10 02:14 UTC: Alert: sustained 99th pct latency > 2.5s across cluster-dedicated-12.
- 02:18 UTC: Autoscaler attempted to add capacity but reservation rebalancer was delayed by lock contention.
- 02:23 UTC: Several tenants reported 429s when bursts were consumed; tokenbucket metrics show exhaustion event spikes.
- 02:45 UTC: Manual mitigation: soft-fence noisy tenants and temporarily increase reservation of impacted tenants.

Root causes (synthesized):
- Reservation mismatch: historic quota computation used an exponentially weighted moving average tuned for steady-state, underreacting to sudden growth of 2.5x in a top tenant's QPS.
- Burst policy gap: burst allowance (token refill rate) was permissive but lacked graceful decay; no intermediate throttling step between full allowance and hard throttles.
- Telemetry blind spots: synthetic replay tests were not run for cross-tenant KV cache eviction patterns which amplified per-request cost.
- Operational contention: reservation rebalance run serialized under global lock; during incident it queued behind long-running topology reconciliation.

Proposed action items (short, medium, long):
1) Short (this sprint):
  - Build "synthetic-load-validator" harness that can emulate: multi-tenant steady + burst traffic, KV cache pressure, variable sequence lengths, and controlled noisy behavior. (Owner: ENG-ops)
  - Add tokenbucket-exhaustion alert with correlated reservation-change events in Grafana and link to runbook. (Owner: Observability)
  - Implement emergency soft-fence API: quick-apply tenant isolation mode that limits new bursts for a tenant while preserving inflight inference. (Owner: runtime)
2) Medium (next 2 sprints):
  - Autocalibration algorithm: forecast-driven reservation adjustment using short-window percentile (P90 over 10m + burst headroom) plus hysteresis and cooldown. Provide dry-run outputs in staging for 24h before primetime. (Owner: scheduler)
  - Replace global rebalance lock with per-pool coordination and deadline-based preemption to avoid queueing. (Owner: allocator)
  - Add synthetic test step to CI that runs a 30m noisy-neighbor scenario and validates no 99th-pt uplift beyond baseline. (Owner: testing infra)
3) Long (Q2+):
  - Policy simulation tooling to model cluster-level fairness and revenue impact of reservation deltas. (Owner: capacity-analytics)
  - Explore tiered burst decay policies (soft-throttle -> token-rate-reduction -> hard-throttle) with configurable knobs per customer tier. (Owner: runtime/policy)

Acceptance criteria:
- Synthetic harness reproduces observed tokenbucket exhaustion within 10% of incident metrics when fed historical traffic profile.
- Autocalibration performs reservation adjustments that reduce 99th-pt latency by >= 30% in the noisy scenario while keeping overall throughput within 5% of baseline.
- Emergency soft-fence can be applied in < 90 seconds and documented in the runbook.
- CI synthetic test runs in under 40 minutes and fails the PR if 99th-pt latency or reservation drift regression occurs.

Design notes and trade-offs:
- Forecasting approach: choose between reactive short-window percentile (less aggressive) vs ML model (more accurate but operationally heavier). We'll begin with deterministic percentile+cooldown for safety, with a supervised ML model as future work.
- Synthetic fidelity: running full-scale GPU-backed synthetic tests is expensive. We'll use a tiered strategy: simulated CPU-only high-level load for quick CI checks, and nightly full-GPU clusters in staging for high-fidelity validation.
- Soft-fence semantics: design to avoid disrupting in-flight requests by throttling new token grants; this reduces blast radius while preserving ongoing responses.

Runbook and CI changes:
- Add step in Dedicated Incident runbook: "Apply soft-fence to tenant -> verify tokenbucket metrics -> run synthetic validator in local-mode". Include rollback steps for soft-fence.
- CI: add job `noisy_neighbour_smoke` that executes the lightweight harness and validates P95/P99 and reservation stability.

Telemetry and dashboards to build:
- Grafana: "Reservation Autocalibrator" dashboard: per-tenant reservation, estimated desired reservation, tokenbucket fill-rate, exhaustion events, KV-eviction rate.
- Alerting: new composite alert combining tokenbucket_exhaustion OR reservation_delta > 3x within 5m, with pager if customer_impact==high.

Owners and collaborators:
- Runtime: Priya Nair (design soft-fence, burst decay)
- Scheduler/Allocator: Liam Patel (autocalibration, lock removal) [assignee]
- Observability: Marco Ruiz (dashboards, alerts)
- Testing infra: Zoe Kim (synthetic harness + CI jobs)
- Capacity analytics: Elias Novak (policy simulation)

Deliverables and timeline:
- Week 1: lightweight synthetic harness prototype + initial dashboard (MVP).
- Week 2-3: autocalibration prototype in staging (dry-run logs) + lock removal POC.
- Week 4: deploy soft-fence + enable CI synthetic test; measure impact with 2-week canary.

Notes and links:
- Postmortem: https://confluence.redwood.ai/dedicated-capacity-postmortem-2025-02-10
- Incident Grafana: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/alerts/dedicated-capacity-2025-02-10
- Design doc (prototype harness): https://drive.redwood.ai/drive/folders/synthetic-load-validator-proto
- Related tickets: ENG-29315 (spike-mitigation-autotuner), ENG-52489 (reservation policy review), ENG-41322 (burst calibration)
comments: 2025-02-19: Kickoff sync completed. Zoe delivered an initial CPU-mode harness that can emulate tokenbucket behavior and KV eviction pressure. Rough parity with incident metrics for token exhaustion events achieved (within 20%).
2025-02-22: Design review feedback from Priya: prefer stepwise burst decay (soft-throttle first) and include SDK flag to allow customer-tier overrides. Action: include tier mapping in policy design.
2025-02-25: Marco added dashboard panels and a composite alert rule to the staging Grafana folder. Observability PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/observability/pull/812
2025-03-01: Scheduler team implemented a dry-run mode for autocalibration. Dry-run logs stored in GCS: gs://redwood-staging/autocalib/dryrun-2025-03-01.log
2025-03-03: Lock removal POC showed 85% reduction in rebalance latency under synthetic load. Follow-up: integrate deadline-based preemption to avoid live topology churn.
2025-03-04: Soft-fence API spec reviewed by security; no elevated risk. Implementation PR planned next week: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/1409

source.title: Autonomous SLO-driven traffic throttler with contextual paging and runbook automation
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Design and implement an autonomous traffic throttler that uses per-customer and per-route SLO slack to drive probabilistic load shedding and region-aware circuit breaker adjustments. Integrate with the paging layer to suppress low-confidence pages and auto-generate on-call runbook actions (scale, redirect, degraded-mode) when mitigation is automated. Goal is to reduce paging noise during transient overloads while keeping customer-visible SLOs within agreed bounds.
acceptance_criteria: Deployable service behind feature flag that computes SLO slack per-customer and per-route every 10s
Probabilistic shedding policy exercised in chaos tests with controlled error injection (see test plan) and reduces CPU/GPU pressure by >= 25% under overload scenarios
Paging volume reduced by >= 60% for transient overloads in synthetic test without materially increasing customer SLO breaches (<= 5% delta to baseline)
Circuit breaker state transitions logged and observable in Console with per-region decisions and root-cause tags
Automated runbook action execution (scale-up, redirect-to-secondary, enable-degraded-mode) triggered by policy and reversible via a single console toggle
Comprehensive unit/integration tests and a staging canary validated across two regions
Documentation and runbook templates added to Confluence and linked from Console incident UI
implementation_notes: Component will run as a control-plane actor adjacent to router service; computes per-customer SLO slack using token consumption and historical latency/error windows (30m, 5m, 1m)
Shedding algorithm: compute p_shed = min(1, alpha * (expected_cost / available_slack)) where expected_cost is estimated token cost + GPU time; alpha tunable per-tier

Circuit breaker adjustments: maintain three soft thresholds (warning, throttling, hard-open). Soft thresholds scale with observed regional capacity and predicted 1min load. When hard-open hit, route to fallback model variant or secondary region depending on policy

Paging integration: enrich alerts with anomaly_score (0-100), policy_confidence and remediation_suggestion; suppress pages where policy_confidence < 0.6 and anomaly_score < 30, instead create a ticket in Ops backlog and annotate console incident

Runbook automation: predefined playbooks (scale-gpu-pool, route-to-secondary, enable-degraded-outputs) implementable as idempotent actions. Execution tracked with runbook_execution_id and rollback hooks
Decision to keep probabilistic shedding (vs deterministic) to avoid synchronized failure modes across tenants; this reduces cascading scaling events
Telemetry: rely on existing traces/metrics (latency p50/p95/p99, error rate, queue length, gpu_util, region_capacity_remaining, kv_cache_hit_rate) plus synthetic health-checks per-region per-minute
Data retention: sliding windows computed in-memory with bounded memory; persistent audit log of mitigation decisions stored in infra-events bucket for 90d
Risk: possible customer-visible quality degradation when shedding; mitigate by tiered policies (enterprise customers have epsilon shedding) and opt-out control for Dedicated/Private deployments
rollout_plan: Phase 0 (dev): unit tests and local simulator for 1000 tenants; algorithmic validation
Phase 1 (staging): deploy behind feature flag in staging with synthetic overloads against staging GPUs; validate metrics and page suppression behavior
Phase 2 (canary): enable for 2% of production traffic in region-us-west with detailed tracing and mandatory observability dashboard
Phase 3 (regional expand): increase to 20% then 50% after 48-72h validation windows; monitor SLO delta, paging reductions, and false-suppression events
Phase 4 (global): enable cross-region coordination for multi-region customers and enable automated redirect policies; ship doc and runbook updates
Rollback: feature flag kill switch that disables shedding and circuit-breaker overrides; runbook defines immediate rollback and forced full-fidelity serving
comments: 2025-02-11 - Ava Chen: Initial spike completed. Local simulator validates probabilistic shedding reduces GPU pressure by ~28% in 30% overload scenario. Next: integrate per-tenant SLO inputs.
2025-02-18 - Diego Martinez: Implemented initial control-plane actor with in-memory sliding windows. Pushed prototype to PR #2761 for early feedback.
2025-02-21 - SRE (review): Concern about race between shedding and autoscaler; recommended adding a short cooldown window (30s) before shedding re-evaluation. Added to implementation notes.
2025-02-25 - PM (Riya Patel): Request to expose an enterprise-safe policy that never sheds for committed dedicated capacity customers. Added tiered policy requirement.

2025-03-01 - Observability (Kai Nguyen): Dashboard added; synthetic test shows page volume down 64% during transient spike, SLO breach delta +2.1% (within acceptance bounds). Need to validate for longer burst patterns.

2025-03-04 - Diego Martinez: Began staging rollout behind feature flag. Running 6h canary synthetic runs. Logged edge-case where high-fidelity customers saw increased fallback-to-secondary due to miscalibrated expected_cost. Tuning in progress.

source.title: Tenant credit-based region routing with variant shadowing and graceful hosted backfill
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Summary:
Design and implement a tenant credit allocator that influences region & tier selection for dedicated capacity while enabling controlled hosted spillback. The allocator issues soft "credits" (soft reservations) based on pre-commit, historical consumption, and SLA tier. Credits drive probabilistic routing decisions: prefer dedicated when credits >= cost_estimate, gradually bleed to hosted when credits are low.

Goals:
- Reduce unexpected hosted spillovers for customers with reserved capacity while allowing controlled, observable backfill to hosted for short bursts.
- Maintain latency SLOs for P0/P1 tenants and keep unit cost predictable for reserved customers.
- Provide a transparent, debuggable fallback mechanism to hosted and to lower-cost model variants.

Design highlights:
1) Credit allocator
- Each tenant has a credit balance updated continuously: credits += reserved_rate * time_window - actual_consumption_cost. Credits are bounded and never drop below a soft negative floor.
- Credit tiers: GREEN (>75%), AMBER (25%-75%), RED (<25%). Routing preference score = base_affinity * f(credit_tier) * tenant_priority_scale.

2) Probabilistic routing with hysteresis
- For each request compute P(dedicated) = sigmoid((score - threshold)/temperature). Add request jitter and short-term moving-average smoothing to avoid flapping.
- Hysteresis windows vary by tier: GREEN (30s), AMBER (2m), RED (10m).

3) Variant shadowing and demotion
- If hosted or lower-cost variant is selected, send a shadow copy to a demoted variant for quality monitoring (no charge to tenant). Shadowing includes: prompt/response hashing, deterministic knob to redact sensitive fields, and TTL for cache.
- Maintain per-variant quality deltas; if demoted variant drops below quality delta threshold for >N requests, automatically escalate to human-in-the-loop review and revert demotion if necessary.

4) Graceful hosted backfill policy
- Backfill is allowed for short bursts if predicted dedicated wait time > backfill_threshold and tenant in RED/AMBER. Backfill must carry metadata (origin:dedicated-backfill, credit_balance, fallback_decision_id) for observability and billing reconciliation.
- Introduce a backfill budget per-tenant per-day to limit cost exposure from hosted spillover.

5) Preemption & recovery
- Dedicated slots are preemptible by a request to reclaim credits: reclaiming triggers a soft-fence preventing re-routing to hosted for a brief cooldown, and emits an event to the tenant alerting them to degraded capacity.
- Recovery path replenishes credits based on committed onboarding schedule and usage smoothing.

Telemetry & observability:
- Emit these metrics at request-level and aggregated: routing_decision(reason), credit_balance_gauge, time_in_tier, backfill_event_count, variant_quality_delta, failed_shadow_rate, cost_delta_per_hour.
- Dashboards: tenant-level SLO heatmap, backfill budget burn-rate, variant-regression alerts.
- Traceable IDs: every decision must surface fallback_decision_id and policy_version in logs to enable postmortems.

Acceptance criteria:
- Simulated 48-hour stress test shows >60% reduction in unexpected hosted spillovers for tenants with >50% reserved commits compared to baseline routing.
- No increase in P0 99th p99 latency beyond 5% during simulated burst tests.
- Integration tests for credit accounting, backfill tagging, and variant shadowing coverage >90%.

Rollout plan:
1) Canary: enable credit allocator for 2 internal tenants and one low-risk external tenant (1 week).
2) Monitoring: create dashboards and an automated guardrail that disables backfill when budget burn-rate exceeds 120% of expected.
3) Progressive exposure: +10% of tenants per day with automated rollback on regression.
4) Post-rollout: 2-week observation then adjust tier thresholds and hysteresis windows based on real traffic.

Open questions / tradeoffs:
- How to reconcile multi-region commitments where tenant reserves span multiple hemisheres? Proposal: maintain per-region credits with cross-region transfer cap. This adds complexity but preserves locality.
- Determining cost_estimate for long-running streaming requests. We propose a moving-window model that predicts token consumption after N tokens.
- Privacy: shadowing preserves request fingerprints but must redact PII; integrate with KMS and redact rules from private deployments.

Links & artifacts:
- Design doc (Confluence): https://confluence.redwood.ai/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=7682002
- Initial simulation notebook: https://github.com/redwood-inference/routing-simulations/tree/tenant-credit-experiments
- Draft PR (routing service): https://github.com/redwood-inference/platform/pull/5312
- Runbook stub: https://wiki.redwood.ai/runbooks/dedicated-credit-backfill-runbook
comments: 2025-02-21 - Ava Chen: Ran initial offline simulation with synthetic spikes (Poisson arrivals). Early numbers: for tenants with 30-50% reserve, expected reduction in hosted spillback ~42%. Need more realistic token-length distribution.

2025-02-25 - Mateo Ruiz: Implemented basic credit ledger prototype in the routing-fn service. Ledger persisted to /kv/tenant-credits; unit tests added for credit decay and recharge. PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/platform/pull/5312

2025-02-28 - SRE (Priya Nair): Observability hooks reviewed. Added backfill_event_count to metrics and an alert if any tenant consumes >50% of backfill budget within 1h. Roadblock: current metrics cardinality needs aggregation to avoid cost blow-up.
2025-03-01 - Design (Liam O'Connor): UX for tenant console: added a visual credit meter and backfill budget control in the dedicated settings page. Will follow up with copy for alerts.

2025-03-03 - Mateo Ruiz: Canary rollout started for 'alpha-tenant-3' and internal 'redwood-dev'. No regressions observed in p50/p95 latencies. Variant shadowing pipeline observed a small increase in infra egress; optimizing serialization next.

source.title: Tenant embedding shift detector for zero-day workload changes
source.creator: Arielle Chen
source.description: Build an online detection service that represents tenant-level usage/cost/latency time-series as compact embeddings and surfaces zero-day workload shifts (novel cluster formation or re-assignment) with probabilistic alert scoring and noise-aware suppression. Goal: catch new behavioral regimes (engineered model changes, synthetic traffic, customer load pattern shifts) faster than threshold-based rules while keeping false positive rate low for on-call and product owners.
source.background: Current console guards and anomaly pipelines rely on rule thresholds and single-metric changepoints. These miss cross-metric shifts (e.g., small latency increase + model token pattern change causing cost shift) and generate noisy alerts for seasonal tenants. We propose a tenant embedding approach (sliding-window feature vectors -> dimensionality reduction -> streaming cluster model) to detect novel regimes and abrupt assignment changes as early indicators of incidents or misconfigurations.
design_decisions: Use 24h sliding windows sampled at 5m intervals to capture intraday patterns without excessive compute
Compute per-tenant feature vector composed of normalized token_rate, avg_latency_p50/p95, cost_rate, embedding_usage, model_variant_ratio, error_rate and recent prefix-KV cache hit ratio
Project features with incremental PCA to 16 dims for memory and explainability rather than black-box neural embeddings
Adopt an online clustering algorithm (streaming HDBSCAN-lite adaptation) that supports cluster birth/death and per-tenant cluster-probability scores
Alert when tenant cluster-probability drops below 0.15 (novel assignment) OR when tenant switches clusters and magnitude-of-shift score > adaptive threshold
Compute probabilistic alert score that combines novelty magnitude, customer exposure (billing velocity), and recent alert fatigue suppression state
Keep raw tenant identifiers private in embeddings (hash + salt) and only surface tenant id in console for authorized users to meet privacy controls
Run model and scoring in a separate autoscaled service behind the observability VPC; persist embedding history for 14 days for triage and roll-forward analysis
tradeoffs: Higher-frequency windows increase sensitivity but raise CPU/GPU costs — we selected 5m to balance sensitivity and resource usage
Dimensionality reduction preserves interpretability (we can map back to feature loadings) but may miss some nonlinear patterns that deep embeddings could capture
Online clustering reduces alert latency but is harder to tune and explain than offline batch models
Keeping a short history (14 days) reduces storage cost but limits long-term seasonality detection — retention can be increased later if justified
acceptance_criteria: End-to-end pipeline: metrics -> embeddings -> incremental PCA -> cluster model -> alert stream (kafka) -> console UI flagging
Baseline detection recall >= 75% on curated evaluation set of historical incidents (cost, latency, usage change) and precision >= 60% under default suppression settings
Alert volume for top-500 tenants does not exceed 4 actionable alerts/day after suppression during steady-state week
Integration tests that replay synthetic workload shifts produce expected novel cluster alerts and include runbook links
PagerDuty integration for P1 customers and console banner for P2; RBAC-protected tenant links in console
source.runbook: If alert shows novel cluster: 1) open tenant timeline in console (link included), 2) check recent deploys and model variant ratios, 3) validate KV cache hit rate and upstream errors, 4) if cost spike and no active customer work, escalate to platform-sre
source.rollout_plan: Phase 1: internal canary across staging tenants (2 weeks). Phase 2: opt-in beta for 50 enterprise tenants with email alerts (4 weeks). Phase 3: default for all tenants with suppressed noise filters (8 weeks). Backout: disable cluster scoring and fall back to existing rule engine via feature-flag.
notes: Engineering review 2025-02-20: agreed on PCA+streaming cluster hybrid; action: prototype scoring lambda (Mateo)
Security review kickoff 2025-02-25: encryption-at-rest for embedding store required; KMS key rotation plan added
PM feedback 2025-02-28: add tenant opt-out and email digest instead of immediate pager for non-critical customers
Design ask 2025-03-01: add console visualization mockups showing cluster map and feature contribution arrows

source.title: Endpoint behavioral SLI spec and graceful capacity-shedding guidelines
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Create a pragmatic, behavioral SLI specification for model endpoints that maps to observable request behaviors (short vs long context, streaming vs non-streaming) and define a multi-tiered capacity-shedding policy that preserves high-priority traffic using graceful degradation, model fallback, regional evacuation, and circuit breakers. The aim is to give on-call and platform automation a clear decision tree to protect error budgets while minimizing customer-visible regressions.
source.background: We currently have per-endpoint latency and availability SLOs defined at a coarse level, but incidents in Q4 and early 2025 revealed gaps: (1) sudden tail-latency spikes for long-context streaming routes, (2) tenant noise causing disproportionate error-budget burn, and (3) unclear automation boundaries between autoscaler, circuit breakers, and regional failover. This ticket captures a refined SLI taxonomy and a staged shedding policy to address those failure modes.
acceptance_criteria: Define at least three behavioral SLIs per endpoint class (e.g., short-text sync, long-streaming chat, embedding batch)
Publish SLO targets and their error-budget windows (1h burn, 24h burn, 7d burn) and corresponding automated responses
Document circuit breaker thresholds and actions (metric, window, and action) with clear escalation paths
Provide a runbook for automated and manual multi-region evacuation including play-by-play steps and required dashboards/PRs
Add telemetry dashboards for new SLIs and verification checks to the rollout gating pipeline
proposed_spec: Endpoint classes: short-sync (<=128 tokens), long-sync (129-2048 tokens), streaming-chat (stateful, incremental tokens), embeddings (batch/real-time)

SLIs (examples): availability_success_rate = successful responses (2xx) / total requests; latency_p50/p95/p99 measured for server-side completion time (exclude client-side backpressure); correctness_sli = model-level rerank/quality threshold pass rate (linked to eval promptset)

Behavioral SLIs: short-sync_tail = p99 latency for requests with <=128 tokens; streaming_consistency = fraction of streaming sessions with no mid-stream truncation or gap >2s; embedding_throughput = median vectors/sec per allocation

SLO targets: short-sync availability 99.95% monthly; p95 latency <120ms for short-sync; streaming_consistency 99.5% monthly; embedding throughput 95th percentile >= baseline for reserved capacity customers
Error budget windows: realtime short-burn windows = 1h (fast reactions), sustained burn = 24h (escalation), long-term debit = 7d (rollback/rollout gating)

Circuit breaker rules (automated): 1) If availability_success_rate < 98% over 5m AND burn rate >3x expected, trigger soft shed (phase A). 2) If p99 latency breaches target by >3x for 10m, mark as latency incident and enter phase B. 3) If burn persists and customer-impacting endpoints exceed threshold, initiate regional failover and reserve capacity allocation. Each rule includes a cooldown and manual override policy.

Graceful shedding phases: Phase A (soft): reduce per-tenant concurrency on non-critical routes, enable prefix/KV cache bypass for cached responses, switch eligible requests to non-streaming summarized variant; Phase B (fallback): route to quantized/cheaper model variant with documented quality delta; Phase C (regional): re-route traffic to secondary region with reserved capacity and enable degraded features (no realtime audit logs for non-enterprise customers if necessary).

Failover policy: maintain a 10% warm reserve in each region and a tagged set of golden model variants that are smaller/quantized and backwards-compatible for fallback; failover orchestration must include traffic-slicing percentages and a canary window (30m) before full cutover.

Operator/automation boundaries: autoscaler handles capacity adds until warm reserve exhausted; circuit-breaker controller triggers shedding actions; regional-orchestrator executes traffic migration and notifies on-call; all automation must include a human-confirm step for phase C unless preapproved by on-call.
source.rollout_plan: 1) Finalize spec with PM/Design/Runtime (1 week). 2) Implement telemetry for behavioral SLIs and add dashboards (2 sprints). 3) Implement circuit-breaker controller changes and shedding actions behind a feature flag (1 sprint). 4) Run simulated burn tests using traffic replay and chaos tests in staging (2 weeks). 5) Gradual rollout to hosted customers with a canary cohort, monitor error budget and quality regressions, then open for Dedicated/Private with config knobs.
monitoring_and_alerting: Dashboards: per-endpoint SLI dashboard (p50/p95/p99 + availability) with burn-rate overlays
Alerts: SEV3 when 1h burn rate >2x; SEV2 when 1h burn rate >3x AND availability <98%; SEV1 on regional evacuation start
Automated annotations for incidents linking to relevant PRs, eval promptsets, and impacted customer list
runbook: Phase A auto-actions (no human): lower concurrency for low-priority keys; enable cached-response-first policy; tighten batch windows to reduce tail latency
Phase A operator steps: review traffic heatmap, confirm fallback candidates, monitor quality regression dashboard for 15m
Phase B auto-actions: switch eligible routes to quantized model variant; enable partial response summarization for very long contexts
Phase B operator steps: notify customer-success for enterprise customers, triage linked GitHub PRs that change serving configs
Phase C (regional): paged on-call (SEV1) evaluates metrics; if directed, initiate regional-orchestrator failover (30% -> 60% -> 100% slices) and open post-mortem template
Rollback: automated rollback triggers if new region p95 latency >2x baseline within canary window or correctness_sli drops >5% absolute
Post-incident: capture burn-rate timeline, model-variant quality deltas, and any customer-visible degradations in incident notes
dependencies: ENG-62401 (per-model composite SLI) - telemetry hooks and SLI naming conventions
Platform PR: autoscaler expose reserve metrics and per-tenant throttles
Console UX: dashboard tile for behavioral SLI visualization
comments: 2025-02-16 - Ava Chen: Drafted initial taxonomy after reviewing recent incidents. Linked experiment logs: https://drive.redwood.ai/incident-q4-burnrate
2025-02-20 - Priya Singh (PM): Requested explicit customer communication templates for Phase B fallback to quantized models
2025-02-24 - Diego Morales: Suggested adding streaming_consistency metric and expanded canary timing; updated spec accordingly
2025-02-27 - Design review (Liam O'Connor): Dashboard mockups look good; requested a compact view for on-call
2025-03-01 - Security (Marta Ruiz): Confirmed no additional KMS changes needed for this ticket
2025-03-02 - Diego Morales: Marking spec ready for implementation; will split automation tasks into follow-up child tickets

source.title: Archiveable quant bundles with immutable hash index and safety pilots
source.creator: Priya Natarajan
source.description: Create an archiveable artifact format and operational flow for quantization outputs (FP8/INT8 calibration profiles, factor tables, per-layer statistics) that are: (1) content-addressable and signed; (2) discoverable via an immutable hash index; and (3) safe to rehydrate into running runtimes with automated pre-apply validation checks. Goal is to enable reproducible rollbacks and reproducible cross-region reruns of calibration pipelines while minimizing storage growth and avoiding silent accuracy regressions.
acceptance_criteria: Define a compact bundle format (.qbundle) that contains calibration profile, sampling metadata, seed info, environment fingerprint (toolchain + runtime versions), and a human-readable accuracy summary.
Implement content-addressable SHA-256 signing for bundles and an index service that maps signed-hash -> canonical bundle metadata.

Provide a 'dry-run rehydrate' CLI that can simulate applying a bundle to runtime instances and run calibrated micro-evals (10-20 sample prompts) to detect accuracy deviation beyond configured thresholds.
Add automated guards in the rollout pipeline: block canaries if micro-eval accuracy delta > 1.5% absolute or if sampling coverage mismatches expected distribution.
Document operational playbook for rollback: how to fetch prior canonical bundle, run dry-run, execute revert, and update audit log entries.
implementation_plan: Design qbundle format spec (artifact manifest, profile blobs, provenance metadata, optional calibration logs). -- owner: Marco (2d)
Prototype bundle creation in calibration pipeline (calib-worker uploads signed bundle to index service). -- owner: Calibration team (4d)
Index service: lightweight immutable mapping with append-only ledger (append-only S3 prefix + DynamoDB index). Provide API: lookup(hash), list-by-model-version, sign/verify. -- owner: Infra (6d)
Dry-run CLI: local mode that replays KV cache primitives and runs micro-evals against a snapshot runtime. Includes accuracy comparator and exit codes for gating. -- owner: Runtime (5d)
CI integration: add a pipeline step to protected branch merges that verifies bundles referenced in runtime-operator manifests and rejects missing or unverifiable bundles. -- owner: CICD (3d)
Operator console: add ability to view bundle metadata, provenance chain, and initiate replay/rollback from UI. -- owner: Console (5d)
End-to-end safety pilots: run on two customer sandboxes and internal benchmarks; capture false-positive/false-negative stats for micro-evals. -- owner: QA/SRE (6d)
validations: Micro-eval harness: 200 curated prompts per model-family in CI for regression detection; default gating threshold 1.5% absolute token-level logp shift or 0.8% generation accuracy loss (depending on metric).
Determinism checks: verify that bundle rehydration with identical seeds and runtime versions reproduces calibration statistics to within floating-point tolerance (narrow epsilon).
Sanity checks: ensure sample distribution recorded in bundle (tokens per sequence, seq length histogram) matches live traffic within configurable K-L divergence threshold.
rollout_plan: Phase 1 (internal): Implement qbundle creation and index service; run 5 internal models through full create->dry-run->rehydrate loop. Collect latency, storage overhead, and micro-eval delta. (2 weeks)
Phase 2 (safety pilot): Enable for two opt-in customers (non-production sandboxes). Monitor canary rehydrations and capture automated revert metrics. (1 month)
Phase 3 (gradual): Expose in Dedicated clusters with operator flags; default off for Hosted. Enable console actions for rollback in 10% of orgs. (6 weeks)
Phase 4 (default): Require signed bundles for model-versioned rollouts and use index verification as part of runtime startup checks. (release runtime-1.22)
Backout: If micro-eval false negative rate > 3% in pilot phase, pause rollout and widen micro-eval corpus.
risks: Storage blowup: naive bundling could duplicate large artifacts across versions. Mitigation: delta storage and content-addressable dedupe (store blobs by hash).
False confidence from micro-evals: small micro-eval sets can miss rare but customer-visible failures. Mitigation: pilot with customers and expand corpus iteratively and add sampling hooks.
Operational complexity: index service availability becomes critical. Mitigation: local fallback to signed bundle blobs in object store and cached index replicas.
Security: signed bundles must integrate with KMS/HSM to prevent tampering. Mitigation: require envelope signatures and integrate with platform KMS.
source.notes: This ticket takes a systems-first approach: design for small, verifiable bundles that are cheap to rehydrate for quick safety checks. We intentionally avoid a heavy stateful ledger; prefer append-only object store + small index to stay operationally simple. Early design doc: https://redwood.atlassian.net/l/c/SGq9XwVb (private). See calibration RFC: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1-calib-RFC. Related infra PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/1428
comments: 2025-02-12 Priya Natarajan: Initial ticket created. Goal is to avoid repeat work from earlier artifact playbooks and make rehydration cheap.
2025-02-14 Marco Alvarez: Scoped qbundle spec draft attached to PR; needs feedback on what to include for runtime-fingerprint. Posting link: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/1428
2025-02-17 Elena K: Security review requested — will need KMS signing flow and threat model. Blocking until KMS design is accepted. Assigned to Sec team.
2025-02-20 Sec Team (note): Proposed HSM signing using AWS KMS multi-region keys; threat model doc added to drive.
2025-02-23 QA (Jon S): Ran initial micro-eval harness on two internal models. Observed 0.3% average delta but one outlier at 2.1% on long-form summarization test. Investigating sampling mismatch.
2025-02-26 Marco Alvarez: Changed dry-run to include sequence-length histogram check; reduces false positives for long-form workloads. Updated acceptance criteria.
2025-03-01 Priya Natarajan: Pilot sign-up email sent to two customer teams; awaiting sandbox credentials. Added retention policy note: keep last 10 bundles per model-major-version by default.

title: Archiveable quant bundles with immutable hash index and safety pilots
preview: Create an archiveable artifact format and operational flow for quantization outputs (FP8/INT8 calibration profiles, factor tables, per-layer statistics) that are: (1) content-addressable and signed; (2) discoverable via an immutable hash index; and (3) safe to rehydrate into running runtimes with automated pre-apply validation checks. Goal is to enable reproducible rollbacks and reproducible cross-region reruns of calibration pipelines while minimizing storage growth and avoiding silent accuracy regressions.
text: Create an archiveable artifact format and operational flow for quantization outputs (FP8/INT8 calibration profiles, factor tables, per-layer statistics) that are: (1) content-addressable and signed; (2) discoverable via an immutable hash index; and (3) safe to rehydrate into running runtimes with automated pre-apply validation checks. Goal is to enable reproducible rollbacks and reproducible cross-region reruns of calibration pipelines while minimizing storage growth and avoiding silent accuracy regressions.

source.title: Tenant-aware audit data lifecycle and cost-QoS for RBAC v2 events
source.creator: Priya Menon
source.description:
Background:
Redwood needs predictable cost and query performance for audit events generated by RBAC v2 and SSO/SAML admin actions. Tenant activity patterns vary widely: some tenants generate millions of admin-audit events per day, others generate only a few. Current pipeline writes all events to a single hot index topology which drives up storage and query costs for heavy tenants and increases noise for investigations.

Goals:
- Ensure high-value, security-relevant audit events (RBAC changes, SSO admin actions, KMS operations) are retained and indexed for fast query for the configured retention window.
- Provide tenant-level QoS that limits ingestion cost for high-volume tenants while guaranteeing retention/availability for tenants with compliance needs.
- Introduce intelligent partitioning and coldstore spill for older data to reduce per-tenant storage costs.
- Surface clear cost guardrails and automatic throttles to prevent noisy tenants from impacting cluster performance.

Scope (what this ticket delivers):
1) Ingestion API change: add event priority field (system|admin|audit|debug) and tenant-cost-profile tag so producers can mark critical RBAC/SSO events. Backfill mapper to enrich existing events based on action_type and actor_role.
2) Dynamic partitioning: route events into per-tenant or tenant-group indices depending on monthly event volume thresholds. Small tenants go to shared partitioned indices; large/high-compliance tenants get dedicated indices with stronger indexing and retention.
3) Hot/cold lifecycle: implement TTL-driven rollover into coldstore with compacted index representation (column-pruned) after 30/90/365 days configurable per-tenant. Coldstore objects use compressed append-only blobs to lower storage cost.
4) Cost QoS controller: quota and burst tokens per tenant with automatic backpressure; admin escalation flow for tenants that need more capacity.
5) Query indexing strategy: selective primary indexing for security fields (actor_id, action, resource, outcome) and optional full-text for tenants that opt-in to richer search.
6) RBAC v2 integration: ensure index access respects tenant-level RBAC; SSO admin events are only visible to users with audit_read permission.
7) Observability and alerts: dashboards for per-tenant ingestion rate, storage spend, index latency, and throttling incidents.
8) Migration plan: phased cutover with canary tenants, backfill job to re-index critical historical events for tenants in dedicated indices.

Design notes:
- Use tenant fingerprint (tenant_id % N) to place small-tenant shards into stable partitions to avoid shard explosion.
- Dedicated indices for high-volume tenants should be autoscalable and pinned to dedicated node pools (Dedicated capacity or private).
- Cost QoS controller runs in the ingest path and emits trace/span with reason code when throttling is applied.
- Retention policies are owner-configurable but constrained by organization-wide max retention in compliance mode.

Acceptance criteria:
- Ingest path accepts and persists event priority and tenant-cost-profile metadata.
- Tenants above volume threshold are routed to dedicated index with correct mapping.
- Coldstore rollover reduces hot index storage for 90-day dataset by >=40% in benchmark test.
- Query SLA: admin/audit queries for recent 7 days return within 200ms P95 for dedicated-index tenants under normal load.
- Throttling events emit audit entries and are visible in console alerts.
- RBAC enforcement validated: tenant_restricted indexes are unreadable by users lacking audit_read.

Rollout plan:
- Week 1: schema change and feature flag behind "tenant-qos-audit". Unit/integration tests.
- Week 2: deploy ingest QoS controller to staging, enable for 5 non-prod tenants.
- Week 3: canary enable for 3 low-risk prod tenants; exercise backfill for last 90 days.
- Week 4: enable for top 10 heavy tenants with manual monitoring and ops on-call.
- Post-rollout: 30-day review and iterate on thresholds and cost models.

Monitoring & metrics to add:
- per_tenant_ingest_rate, per_tenant_storage_cost_estimate, throttles_by_reason, index_rollover_latency, coldstore_spill_rate, backfill_progress

Testing plan:
- Load test with synthetic tenants (1x, 10x, 100x loads) to validate partitioning and throttling behavior.
- Verify RBAC access controls by scripted user roles and SSO flows.
- Cost analysis using billing-sim harness to model monthly spend with and without coldstore.

Migration/cleanup:
- Backfill job runs in low-traffic windows and marks completed segments. Old unified indices flagged read-only after backfill and then compacted for long-term archive retention.

Notes on security_review_required: this change introduces schema and access controls touching RBAC and SSO events — schedule SecReview with compliance and cloud security teams before canary.
comments: 2025-02-11 - Priya Menon: Initial draft design uploaded to Confluence. Requesting security review and input from infra on shard sizing.
2025-02-13 - Ethan Park: Scoped implementation for ingest QoS controller. Prototype uses token-bucket per-tenant; need feedback on shard routing strategy.
2025-02-17 - Lina Gomez (Security): Security review scheduled. Raised concern about enrichment backfill leaking PII if not scrubbed; added requirement to run scrubber during reindex.
2025-02-20 - Marcus Lee (Infra): Proposed using 64 partitions for shared index ring to balance shard count and query fanout. Running sim to validate.
2025-02-25 - Ethan Park: Implemented feature-flagged ingest changes in staging; observed ~45% reduction in hot index size for 90-day synthetic workload. Preparing canary plan.
2025-02-27 - Priya Menon: Added acceptance criteria for RBAC enforcement and query SLA. Confirmed SecReview kickoff next week.

source.title: staged-activation-and-lexicon-sync-pipeline-cleanup
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Consolidate and simplify the model asset onboarding path to reduce cold-start tail latency and make weight/shard format handling deterministic. This ticket implements a staged activation flow: probe manifests/metadata, reconcile formats, pre-allocate and coalesce shards when possible, and perform a lightweight lexicon sync (vocab warmup + mapping verification) before full model activation. Replace the current ad-hoc format detectors and multiple tokenizer init paths with a single pluggable lexicon initializer that exposes a stable contract for downstream runtime. Also bump the weight-reading dependency (weight-probe-lib) to the latest patch that includes async checksum validation and streaming headers support.
acceptance_criteria: Cold start 95th percentile model-load latency reduced by >= 25% for 8B-class models in workload X (measured on staging).
Staged activation implemented: probe->reconcile->shard-coalesce->lexicon-sync->activate documented and gated by feature flag 'staged_activation_v2'.
Single lexicon initializer used by all ingest code paths; tokenizer init duplication removed from the model-agent.
Weight format detectors consolidated into a single manifest-prober module with unit tests covering all known formats (hf, safetensors, custom-shard-index).
No regressions in GPU memory peak during load (baseline within +/- 5%).
Perf regression and compatibility test matrix added to CI; PRs must pass matrix for 'staged_activation_v2' on at least one GPU profile.
implementation_steps: Add feature flag 'staged_activation_v2' and runtime guard rails (config + metrics).
Extract existing format detection heuristics into sources/model/manifest_prober with documented API and tests.
Implement shard coalescer that attempts to merge small shards or stream them in a prioritized order based on manifest hints.
Create pluggable lexicon_initializer interface and migrate existing tokenizer warmup paths to call through it.
Integrate async checksum validation from weight-probe-lib v1.6.3 and update error handling semantics.
Add end-to-end staging tests that simulate mixed-format bundles and verify activation sequence and fallbacks.
Run perf sweep (latency, memory, throughput) across 4B/8B/13B models and iterate on ordering heuristics.
Roll out behind feature flag with canary traffic and rollout policy (10%->50%->100%) and monitor regressions.
risks_and_mitigations: Risk: New manifest_prober may mis-detect custom vendor bundles. Mitigation: keep legacy detector as fallback behind a runtime switch and log mismatch metrics for 2 weeks.
Risk: Lexicon init serializes on a hot path causing latency spike. Mitigation: make warmup non-blocking and expose KV-prefetch timeout; fall back to lazy tokenization with telemetry.
Risk: Bumping weight-probe-lib introduces API changes. Mitigation: add a compatibility shim and run integration tests against existing checkpoint corpus.
Risk: Shard coalescer increases memory overhead. Mitigation: enforce memory budget and prefer streaming coalescing strategy when budget constrained.
blockers: Await weight-probe-lib v1.6.3 release and signed artifact (security team clearance).
Need perf baseline dashboard update (infra ticket INF-4123) to capture new staging metrics format.
comments: 2025-02-14 Ava Chen: Created ticket after spike showing 35% 95p tail on cold model loads for mixed-shard bundles. Proposal includes staged activation and a unified lexicon path.
2025-02-16 Diego Martinez: Prototype for manifest_prober ready; finds format using header heuristics. Initial microbench shows no CPU regression.
2025-02-18 Design review (Priya K) feedback: ensure lexicon sync is idempotent and provide clear failure telemetry. Added idempotency note to impl plan.
2025-02-20 Security (Omar) requested signed artifact for weight-probe-lib v1.6.3 before upgrade. Reached out to infra for verification steps.
2025-02-24 Diego Martinez: Integrated async checksum validation. Observed 12% improvement in 95p load time on 8B model when streaming headers are used. Draft PR opened.
2025-02-27 QA (Zara): End-to-end test failed on a vendor bundle with non-standard shard order; enabled legacy fallback and added a failing test case to the matrix.

source.title: Incident retro & action plan for RBACv2 audit-log regression and SAML session anomalies
source.creator: Priya Natarajan
source.description: Summary: On 2025-02-15 we observed a ~45 minute window where RBAC v2 change events were not consistently emitted to the central audit stream and several SAML session refresh events were dropped by the audit pipeline. Symptoms included missing admin role-change entries in the audit dashboard, delayed session invalidation notices, and a spike in console-facing 403s for recently promoted admin users. This ticket captures the RCA, mitigations applied, follow-up engineering work, and verification plan for restoring audit SLOs and hardening admin controls.
action_items: Hotfix audit pipeline to ensure RBACv2 change events are always enqueued: PR #7824 merged; verify backfill completed by 2025-02-20
Add end-to-end SAML session telemetry: instrument SAML assertion handling with unique trace IDs and expose to tracing dashboard (owner: Diego Martinez, due 2025-03-07)
Improve alerting: create a dedicated SLO/alert for audit event loss rate >0.1% over 5m (owner: Alex Chen, due 2025-02-28)
Add a safeguard in admin-role promotion flow to synchronously persist critical audit entry before returning 200 (owner: Priya Natarajan, due 2025-03-04)
Backfill missing audit entries from temporary buffering store and tag reconciled entries with 'reconciled=true' (owner: Olivia Park, due 2025-02-25)
Run tabletop incident simulation for RBACv2 and SAML path once fixes are deployed (owner: SRE team, due 2025-03-14)
Document follow-up SLO target: audit-event delivery 99.99% per minute, median latency <250ms; include in SLA review (owner: Product Security, due 2025-03-01)
acceptance_criteria: Audit event delivery SLO defined and accepted by Product Security
New alert fires in staging and production when audit loss >0.1% over a 5m window
Backfilled entries validated against DB of role-change operations with reconciliation report
SAML session traces show assertion IDs propagated through audit pipeline end-to-end
Tabletop simulation run with checklist and any remaining gaps converted to tickets
comments: 2025-02-15 — 09:12 UTC Priya Natarajan: Pager triggered for spike in missing audit records. Initial triage: audit producer error rate increased after a config rollout that touched RBAC v2 serializers.
2025-02-15 — 09:47 UTC Alex Chen: Rolled back RBAC v2 serializer change (config id cfg-rt-441). That stopped new regressions but historical gaps remained. Started backfill plan.

2025-02-15 — 10:20 UTC Diego Martinez: Found SAML session refresh path drops when audit batching buffer reached 1000 events; batching logic silently dropped oldest entries. Patch added bounds-check and drop-metric emission (PR drafted).

2025-02-16 — 02:03 UTC Olivia Park: Backfill job completed for 98% of missing role-change events; remaining 2% require manual reconciliation with customer-facing visibles. Noted customer_impact=medium, customer outreach queued.

2025-02-17 — 11:15 UTC Security Review (Laura Kim): Recommend synchronous write for critical admin promotions and an opt-in strict-audit mode for enterprise tenants. Security review required before enabling strict mode.
2025-02-20 — 16:40 UTC Alex Chen: Alerting changes merged to staging; created grafana panel to monitor 'audit_pipeline_dropped_events_total' and PagerDuty rule for >0.1% loss over 5m.

2025-02-24 — 09:00 UTC Priya Natarajan: Draft retro completed. Seeking approvals on acceptance criteria and timelines. Noted trade-offs: synchronous audit writes increase latency for admin promotions by ~30ms but greatly reduce undetected changes — acceptable for P1 admin flows.
2025-02-25 — 15:22 UTC Diego Martinez: Added instrumentation to SAML handlers; traced a sample assertion end-to-end; pushing to prod canary tomorrow.
2025-02-25 — 17:05 UTC Alex Chen: Marking primary action items as 'In Progress'. Will update when alerting verifies in production Canary cluster.
