source.title: KV-prefix: hit-rate sensitivity curve and tail-latency memory tradeoff benchmark
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Goal: produce a robust, reproducible benchmark that maps KV/prefix cache hit-rate to p95 token latency and memory footprint across representative production-like workloads. Unlike previous load harnesses that focused on end-to-end throughput at a fixed cache size, this work systematically sweeps cache size, prefix retention policy, and request temporal locality to generate hit-rate sensitivity curves and identify knee points where marginal memory increases stop yielding meaningful latency improvements. The harness must output tokens/sec, p50/p95/p99 (in ms and ms/token), memory at steady-state, eviction churn, and a lightweight regression signal for CI.
source.acceptance_criteria: 1) End-to-end harness that can run in CI and on a 4-GPU perf cluster producing identical CSV/JSON artifacts. 2) Sweep matrix covers: cache sizes (32MB, 128MB, 512MB, 2GB), prefix retention windows (1h, 6h, 24h), synthetic locality patterns (zipf alpha=0.6/0.9, bursty session-based, long-tail cold starts) and a sampled real-traffic trace. 3) Visual artifacts: hit-rate vs p95 curve, heatmap of p95 across (cache size, prefix window), and memory vs hit-rate curves. 4) Regression detector: EWMA + control-chart alerts and baseline diff in PRs. 5) Documented methodology and runbook in the perf wiki.
test_plan_steps: Implement synthetic workload generator that supports time-windowed prefixes and session affinity
Instrument runtime to emit per-request: tokens, latency(ms), cache_hit boolean, memory_rss at 5s intervals
Create sweep driver that parametrizes cache_size, retention_window, request_distribution and duration
Add baseline control run (no-cache) and 3 repeated seeds to compute variance
Integrate offline analysis that computes hit-rate CDF, pxx per-bucket, and knee detection heuristic
Hook regression detector to CI which compares PR run vs baseline via EWMA and Mann-Kendall trend test, failing on significant regressions
source.implementation_notes: Decisions: 1) Use a lightweight in-process generator to avoid external QPS bottlenecks; throttle generator to simulate realistic inter-arrival times. 2) Measure ms/token by dividing request latency by tokens emitted (special-case streaming edge-cases). 3) To control noise, pin CPU freq governors on perf cluster; run warming phase (10 minutes) to reach steady-state. 4) Persist raw traces to s3://perf-artifacts/kv-prefix/ENG-23140 for reproducibility. 5) Implement knee detection by computing second derivative of hit-rate vs memory curve and looking for flattening with configurable threshold.
updates: 2025-02-12: Kickoff - scoped matrix and success criteria during triage with runtime and infra leads.
2025-02-14: Prototype synthetic generator completed. Supports zipf and bursty session models. Initial smoke runs show plausible hit-rate variance across seeds.
2025-02-16: Observed high p95 noise when NVMe checkpointing coincided with runs. Added control to schedule checkpoint outside run window; re-runs reduced variance by ~18%.
2025-02-18: Integrated per-request memory RSS sampling. Found eviction churn metric correlates strongly with p95 spikes for very small cache sizes.
2025-02-20: First full sweep executed on 4x A40 cluster (2-hour runs). Artifacts uploaded to s3 and initial hit-rate vs p95 plots landed in the perf wiki.

2025-02-22: Implemented EWMA-based regression detector and baseline comparison script. Toyed with Mann-Kendall for trend detection; results promising but noisy for short runs—keeping as optional secondary check.

2025-02-24: Design review with PM and Observability team. Feedback: include tokens/sec heatmaps and a normalized p95/ms-per-token metric to compare across sequence lengths. Added to acceptance criteria.
2025-02-25: PR opened for harness code and analysis notebooks (see links). Next: integrate with CI failing criteria and perform a stability run across 6 seeds for the baseline.
found_issues: Initial runs exposed a pattern where small caches produced frequent small GC-like stalls due to eviction bookkeeping; root cause tracked to per-eviction lock contention in the prefix map.
Disk IO (checkpoints) and Node-level noise were contaminating p95; infra adjusted maintenance windows and more aggressive isolation for perf runs.
next_steps: Finalize CI gating thresholds and add the harness to the nightly perf job (nightly-perf-benchmark).
Work with runtime team on mitigation for eviction lock contention (ENG-23198 reference) and re-run heatmap post-fix.
Add sampled real-traffic trace from canary for one customer to validate synthetic models and add customer-facing note if behavior differs.
Ship wiki runbook with TL;DR charts and a short PR template for performance changes that must include harness outputs.

title: KV-prefix: hit-rate sensitivity curve and tail-latency memory tradeoff benchmark
preview: Goal: produce a robust, reproducible benchmark that maps KV/prefix cache hit-rate to p95 token latency and memory footprint across representative production-like workloads. Unlike previous load harnesses that focused on end-to-end throughput at a fixed cache size, this work systematically sweeps cache size, prefix retention policy, and request temporal locality to generate hit-rate sensitivity curves and identify knee points where marginal memory increases stop yielding meaningful latency improvements. The harness must output tokens/sec, p50/p95/p99 (in ms and ms/token), memory at steady-state, eviction churn, and a lightweight regression signal for CI.

source.title: Signal fencing and delegated ingest proxy for dedicated / on-prem observability
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem: Dedicated and on-prem customers run remote collectors and forwarders into Redwood’s observability pipeline. Current designs either (a) place heavy responsibility on the customer-side agent to enforce tenant isolation, or (b) accept mixed signals at the perimeter and rely on downstream filters. Both approaches increase blast radius for misconfiguration, leak risk for cross-tenant telemetry, and operational complexity for private deployments.

Goal: Build a delegated ingest proxy — a lightweight, policy-enforcing ingress layer that performs 'signal fencing': validate and bind incoming traces/metrics/logs to tenant-scoped identities, apply tenant-specific sampling & enrichment, and steer signals into isolated pipeline partitions. The proxy must work in VPC connectors and on-prem collectors, support zero-trust transport (mutual TLS), integrate with KMS for per-tenant encryption keys, and expose metrics and traces for its own SLO monitoring.

Scope and features: 
- Auth & identity binding: accept mTLS/client certs + signed tokens from customer sidecar to assert tenant identity; fall back to signed SAML/JWT where certs aren't available.
- Signal fencing ruleset: enforce allowed sources (IP ranges, cert CNs), mapping rules from incoming attributes to internal tenant IDs, and reject/flag ambiguous requests.
- Delegated sampling & enrichment: apply tenant-configured sampling policies (error-priority presampling, head-based retain-on-error) and enrichment (resource tags normalization, redact PII using configured rules).
- Routing & isolation: pin streams to tenant-specific ingest lanes (logical partitions) and optionally to dedicated Kafka topics/storage buckets for Dedicated customers. Support transparent backpressure and graceful degradation to low-cost backup lanes.
- Observability & transparency: expose per-tenant ingress metrics (ingest_rps, drop_rate, sampling_ratio), internal traces for debugging, and structured audit log entries for each admitted/ rejected batch.
- Data residency & encryption: optional per-tenant KMS key selection, envelope encryption for persisted batches, and retention enforcement in the proxy where required for air-gapped on-prem setups.
- Compatibility: OpenTelemetry protocol support (OTLP/gRPC, OTLP/HTTP), Fluent Forwarder, and a small plugin for legacy syslog-based collectors.

Non-goals: full in-proxy analytics or long-term retention; heavy schema transformations beyond tag normalization. The proxy should be lightweight and deterministic.

Design trade-offs considered: 
- Inline vs async validation: we favor inline validation with small batching to ensure early filtering of bad signals. This adds minimal latency (~2-8ms) but prevents downstream leakage.
- Per-tenant dedicated storage vs logical partitioning: to reduce operational overhead for mid-market customers we'll default to logical lanes; allow dedicated physical partitions as a paid option.
- Rich policy language vs simple RBAC: start with an expressive but bounded rule set (allow/deny, sample-rates, enrichments) and plan a policy UI in Console later.

Acceptance criteria: 
- Proxy can successfully authenticate and bind identity for >95% of test collectors in internal fleet.
- End-to-end latency increase <=10ms p50 vs baseline for OTLP/gRPC small batches.
- No cross-tenant telemetry in blue/green canary runs (proof via fuzz tests and synthetic traffic).
- Audit logs show per-batch tenant mapping and rejection reason when applicable.

Implementation plan: 
1) RFC + API sketch and policy JSON schema (Confluence doc).
2) PoC: Go binary supporting OTLP/gRPC with mTLS auth + simple rules engine.
3) Integrate KMS selection & envelope encryption MVP.
4) Add enrichments + sampling engine and per-tenant metrics.
5) End-to-end tests, chaos tests (certificate rotation, partial network partitions).
6) Gradual rollout: internal -> Dedicated customers (canary) -> Private/VPC customers.

Rollout strategy and safety: canary traffic limited to 1% of Dedicated ingress for first two weeks, automated rollback on elevated drop_rate or >1% increase in latency p95. Enable audit-mode (log-only) behind a feature flag for first Private customers to validate mapping rules without rejecting signals.

Monitoring and SLOs: proxy exposes metrics and traces to Redwood's internal monitoring. SLOs: availability 99.95% for ingress path, p95 latency target <=50ms, error rate <0.5% for authenticated traffic. Dashboards: new per-tenant ingestion dashboard, latency/error heatmap, and a cross-tenant leakage detection dashboard.

Testing & QA: unit tests for rules engine, integration tests with OTLP exporters, synthetic cross-tenant fuzz generator, and live canary harness. Performance test plan includes 100k RPS capacity test in a Dedicated-like environment and verifying resource footprint.

Dependencies: ENG-59901 (observability-proxy-splitter-for-vpc-batching) for shared backlog handling primitives, infra-keys team for KMS provisioning, and Console team for future policy UI.

Deliverables: Go proxy repo with API docs, Confluence design, load test results, canary run report, and a Console integration ticket to surface per-tenant metrics.

Links: Confluence RFC: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/OPS/Signal+Fencing+RFC, GH PoC: https://github.com/redwood-inference/obs-delegated-proxy/pulls/12
comments: 2025-02-07: Initial RFC drafted by Ava. Focused on OTLP-first compatibility. Asked Security to review KMS design.
2025-02-10: Security review (Dana Patel): require mTLS by default for VPC connectors, JWT fallback allowed for legacy agents. Added security_review_required flag.
2025-02-12: Design sync with Console (Liam O'Neill) — agreed to surface per-tenant ingress metrics and an audit search view in Console later in Q2.
2025-02-15: PoC PR opened: GH #12. Early benchmark: 30k RPS on a c5.9xlarge equivalent with p50 latency +6ms vs baseline. Need to optimize batching.
2025-02-20: Diego: Implemented reject-vs-log-only feature flag. Planning canary for week of 2025-03-03 with EngOps.
2025-02-22: PM review (Sofia Liu): add customer-facing docs and example collector config; added to acceptance criteria.

source.title: Route Sentry: route-level observability & alerting for embedding+ranker endpoints
source.creator: Aisha Patel
source.description:
Goal: Provide route-scoped dashboards, tracing enrichment, token-level cost breakdowns, and automated alerts for embedding + reranking endpoints so product owners can detect quality regressions, latency spikes, and cost anomalies tied to specific routes and model versions.

Scope: instrument runtime and scorer layers to emit route_id, request_tags (caller_id, route_name), embedding_model_version, rerank_model_version, candidate_count, kv_cache_hit, batch_size, vector_index_latency_ms, scorer_latency_ms, tokens_generated, token_cost_usd, compute_gpu_ms. Build dashboards per-route (p50/p95 latency, SLI: successful responses, rerank_precision@10, embedding_distance_drift, cost_per_request, cost_per_1k_tokens) and a cross-route heatmap for sudden delta detection. Implement alert rules for: 1) p95 latency increase > 30% for 15m; 2) cost per request increase > 40% over baseline for 1h; 3) rerank_precision@10 drop > 10% on evaluated prompt set; 4) sudden rise in kv_cache_miss rate > 20%.

Acceptance criteria:
- Traces include route_id and model hashes in 100% of scorer spans.
- Per-route dashboard template available in Console and linked to trace explorer.
- Token-level cost decomposition stored as timeseries and exposed in per-route cost tab.
- Three alert policies wired to PagerDuty and Slack channel #sentry-emb-rank.
- Runbook with diagnostic steps and rollbacks checked into Confluence.

Trade-offs & decisions:
- Sampling: keep 1% full traces for 90 days and store aggregated statistics for all requests. Decision balances disk/TCO vs post-facto debugging needs.
- Granularity: we will not store per-request embeddings for long-term; instead persist summary metrics (mean distance, variance) per route to avoid storage blowup.
- Cost attribution: attribute request cost as embedding_cost + rerank_cost + index_lookup_cost; initial token cost uses dk-token-rate with per-model coefficients until runtime metering is accurate.

Risk & mitigations:
- Increased telemetry volume => throttle spans at client library and rely on aggregated metrics pipeline.
- Alert noise => use warm-up windows, require 3 consecutive evaluation windows before PD.

Open questions:
- Should drift alerts be tied to canary vs production model versions? (Proposed: yes — tag by rollout and include canary flag in spans.)
- Long tail routes with low traffic: what baseline should we use? (Proposed: cohort baseline grouping by request size and candidate_count.)
comments: 2025-02-12 - Aisha Patel: Created ticket. Initial spike showed insufficient route tags in scorer spans; mapped changes across runtime and SDK.
2025-02-13 - Mateo Alvarez: Added schema for new trace tags and example span payload; created draft PR with runtime changes: https://github.com/redwood-inference/platform/pull/7234

2025-02-14 - Priya Singh (Product): Reviewed acceptance criteria; asked for explicit precision@k metric and a monthly cost budget alert per customer. Action: add precision@10 and budget alert as optional labels.
2025-02-16 - Engineering review: Decided to sample 1% of full traces and keep aggregated metrics for all requests. Tracing team requested a backfill plan for routes created in last 30 days.

2025-02-18 - Design feedback (Evan Liu): Dashboard mockups look good; suggested adding a time-series of model rollout ratio so we can correlate baseline changes with model percentage during canary. Design will hand off assets in Figma: https://www.figma.com/file/xyz/route-sentry

2025-02-19 - Mateo Alvarez: Implemented metric emitters for candidate_count and kv_cache_hit in scorer; unit tests passing. Next: wire metrics to Console dashboard and create alert policies in Grafana/Notify.
2025-02-20 - SRE (Olivia Chen): Requested runbook steps for escalations and a synthetic traffic job to validate alerts. I added a TODO to schedule synthetic job in Cycle 2025-W08.

TODO: create Grafana dashboard template (dash-id TBD), add PD escalation policy, schedule synthetic validation job, and finalize Confluence runbook: https://redwood.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/ENG/pages/987654/Route+Sentry

source.title: SLO cohort preemptor with GPU fragmentation reduction
source.creator: Aisha Patel
source.description:
Problem statement: Continuous batching currently favors throughput but can cause unfair latency regressions for bursty tenants and creates GPU memory fragmentation from repeated small KV cache evictions. We need an SLO-aware preemption policy that groups tenants into latency cohorts and performs preemptive slot retirement + opportunistic compaction to reduce fragmentation while preserving throughput and overall GPU utilization.

Goals:
- Keep tenant P95/P99 latency within configured SLO bands during normal and bursty loads.
- Improve memory fragmentation (free slab count and largest contiguous free region) to reduce out-of-memory spikes for large requests.
- Maintain end-to-end throughput within 5% of baseline for steady-state workloads.
- Provide observability and safe rollout via feature flags and canaries.

Non-goals: full KV GC redesign, global tenant rebalancer, or changing model selection routing decisions in this ticket.
acceptance_criteria: Cohorting: tenants are grouped by recent P95/P99 into at least three cohorts (gold/silver/bronze) and cohort assignment updates are computed every 1 minute
Preemption: when a GPU slot is needed, the preemptor chooses victims preferring bronze then silver within the same fairness window and enforces a grace retirement window before eviction
Latency SLOs: under a mixed synthetic workload the platform maintains configured P95/P99 for >95% of tenants in gold cohort and >90% in silver cohort (measured over 30-minute windows)
Fragmentation: average number of free slabs > baseline by at least 25% (i.e. fewer small free fragments) and largest contiguous free region increased by >=20% in synthetic fragmentation tests
No throughput regression: overall tokens/sec does not drop more than 5% compared to baseline under steady traffic
Safety: feature gated and canaryed; automatic rollback trigger if P99 tail permanently increases by >30% for >10 minutes
design_notes: Cohort preemptor: compute tenant latency score using exponential moving percentiles (EM-P95/EM-P99) and bucket into cohorts; make preempt decisions at slot request time using cohort quotas and recent eviction impact
Grace retirement: victims receive a 100-300ms grace window to flush in-flight KV refs before hard eviction; this reduces unnecessary cold-KV churn
Slot retirement + coalescing: when a slot is vacated, attempt to merge contiguous KV slabs and mark for opportunistic compaction during idle epochs
Fragmentation reduction heuristic: prefer evicting small, low-reuse KV items and consolidate freed slabs; run cheap compaction on idle GPUs (<30% util) to avoid impacting throughput
Soft quotas: maintain per-cohort soft-memory budgets expressed as fraction of GPU memory to bound worst-case fragmentation and eviction rate

Victim selection fairness: within a cohort use a round-robin window with weighted fairness based on recent served tokens and missed SLOs (Jain-style fairness factor) to avoid starving low-traffic tenants
Backpressure integration: when preemptor evicts, emit backpressure hints to the router to prefer alternative regions or fallback model variants for affected tenants
Telemetry: emit cohort_assignment, preempt_reason, victim_slot_id, grace_duration_ms, post_eviction_kv_moved_count, fragmentation_delta
Config knobs: cohort_update_interval_ms, grace_window_ms, compaction_idle_threshold_pct, eviction_size_threshold_bytes, cohort_memory_fraction
rollout_plan: Implement behind feature flag: slo_cohort_preemptor_v1
Unit tests + simulator run to validate fairness vs known adversarial patterns
Canary to 5% of tenants in us-west-2 for 48 hours with continuous monitoring
Expand to 25% if no rollback triggers; hold for one full week of traffic patterns
Full release toggled on after two weeks and verified SLAs for 3 days
test_plan: Unit tests: cohort bucketing, grace window timers, victim chooser determinism
Simulator: synthetic mix of bursty and steady tenants to validate fairness metrics and fragmentation improvements
Integration: run on staging fleet with real trace replay for 72 hours, compare P95/P99 and fragmentation metrics to baseline
Chaos: introduce random GPU slot pressure and ensure rollbacks fire and system recovers
Performance: measure tokens/sec and end-to-end latency under fixed throughput load
progress_updates: 2025-01-08: Ticket created; initial problem analysis and goals documented
2025-01-15: Prototyped cohorting library and EM percentile estimator (PR https://github.com/redwood-inference/serving/pull/4897)
2025-01-22: Internal design review with runtime and product; agreed on grace retirement semantics and compaction budget
2025-01-30: Simulator tests show 18% improvement in largest contiguous free region for synthetic fragmentation traces; minor P95 regression for low-priority tenants fixed by adjusting soft quotas
2025-02-05: Implemented initial compaction task and telemetry (PR https://github.com/redwood-inference/serving/pull/4992) - awaiting perf bench
2025-02-12: Integration tests running on staging; canary plan drafted
review_feedback: PM: prefer explicit SLA mapping for cohort labels (gold->p95<=80ms etc.) before canary
Reliability: ensure preempt decisions are idempotent and safe across control-plane retries
Security: no user data is moved between tenants, compaction operates only on per-tenant KV slab metadata
OBS: add dashboards for victim churn and compaction CPU usage before canary
source.notes: Trade-offs considered: aggressive eviction reduces tail but increases cold-KV misses; we bias toward cohort-level fairness with a configurable knob to favor SLOs for higher tiers. We chose local compaction and slot retirement to avoid global rebalancing complexity. Future: extend to cross-GPU compaction and tenant draining APIs.

title: SLO cohort preemptor with GPU fragmentation reduction
preview:
Problem statement: Continuous batching currently favors throughput but can cause unfair latency regressions for bursty tenants and creates GPU memory fragmentation from repeated small KV cache evictions. We need an SLO-aware preemption policy that groups tenants into latency cohorts and performs preemptive slot retirement + opportunistic compaction to reduce fragmentation while preserving throughput and overall GPU utilization.

Goals:
- Keep tenant P95/P99 latency within configured SLO bands during normal and bursty loads.
- Improve memory fragmentation (free slab count and largest contiguous free region) to reduce out-of-memory spikes for large requests.
- Maintain end-to-end throughput within 5% of baseline for steady-state workloads.
- Provide observability and safe rollout via feature flags and canaries.

Non-goals: full KV GC redesign, global tenant rebalancer, or changing model selection routing decisions in this ticket.

source.title: Deprecation sweep: compat helpers, codemods, and automated changelog for SDK v1->v2
source.creator: Aisha Kapoor
source.description: Goal: execute a coordinated deprecation sweep across Python/TypeScript/Go SDKs to make v2 the default, provide engineering-safe compatibility helpers and codemods, and ship an automated changelog pipeline so customers can find breaking changes and migration examples. Scope: (1) identify all v1 surface area to be removed/alias'd; (2) implement light-weight runtime shims and opt-in compatibility packages where removal would cause large customer impact; (3) author codemods for common patterns (streaming, retries, structured output -> function-calling); (4) update docs and sample apps with side-by-side v1->v2 examples; (5) add CI migration tests and a changelog automation job that extracts API-level deprecations from source and PR titles. Deliverables: - compat-helpers packages (python redwood_compat, ts @redwood/compat, go compat module) - codemod scripts + examples (jscodeshift for TS, rope/codemod patterns for Python, go/repair tools) - migration page with runnable snippets and diffable examples covering streaming ergonomics, retry semantics, and new structured output patterns - changelog automation that parses PR labels and deprecation annotations and publishes a machine and human-readable changelog artifact (CHANGELOG_API.json + CHANGELOG.md) - rollout plan for toggling v2-as-default in hosted API and deprecation notices for Dedicated/Private nodes Risks & trade-offs: - Heavy-handed shims increase maintenance burden; we prefer opt-in compat packages rather than leaving ambiguous runtime behavior. - Codemods cannot guarantee semantics with custom user wrappers around SDKs; emphasize CLA/backup in migration docs. - Streaming retry semantics: v2's streaming backpressure model is stricter; add an examples section showing how to migrate streaming consumers to the new async iterator + reconnect pattern. Out-of-scope for this ticket: large refactors to internals (kernel selection), language-specific re-architecting of SDKs beyond surface shims. Links and artifacts: - Migration notebook (Confluence): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/SDK/SDK+v1->v2+Deprecation+Sweep - Draft migration page (Drive): https://drive.redwood.ai/drive/folders/SDK-migration-drafts - Draft PRs: https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk-python/pull/834, https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk-js/pull/1201, https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk-go/pull/279 - Codemod repo: https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk-codemods - Changelog automation PoC: https://github.com/redwood-inference/changelog-bot (contains rule set for deprecation parsing and sample webhook) Acceptance criteria: 1) Compat packages published to PyPI/NPM/go proxy with basic test coverage and a README migration example for streaming and retry cases. 2) Codemods able to transform 80% of internal migration patterns used in our sample apps. 3) Changelog automation added to CI that emits CHANGELOG_API.json and a human CHANGELOG.md on release branches. 4) Docs updated with step-by-step migration recipes and a rollback section. 5) Runbook for customer support and SEs describing common migration gotchas and recommended fixes.
comments: 2025-01-10 - Ethan Park: Kickoff meeting notes uploaded to Confluence. Decided to prefer separate compat packages (opt-in) over runtime toggles to reduce risk. Action: Aisha to draft compat API surface by 2025-01-15.

2025-01-15 - Aisha Kapoor: Initial compat API draft uploaded. Key patterns: rename client.create -> client.generate (alias with DeprecationWarning in v2 compat package). Attached sample diff for Python streaming consumer migration.

2025-01-20 - Marisol Vega (Docs): Reviewed migration examples. Request: add a 'large-output' streaming example showing reconnect + resume with KV cache hints. Marisol will author doc snippets by 2025-01-27.
2025-01-23 - PR sdk-python#834 opened by @ethan-park: Adds redwood_compat package, pytest matrix and simple alias implementations. CI failing on Windows-specific path handling (investigating).

2025-01-28 - Liam O'Connor (QA): Created E2E migration test harness that runs sample apps against both v1 and compat package. Found 2 failure modes: (1) streaming chunk-order assumption, (2) retry-on-idempotent vs non-idempotent calls. Test cases added to CI job 'migration-e2e'.

2025-02-03 - Security review request added: compat packages include a small shim that touches token handling; security_review_required=true. Security: @Yuri Petrov assigned review and recommended audit scope restricted to auth flows only.

2025-02-06 - Aisha Kapoor: Codemod PoC (TS) converts event-based streaming to async-iterator usage. Example migration output included in Drive. Need Python codemod for generator->async iterator pattern.

2025-02-09 - Ethan Park: Changelog automation PoC merged into changelog-bot repo. Needs integration in release pipeline to publish CHANGELOG_API.json. Next: wire webhook on PR merge that tags deprecation annotations.

2025-02-12 - Product review: PM checklist completed. PM notes: prioritize migration examples for customers on Dedicated instances and update Support runbook. Requesting one-week alpha internal rollout after publishing compat packages.
source.release_notes_summary: Introduces opt-in compatibility helpers for SDK v1 users, codemods for common migration patterns (streaming, retries, structured output), and an automated changelog pipeline to surface deprecations and breaking changes.
source.qa_notes: Run 'migration-e2e' CI job: - sample_apps/python/* against sdk-python@compat - sample_apps/js/* against sdk-js@compat - confirm streaming resume cases and retry idempotency flags. Document observed failures with reproduction steps and attach logs.

source.title: LLM handoff curation and profile publish orchestrator
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Goal: Implement an automated, auditable pipeline that takes community weights -> deterministic conversion -> multi-variant runtime artifacts -> smoke/quality/latency validation -> generates a canonical pinned version and publishes a standardized performance profile. This ticket covers orchestration and policy decisions for curation (safety heuristics, trusted manifests), deterministic conversion reproducibility, multi-quantization candidate generation, and the profile publish flow that feeds Console and the model catalog.

Scope: 1) Intake manifest validation (signed/unsigned handling, provenance metadata extraction), 2) deterministic conversion engine invocation and artifact hashing, 3) compatibility matrix enforcement (opset/kernel constraints), 4) candidate quantization passes (FP16, q4_0, q4_k_maybe) with smoke latency/accuracy checks, 5) end-to-end KV/cache warmup smoke harness, 6) profile aggregation and publish to model-catalog, 7) automatic pin creation and rollout gating into staging.

Design constraints / decisions: - Use convex-convert v0.9+ as conversion baseline for reproducibility (deterministic build flags enabled). - Keep original weight blobs in immutable storage with signed manifest; converted artifacts live in artifact-store under canonical pin. - For quantization we run three deterministic passes and pick the best candidate that meets quality SLOs (top-1/EM on small eval shards + no more than 10% latency regression vs FP16 baseline). - Security gate: models flagged by heuristic safety scanner (safety-scan-v2) land in a quarantine bucket and require manual review. - Publish profile fields: model_size, peak_memory_32seq, median_tok_latency_8seq, throughput_128batch, quality_shard_1, quantization_candidates, conversion_artifact_hash, provenance_link.

Acceptance criteria: - Pipeline processes a sample OSS model (weights + repo) end-to-end and produces a canonical pin with published profile JSON that Console can ingest. - Conversion must be reproducible across two runs (artifact hashes match). - Telemetry emitted for every step with success/failure codes and per-step latency. - Security gating and quarantine flow exercised in integration tests.

Out of scope: manual model governance UI, advanced eval suites beyond the smoke shard, long-term retention policy changes.
comments: 2025-01-15 - Ava Chen: Initial ticket created after spike with intake proof-of-concept. Spike notes: deterministic conversion reproducible at small scale but fails for sharded LoRA checkpoints; recommended adding shard-normalization pre-step.

2025-01-22 - Miguel Rosario: Implemented manifest schema v2 and extractor. Pushed parser to gh:rw/convex-intake PR#482. Parser extracts commit, author, huggingface id (if present), and generates canonical provenance_url. Leaving unsigned manifests in /quarantine until signature workflow exists.

2025-01-29 - QA: Ran conversion matrix against 5 community models. Results: convex-convert v0.9.2 produced matching artifact hashes for 4/5; the failing model had non-deterministic layer ordering — added deterministic-layer-sort to preprocessor. Documented workaround in Confluence: /wiki/DeterministicConversion.

2025-02-03 - Perf lead (Ravi): Smoke latency harness results for candidate quantizations: FP16 median_tok_latency_8seq = 18.2ms, q4_0 = 21.9ms (20% regression), q4_k_maybe = 19.6ms (8% regression). Decided to accept q4_k_maybe when quality delta < 2% on smoke shard. Added that rule to policy engine.

2025-02-07 - Security (Jamal): safety-scan-v2 flagged a model for possible prompt-injection vectors based on training corpus heuristics. Model moved to quarantine; manual review required. Recommend adding additional training-corpus provenance checks later.

2025-02-10 - Miguel Rosario: Orchestrator prototype (k8s Job + Argo workflow) merged to gh:rw/artifact-orch PR#503. It emits step telemetry to metrics/ingest and writes profile JSON to catalog-api staging. Next: wire canonical pin creation and add canary rollout plan to runtime staging.

2025-02-10 - Ava Chen: Notes on rollout policy: initial rollout should be: staging-only pin -> smoke-run for 24h -> canary traffic 5% for 6h -> progressive 25%/50%/100% with automatic rollback on latency/quality regressions. Documented in rollout playbook draft.

2025-02-10 - TODOs: (1) Add artifact-signing step and HSM/KMS integration (security). (2) Add tolerance threshold config per model-family. (3) Create e2e integration test that runs every nightly pipeline run and fails build on non-reproducible conversion.

source.title: Incident slate: lightweight incident artifact and escalation attachments
source.creator: Priya Raman
source.description:
Background:
Redwood's escalation channels (PagerDuty + Slack) currently post full evidence bundles which are heavyweight and often duplicate information across attachments. This work introduces an "incident slate": a compact, structured artifact (JSON blob + small rich attachment) generated at signal ingestion and included in PD and Slack notifications. The slate is designed to: surface the minimal set of decision-making facts, link to the detailed evidence bundle, and seed runbook templates and postmortem drafts automatically.

Goals:
- Reduce cognitive load for oncall by surfacing the top 3 signals (latency spike, error class, top affected customers) and immediate remediation hints.
- Ensure the slate can be consumed programmatically by runbook automation and postmortem seeding systems.
- Preserve privacy: redact PII and adhere to retention policies before any external notification.
- Make the slate resilient to rate-limited backends and graceful in cases of missing telemetry.

Success metrics (first 12 weeks):
- Median time-to-triage reduced by 25% for P1 latency incidents across runtime services.
- Oncall positive feedback score >= 4.0 in internal survey for new PD/Slack attachments.
- Automatic postmortem seed acceptance rate >= 70% (seeded drafts require < 2 edits on average).

Design decisions & trade-offs:
1) Compact vs verbose: We selected a 10-field maximum slate instead of full evidence to keep PD cards scannable. Trade-off: may omit low-signal spans that are useful in rare cases; detailed evidence bundle remains linked.
2) Data retention & redaction: Slate will include hashed identifiers (sha256) for customer IDs and redact raw request payloads. This reduces debugging fidelity but is required for privacy and regulator alignment.
3) Generation point: create slate at the tracing ingestion worker (closest to the signal) to minimize telemetry enrichment latency. This increases worker CPU but reduces notification latency.
4) Fallback behavior: If span extraction fails, attach a short summary: "Trace metadata unavailable — evidence bundle queued". Prevents noisy PD alerts caused by incomplete payloads.

Slate schema (summary):
- incident_id, short_summary, severity, top_error_class, top_latency_p95_ms, top_3_spans (span_name + relative_time_ms), affected_customers_hashed, suggested_runbook_id, links {evidence_bundle, grafana_slice, trace_link}, generated_at_iso

Acceptance criteria:
- PD integration: PD webhook receives slate JSON in the "details" payload and renders a compact card matching the design spec.
- Slack integration: Slack attachment uses Block Kit to show the short_summary + top_error_class and a button linking to the full evidence bundle.
- Runbook seeding: When suggested_runbook_id is present, runbook seeder creates a draft with the slate fields filled and assigns the draft to the incident owner.
- Telemetry: Emit metrics: slate.generated.count, slate.redactions.count, slate.fallback.count, slate.processing.latency_ms (p95/p99).
- Tests: Unit tests for schema validation, e2e test that simulates a latency spike and verifies slate in PD and Slack.

Implementation plan (high level):
1) Slate generator service (4 pts): implement extractor that composes slate from trace + metrics + alerts. Add config to cap included spans to 3 and perform PII redaction.
2) PD adapter (1 pt): extend PD webhook forwarder to embed slate in notification payload and add JSON schema validation.
3) Slack adapter (1 pt): Block Kit template and link handling + localized fallback for mobile clients.
4) Runbook seeder hook (1 pt): small service to accept slate and instantiate runbook draft.
5) Metrics & dashboards (1 pt): new Grafana panels and Alert rules for slate errors/latency.

Rollout plan:
- Week 1 (canary): Internal infra teams only; PD group alerts routed to a dev PD escalation policy; collect feedback.
- Week 2 (expanded): Add a subset of customer-facing services (search, embeddings) behind feature flag.
- Week 3 (full): Default for all new P1 incidents; old attachments deprecated but available via link.
- Post rollout: Measure triage time and iterate on slate fields based on oncall feedback.

QA & validation:
- e2e harness (github action): synthetic load that triggers a latency SLO breach and asserts slate appears in PD and Slack with correct fields.
- Manual walkthrough checklist for SRE oncall: verify redaction, verify runbook seed creation, sanity-check links.

Risks & mitigations:
- Over-redaction reduces debuggability: keep a privacy-exempt audit mode only accessible to SREs for 7 days.
- Increased generation latency: move heavy enrichment to async background job if 95th percentile exceeds 150ms.
- PD rate limits: throttle notifications and collapse repeated slates into a single update with incrementing summary.

Longer-term extensions:
- Integrate with postmortem auto-population: map slate fields to postmortem sections (impact, timeline seeds).
- Enrich slate with model inference traces for Redwood Dedicated/private customers when available.
- Allow customer opt-in for non-redacted slates when contractually permitted.
acceptance_criteria: PD webhook embeds slate JSON and PD card rendering matches spec
Slack Block Kit shows short_summary and top_error_class with evidence link
Runbook seeder creates draft when suggested_runbook_id present
Telemetry metrics (slate.generated.count, slate.redactions.count) exist and are wired to Grafana
Unit + e2e tests passing in CI
tasks: Implement slate extractor in tracing ingestion (ENG-87531-a)
Add PII redaction module and unit tests (ENG-87531-b)
PD adapter: embed slate and add schema validation (ENG-87531-c)
Slack adapter: Block Kit template + mobile fallback (ENG-87531-d)
Runbook seeder hook and permission checks (ENG-87531-e)
Add slate metrics and Grafana panels (ENG-87531-f)
Write e2e harness and CI job (ENG-87531-g)
Documentation: Confluence spec + runbook template (ENG-87531-h)
comments: 2025-01-16 - Priya: Initial design draft posted to Confluence (link in attachments). Asking infra SIG for feedback on generator location.
2025-01-22 - Mateo: Prototype extractor implemented; pulled in basic span selector that chooses top 3 by cumulative duration. PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/observability/pull/4123
2025-01-28 - Design review (Nina K.): Block Kit looks good, requested an extra line for \"suggested action\" so oncall sees immediate next step.
2025-02-03 - Security (Aisha S.): Confirmed schema values are safe; insisting on hashing customer IDs and adding retention config. Added security_review_required flag.
2025-02-07 - Mateo: Added runbook seeder prototype; it creates drafts under /Runbooks/AutoSeeds/<incident_id>. PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runbooks/pull/221
2025-02-10 - QA: e2e harness failing intermittently due to flaky trace-link generation; creating ticket ENG-87620 to stabilize test trace replay.
source.notes_for_reviewers: Key review areas: 1) Privacy owners — confirm redaction strategy is sufficient. 2) SREs — validate suggested_runbook_id mapping to canonical runbooks. 3) Console UX — verify PD card and Slack block render on mobile. Please leave comments on the Confluence spec or directly on PR #4123.
qa_checks: Synthetic latency incident triggers slate and PD card (CI job)
Runbook draft created and fields populated
Metrics emitted and Grafana panels display p95 and counts

Design decisions & trade-offs:
1) Compact vs verbose: We selected a 10-field maximum slate instead of full evidence to keep PD cards scannable. Trade-off: may omit low-signal spans that are useful in rare cases; detailed evidence bundle remains linked.
2) Data retention & redaction: Slate will include hashed identifiers (sha256) for customer IDs and redact raw request payloads. This reduces debugging fidelity but is required for privacy and regulator alignment.
3) Generation point: create slate at the tracing ingestion worker (closest to the signal) to minimize telemetry enrichment latency. This increases worker CPU but reduces notification latency.
4) Fallback behavior: If span extraction fails, attach a short summary: \"Trace metadata unavailable — evidence bundle queued\". Prevents noisy PD alerts caused by incomplete payloads.

Slate schema (summary):
- incident_id, short_summary, severity, top_error_class, top_latency_p95_ms, top_3_spans (span_name + relative_time_ms), affected_customers_hashed, suggested_runbook_id, links {evidence_bundle, grafana_slice, trace_link}, generated_at_iso

Acceptance criteria:
- PD integration: PD webhook receives slate JSON in the \"details\" payload and renders a compact card matching the design spec.
- Slack integration: Slack attachment uses Block Kit to show the short_summary + top_error_class and a button linking to the full evidence bundle.
- Runbook seeding: When suggested_runbook_id is present, runbook seeder creates a draft with the slate fields filled and assigns the draft to the incident owner.
- Telemetry: Emit metrics: slate.generated.count, slate.redactions.count, slate.fallback.count, slate.processing.latency_ms (p95/p99).
- Tests: Unit tests for schema validation, e2e test that simulates a latency spike and verifies slate in PD and Slack.

Implementation plan (high level):
1) Slate generator service (4 pts): implement extractor that composes slate from trace + metrics + alerts. Add config to cap included spans to 3 and perform PII redaction.
2) PD adapter (1 pt): extend PD webhook forwarder to embed slate in notification payload and add JSON schema validation.
3) Slack adapter (1 pt): Block Kit template and link handling + localized fallback for mobile clients.
4) Runbook seeder hook (1 pt): small service to accept slate and instantiate runbook draft.
5) Metrics & dashboards (1 pt): new Grafana panels and Alert rules for slate errors/latency.

Rollout plan:
- Week 1 (canary): Internal infra teams only; PD group alerts routed to a dev PD escalation policy; collect feedback.
- Week 2 (expanded): Add a subset of customer-facing services (search, embeddings) behind feature flag.
- Week 3 (full): Default for all new P1 incidents; old attachments deprecated but available via link.
- Post rollout: Measure triage time and iterate on slate fields based on oncall feedback.

QA & validation:
- e2e harness (github action): synthetic load that triggers a latency SLO breach and asserts slate appears in PD and Slack with correct fields.
- Manual walkthrough checklist for SRE oncall: verify redaction, verify runbook seed creation, sanity-check links.

Risks & mitigations:
- Over-redaction reduces debuggability: keep a privacy-exempt audit mode only accessible to SREs for 7 days.
- Increased generation latency: move heavy enrichment to async background job if 95th percentile exceeds 150ms.
- PD rate limits: throttle notifications and collapse repeated slates into a single update with incrementing summary.

Longer-term extensions:
- Integrate with postmortem auto-population: map slate fields to postmortem sections (impact, timeline seeds).
- Enrich slate with model inference traces for Redwood Dedicated/private customers when available.
- Allow customer opt-in for non-redacted slates when contractually permitted.
text:
Background:
Redwood's escalation channels (PagerDuty + Slack) currently post full evidence bundles which are heavyweight and often duplicate information across attachments. This work introduces an \"incident slate\": a compact, structured artifact (JSON blob + small rich attachment) generated at signal ingestion and included in PD and Slack notifications. The slate is designed to: surface the minimal set of decision-making facts, link to the detailed evidence bundle, and seed runbook templates and postmortem drafts automatically.

Goals:
- Reduce cognitive load for oncall by surfacing the top 3 signals (latency spike, error class, top affected customers) and immediate remediation hints.
- Ensure the slate can be consumed programmatically by runbook automation and postmortem seeding systems.
- Preserve privacy: redact PII and adhere to retention policies before any external notification.
- Make the slate resilient to rate-limited backends and graceful in cases of missing telemetry.

Success metrics (first 12 weeks):
- Median time-to-triage reduced by 25% for P1 latency incidents across runtime services.
- Oncall positive feedback score >= 4.0 in internal survey for new PD/Slack attachments.
- Automatic postmortem seed acceptance rate >= 70% (seeded drafts require < 2 edits on average).

source.title: Reduce workspace churn and improve tiling heuristics for FA3 integration
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Integrate FlashAttention v3 (FA3) into the serving runtime while eliminating excessive workspace allocation churn and improving tiling decisions for common sequence-length distributions. Current FA3 integration yields higher end-to-end p99 latency on some models due to repeated cudaMalloc/cudaFree cycles and suboptimal tile selection for mixed-length traffic. This ticket scopes an audit, targeted engine changes (workspace reuse, allocator pooling), and new tiling heuristics driven by model profiles.
acceptance_criteria: Reduce number of device allocation calls during typical prefill+decode workload by >= 70% compared to current FA3 baseline.
P50/P95/P99 token latency for resnet-llm-13b and conv-llm-7b workloads should not regress more than 3% relative to runtime-1.20 baseline with FA2 kernels.
End-to-end memory fragmentation metric (tracked by per-GPU free-chunks count) improves by at least 40% for mixed-length workloads.
No increase in served request error rate attributable to workspace reuse or tiling changes during a 48h canary in staging cluster.
Documentation updated: allocator behavior and new runtime flags documented in runtime/ops/engine/README.md and console rollout notes.
steps_to_reproduce: Deploy to staging dedicated pool (staging-fa3-alloc) with runtime-1.21 candidate image
Collect metrics: prometheus query 'runtime_gpu_alloc_calls_total', 'runtime_alloc_free_latency_seconds', and p99_token_latency_tagged
Compare against baseline runtime-1.20 FA2 run stored in dashboards: https://grafana.redwood/internal/d/allocs/fa2-baseline
source.proposed_changes: 1) Implement a per-GPU slab pool allocator for FA3 scratch memory with LRU-based reuse across requests. 2) Add adaptive tiling that uses a short-window histogram of sequence lengths per model route to pick tile sizes that minimize extra KV-cache padding. 3) Expose two runtime flags: --fa3-slab-size and --tiling-hist-window to tune in canary. 4) Safety: fall back to per-request fresh allocation on OOM or allocator corruption.
decisions: Choose slab pool allocator over CUDA caching allocator because it provides deterministic reuse and easier accounting for our SLOs.
Tiling will be model-route scoped (not global) to allow fine-grained tuning for heavy customers without global regressions.
Keep default behavior conservative for lowest-tier customers; enable aggressive pooling behind a runtime config toggle for Dedicated/Private customers first.
risks_and_mitigation: Memory corruption risk from incorrect reuse: add allocator checksum and UAF detectors; plan to disable pooling on first sign of corruption.
Edge-case latency spikes for long-tail sequence lengths: implement circuit-breaker that disables adaptive tiling when tail variability exceeds threshold.
Compatibility with FA2 paths: maintain parallel codepaths and a feature flag to revert to FA2 tiling decisions if metrics deteriorate.
test_plan: Unit tests: allocator invariants, reuse accounting, and simulated OOM paths (CI runtime-tests alloc-suite).
Integration: end-to-end prefill+decode tests in CI with deterministic seeds for KV-cache sizes.
Staging canary: 2-node dedicated pool, 48h running mixed-length production-like traffic with real routing rules.
Chaos injection: forced cudaMalloc failures to verify fallback safety path and alerting.
experiment_results: 2025-01-20: prototype slab allocator reduced cudaMalloc calls by ~76% on microbenchmark (single-GPU, 16 concurrent actors).
2025-01-25: mixed-length staging run showed P99 token latency -1.8% on conv-llm-7b, +2.1% on resnet-llm-13b vs FA2 baseline; further tuning of tiling hist window improved resnet P99 to -0.6%.
2025-01-30: observed rare OOM during long rolling windows when slab size was undersized; mitigation: dynamic slab grow with cap and instrumentation alert on growth events.
source.implementation_notes: Patch lands under runtime/engine/fa3_allocator.{cc,h} and runtime/engine/tiling_profile.cc. Add metrics: runtime_fa3_slab_reuse_ratio and runtime_tiling_choice_histogram. New config keys in runtime-config: fa3.slab_size_mb, fa3.max_slabs_per_gpu, tiling.hist_window_seconds.
monitoring_and_rollout: Prometheus alerts: increase in runtime_fa3_slab_growth_events OR p99_token_latency > baseline * 1.1 => Pager for Oncall-Infra.
Dashboards: add 'FA3 allocator' panel to runtime/allocations dashboard with reuse ratio and growth events.
Canary rollout: 5% of traffic in staging -> 25% -> 60% -> 100% across 4 steps with 1h observation windows and automatic rollback on defined SLO breach.
Post-rollout: 72h burn-in with continuous synthetic probes for long-tail sequences.
notes: Design review 2025-01-15: attendees Miguel Torres, Priya Nair, Liam O'Connor. Agreed on slab sizes initially set to 256MB per GPU with dynamic growth capped at 1GB.
Follow-up: create a lightweight runtime CLI 'runtimectl fa3-dump' to snapshot allocator state for oncall investigations.
PM feedback (Sofia Martinez): prefer conservative default + opt-in aggressive pooling for Dedicated customers during Q1 to minimize support churn.
